topics.title: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) in Supply Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET502 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) in Supply Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Supply Chains?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> **Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Supply Chains** refers to the deployment of computational systems capable of performing tasks that traditionally require human cognitive functions — such as reasoning, learning, perception, and decision-making — to automate, augment, and optimize the end-to-end flow of goods, information, and finances from raw material suppliers to the end consumer.

In the context of **PCIET502**, AI is not viewed as a single technology but as an **umbrella discipline** encompassing Machine Learning (ML), Natural Language Processing (NLP), Computer Vision, and Robotics, all integrated with **Supply Chain Management (SCM)** processes.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Smart Warehouse Manager"**
> Imagine a warehouse manager named *Ravi* who has worked for 30 years. He knows which products sell more on festival days, which supplier always delivers late, and which delivery route has a hidden pothole. Now imagine cloning Ravi's brain into a computer that can process **1 million SKUs** in **milliseconds**, never sleeps, never takes a holiday, and learns from every single transaction.
> That computational clone is **AI in the Supply Chain**. It is essentially the digitization and scaling of expert human intuition across the entire value network.

## 1.2 What is Machine Learning (ML)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition**
> **Machine Learning (ML)** is a subset of AI that enables systems to **learn from data**, identify hidden patterns, and improve their performance over time **without being explicitly programmed** for every scenario. In supply chains, ML algorithms consume historical transactional data (sales, shipments, weather, demand) to produce predictive and prescriptive insights.

### The Three Paradigms of ML (Critical for KTU Exams)

| ML Type | Learning Style | Supply Chain Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Supervised Learning** | Learns from **labeled** input-output pairs | Demand forecasting (sales history → future sales) |
| **Unsupervised Learning** | Finds hidden structure in **unlabeled** data | Customer segmentation, anomaly detection |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | Learns by **trial-and-error** with rewards/penalties | Dynamic routing, warehouse robot navigation |

## 1.3 Key Terminology Students MUST Memorize

> [!NOTE]
> **Glossary of High-Frequency Exam Terms**
> - **Algorithm:** A set of mathematical instructions (e.g., decision tree, neural network).
> - **Training Data:** Historical dataset used to "teach" the model.
> - **Features (X):** Input variables (e.g., price, weather, day-of-week).
> - **Target (Y):** Output variable to predict (e.g., demand quantity).
> - **Model:** The mathematical function learned from data.
> - **Inference:** Using a trained model to make predictions on new data.
> - **SKU:** Stock Keeping Unit (a unique product identifier).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AI/ML Decision Pipeline in SCM

The application of AI/ML in supply chains follows a structured **6-step operational pipeline**:

1. **Problem Definition:** Identify a specific SCM problem (e.g., "Reduce stockouts in Region X").
2. **Data Collection:** Aggregate historical data from ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning), WMS (Warehouse Management System), TMS (Transportation Management System), IoT sensors, and external sources.
3. **Data Preprocessing:** Clean missing values, normalize scales, encode categorical variables.
4. **Model Selection & Training:** Choose an algorithm (e.g., ARIMA, Random Forest, LSTM) and train it on the data.
5. **Validation & Testing:** Evaluate using metrics like **MAPE** (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) or **RMSE** (Root Mean Square Error).
6. **Deployment & Monitoring:** Integrate the model into live SCM operations and continuously retrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step**
> Supply chain data is inherently **noisy, non-stationary, and seasonal**. A model trained on last year's data may fail during a pandemic or a geopolitical shock. This is why steps 5 and 6 involve continuous retraining — the model must adapt to *concept drift*.

## 2.2 Core SCM Functions Transformed by AI/ML

| SCM Function | Traditional Approach | AI/ML-Enhanced Approach |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Demand Forecasting** | Moving averages, exponential smoothing | LSTM neural networks, XGBoost, Prophet |
| **Inventory Optimization** | EOQ, safety stock formulas | Reinforcement learning for dynamic reorder points |
| **Supplier Selection** | Manual scoring, RFPs | Multi-criteria decision analysis with NLP on contracts |
| **Transportation Routing** | Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) heuristics | Real-time route optimization using Graph Neural Networks |
| **Warehouse Operations** | Static slotting | Computer vision for automated picking, robotic process automation |
| **Risk Management** | Historical risk reports | Predictive risk scoring using satellite imagery & news sentiment |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **mathematically verifiable** and form the quantitative backbone of AI-driven SCM analytics. Master these for numerical problems in Part B.

| Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)** | $\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{100\%}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left \vert \dfrac{A_i - F_i}{A_i} \right \vert$ | $A_i$ = Actual demand, $F_i$ = Forecast, $n$ = number of periods | % (lower is better) |
| **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (A_i - F_i)^2}$ | Penalizes large errors quadratically | Same as $A_i$ units |
| **Forecast Bias (Tracking Signal)** | $\text{TS} = \dfrac{\sum (A_i - F_i)}{\text{MAD}}$ | MAD = Mean Absolute Deviation | Dimensionless |
| **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$ = Annual demand, $S$ = Ordering cost, $H$ = Holding cost per unit | Units |
| **Safety Stock (AI-Adjusted)** | $\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{\mu_d}$ | $z$ = service-level z-score, $\sigma_{LT}$ = std. dev. of lead time, $\mu_d$ = mean demand | Units |
| **Service Level Probability** | $P(X \leq \text{Stock}) = \Phi(z)$ | $\Phi$ = Standard normal CDF | Probability (0–1) |
| **Neural Network Output (Perceptron)** | $y = \sigma\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$ | $w_i$ = weights, $b$ = bias, $\sigma$ = activation function | Output value |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility & Industry Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU expects you to know this:**
> The 2024 scheme emphasizes **Industry 4.0 readiness**. AI/ML is the operational engine of **Smart Supply Chains (SSC)** and **Digital Twins**. Companies like **Amazon** (anticipatory shipping), **Walmart** (ML-driven inventory), **Maersk** (predictive container maintenance), and **FedEx** (AI-driven route optimization) are benchmark implementations. For a B.Tech Industrial Engineering student, this knowledge bridges **operations research** with **modern data science**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1: Calculating MAPE for Demand Forecasting

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-style Numerical Problem**
> A regional warehouse recorded the following actual vs. AI-forecasted demand (in units) for 5 weeks. Calculate the **MAPE** and comment on the model's accuracy.

| Week | Actual Demand ($A_i$) | AI Forecast ($F_i$) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 120 | 110 |
| 2 | 135 | 140 |
| 3 | 150 | 145 |
| 4 | 160 | 170 |
| 5 | 140 | 138 |

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Recall the MAPE formula from the cheat sheet.

$$
\text{MAPE} = \frac{100\%}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left \vert \frac{A_i - F_i}{A_i} \right \vert
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the absolute percentage error for each week.

$$
\text{Week 1: } \left \vert \frac{120 - 110}{120} \right \vert = \left \vert \frac{10}{120} \right \vert \approx 0.0833
$$

$$
\text{Week 2: } \left \vert \frac{135 - 140}{135} \right \vert = \left \vert \frac{-5}{135} \right \vert \approx 0.0370
$$

$$
\text{Week 3: } \left \vert \frac{150 - 145}{150} \right \vert = \left \vert \frac{5}{150} \right \vert \approx 0.0333
$$

$$
\text{Week 4: } \left \vert \frac{160 - 170}{160} \right \vert = \left \vert \frac{-10}{160} \right \vert = 0.0625
$$

$$
\text{Week 5: } \left \vert \frac{140 - 138}{140} \right \vert = \left \vert \frac{2}{140} \right \vert \approx 0.0143
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the absolute percentage errors.

$$
\sum = 0.0833 + 0.0370 + 0.0333 + 0.0625 + 0.0143 = 0.2304
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $\frac{100\%}{n}$ where $n = 5$.

$$
\text{MAPE} = \frac{100\%}{5} \times 0.2304 = 20\% \times 0.2304 = 4.608\%
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**
A MAPE of **4.608%** indicates a **highly accurate** forecasting model. Industry rule of thumb:
- MAPE < 10% → Highly accurate
- 10% – 20% → Good
- 20% – 50% → Reasonable
- > 50% → Inaccurate

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2: AI-Adjusted Safety Stock

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement**
> A retailer wants a **95% service level** ($z = 1.65$). Historical data shows: average daily demand $\mu_d = 50$ units, standard deviation of lead time $\sigma_{LT} = 2$ days. Calculate the **AI-adjusted safety stock**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the safety stock formula from the cheat sheet.

$$
\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{\mu_d}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values.

$$
\text{SS} = 1.65 \times 2 \times \sqrt{50}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the square root.

$$
\sqrt{50} = 7.0711
$$

**Step 4:** Perform the multiplication.

$$
\text{SS} = 1.65 \times 2 \times 7.0711 = 3.30 \times 7.0711
$$

$$
\text{SS} \approx 23.33 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5: Round up** (safety stock is always rounded **up** to the next whole unit to ensure no stockout).

$$
\text{SS} = 24 \text{ units}
$$

**Interpretation:** The retailer must hold an **extra buffer of 24 units** above the expected lead-time demand to maintain a 95% probability of not stocking out. In AI-enhanced systems, $\sigma_{LT}$ and $\mu_d$ are dynamically updated in real time, allowing this safety stock to fluctuate with market conditions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing AI/ML Techniques in SCM

| ML Technique | SCM Problem Solved | Strength | Limitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linear Regression** | Basic demand forecasting | Simple, interpretable | Cannot capture non-linear patterns |
| **Random Forest** | Supplier risk classification | Handles mixed data types | Less effective on time-series |
| **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)** | Sequential demand forecasting | Captures long-term dependencies | Computationally expensive |
| **K-Means Clustering** | Customer segmentation | Unsupervised, fast | Requires pre-specifying $k$ |
| **Q-Learning (Reinforcement)** | Dynamic inventory replenishment | Adapts to changing demand | Slow convergence |
| **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)** | Visual quality inspection in warehouses | High accuracy in image tasks | Requires large labeled datasets |
| **Graph Neural Networks (GNN)** | Supply network risk propagation | Models supplier-supplier relationships | Emerging, less mature tooling |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AI/ML Integration Architecture in a Supply Chain

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** illustrates how AI/ML modules integrate with classical supply chain infrastructure.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeDataSources[Data Sources Layer] --> nodePreprocess[Data Preprocessing Engine]
    nodeERP[ERP Systems] --> nodePreprocess
    nodeIoT[IoT Sensors and RFID] --> nodePreprocess
    nodeWeather[External Feeds Weather News] --> nodePreprocess
    nodePreprocess --> nodeFeatureEng[Feature Engineering Module]
    nodeFeatureEng --> nodeModelTrain[Model Training Engine]
    nodeModelTrain --> nodeSupervised[Supervised Learning Models]
    nodeModelTrain --> nodeUnsupervised[Unsupervised Learning Models]
    nodeModelTrain --> nodeRL[Reinforcement Learning Agent]
    nodeSupervised --> nodeInference[Real Time Inference Layer]
    nodeUnsupervised --> nodeInference
    nodeRL --> nodeInference
    nodeInference --> nodeDemandForecast[Demand Forecasting Dashboard]
    nodeInference --> nodeInventoryOpt[Inventory Optimization System]
    nodeInference --> nodeRouteOpt[Transportation Route Optimizer]
    nodeInference --> nodeRiskScore[Supplier Risk Scoring Engine]
    nodeInference --> nodeMaintenance[Predictive Maintenance Module]
    nodeDemandForecast --> nodeDecision[Decision Support and Human Override]
    nodeInventoryOpt --> nodeDecision
    nodeRouteOpt --> nodeDecision
    nodeRiskScore --> nodeDecision
    nodeMaintenance --> nodeDecision
    nodeDecision --> nodeExec[Execution Layer Warehouse Transport Procurement]
    nodeExec -.feedback loop.-> nodePreprocess
```

## 4.2 Demand Forecasting Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Historical Sales Data] --> nodeB[Data Cleaning and Outlier Removal]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Feature Engineering Lag Variables Holidays Promotions]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Algorithm Selection ARIMA vs LSTM vs XGBoost]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Model Training on 80 Percent Data]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Validation on 20 Percent Holdout]
    nodeF --> nodeG{MAPE less than 10 Percent}
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH[Deploy to Production]
    nodeG -- No --> nodeD
    nodeH --> nodeI[Continuous Monitoring and Retraining]
```

## 4.3 ML Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Start: Define SCM Problem] --> nodeQ1{Is data labeled?}
    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeQ2{Is it a regression problem?}
    nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeUnsup[Use Unsupervised: K-Means, PCA, Anomaly Detection]
    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeReg[Use Regression: Linear, Random Forest, XGBoost]
    nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeClass[Use Classification: Logistic Regression, SVM, Neural Net]
    nodeQ1 -.Alternative.- nodeRL2{Need sequential decisions?}
    nodeRL2 -- Yes --> nodeRLApp[Use Reinforcement Learning: Q-Learning, Deep Q-Network]
    nodeRL2 -- No --> nodeQ1
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]**
> **Q1. Define Artificial Intelligence in the context of Supply Chain Management. List any two applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Artificial Intelligence in SCM refers to the use of computer systems that simulate human intelligence to automate and optimize supply chain operations such as forecasting, inventory, and logistics. **(2 marks)**
Two applications: (i) AI-driven **demand forecasting** using neural networks, and (ii) **predictive maintenance** of fleet vehicles using IoT sensor data. **(1 mark)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between Supervised and Unsupervised Learning with one supply chain example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Supervised Learning** uses labeled input-output data to train models; *Example:* predicting next month's sales from past sales data (regression). **(1.5 marks)**
**Unsupervised Learning** finds hidden patterns in unlabeled data; *Example:* segmenting customers into groups based on purchasing behavior (clustering). **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question — Question A]**

### Question A: (a) Explain the role of Machine Learning in demand forecasting. Discuss any two ML algorithms used for this purpose. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
Demand forecasting is the backbone of supply chain planning. Traditional statistical methods like moving averages fail to capture complex, non-linear relationships in modern, volatile markets. Machine Learning (ML) overcomes this by learning from large, multi-dimensional historical datasets (sales, promotions, weather, economic indicators) to predict future demand with high accuracy.

**Algorithm 1: Random Forest Regression (2.5 marks):**
Random Forest is an ensemble learning method that constructs multiple decision trees during training and outputs the average prediction. In demand forecasting, it handles mixed data types (numerical + categorical) and captures non-linear interactions between features like price, season, and competitor activity. It is robust to outliers and provides feature importance rankings, helping managers understand which factors drive demand most strongly.

**Algorithm 2: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) Networks (2.5 marks):**
LSTM is a specialized Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture designed to remember long-term dependencies in sequential data. Unlike traditional models, LSTM retains information from many past time steps through gated memory cells. For demand forecasting, this is crucial for capturing seasonality — for instance, ice-cream demand spikes every summer, and LSTM "remembers" this pattern across years. It excels in volatile, highly seasonal supply chains (apparel, FMCG, e-commerce).

---

### Question A: (b) A logistics company recorded the following data for monthly demand (in tons) and AI-based forecast. Compute the MAPE and RMSE. Comment on the model's accuracy. (7 marks)

| Month | Actual ($A_i$) | Forecast ($F_i$) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 200 | 210 |
| 2 | 220 | 215 |
| 3 | 250 | 245 |
| 4 | 230 | 240 |
| 5 | 260 | 255 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate errors and squared errors.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Month 1: } & A_1 - F_1 = 200 - 210 = -10, \quad (-10)^2 = 100 \\
\text{Month 2: } & A_2 - F_2 = 220 - 215 = 5, \quad (5)^2 = 25 \\
\text{Month 3: } & A_3 - F_3 = 250 - 245 = 5, \quad (5)^2 = 25 \\
\text{Month 4: } & A_4 - F_4 = 230 - 240 = -10, \quad (-10)^2 = 100 \\
\text{Month 5: } & A_5 - F_5 = 260 - 255 = 5, \quad (5)^2 = 25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute MAPE.**

$$
\text{MAPE} = \frac{100\%}{5} \left[ \frac{10}{200} + \frac{5}{220} + \frac{5}{250} + \frac{10}{230} + \frac{5}{260} \right]
$$

$$
\text{MAPE} = 20\% \times \left[ 0.0500 + 0.0227 + 0.0200 + 0.0435 + 0.0192 \right]
$$

$$
\text{MAPE} = 20\% \times 0.1554 = 3.108\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute RMSE.**

$$
\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{5} (100 + 25 + 25 + 100 + 25)} = \sqrt{\frac{275}{5}} = \sqrt{55} \approx 7.416 \text{ tons}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation (1 mark).**
MAPE of **3.108%** and RMSE of **7.416 tons** both indicate a **highly accurate** forecasting model. RMSE penalizes the two larger errors (Months 1 & 4) more heavily, which is useful for risk-averse inventory planning.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question — Question B (Alternative Choice)]**

### Question B: (a) Discuss the application of AI/ML in inventory optimization. How does reinforcement learning improve upon traditional EOQ models? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**AI/ML in Inventory Optimization (3 marks):**
AI/ML transforms static inventory models into dynamic, self-adjusting systems. ML algorithms analyze real-time sales velocity, lead-time variability, and external signals (weather, festivals) to dynamically adjust **reorder points (ROP)** and **safety stock levels**. Predictive models can identify slow-moving SKUs that need clearance, and fast-moving SKUs that need buffer stock, enabling **portfolio-level optimization**.

**Reinforcement Learning vs. Traditional EOQ (4 marks):**
Traditional EOQ assumes **constant, known** demand and lead times — a flawed assumption in modern supply chains.
**Reinforcement Learning (RL)** addresses this through:
- **State:** Current inventory level, pending orders, demand rate.
- **Action:** Order quantity at each decision epoch.
- **Reward:** Penalty for stockouts (lost sales) and holding costs (excess inventory).
The RL agent learns an **optimal policy** by trial-and-error, continuously adapting to demand shocks. Over time, it discovers non-obvious strategies (e.g., ordering slightly more on Mondays to avoid weekend stockouts) that fixed EOQ formulas cannot capture. This results in **10–30% reductions in total inventory costs** in real-world deployments.

---

### Question B: (b) A pharmaceutical company wants a 97.5% service level ($z = 2.05$). Average daily demand is 80 units, with a standard deviation of lead time = 3 days. Calculate the safety stock. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the AI-adjusted safety stock formula.

$$
\text{SS} = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{\mu_d}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $z = 2.05$, $\sigma_{LT} = 3$, $\mu_d = 80$.

$$
\text{SS} = 2.05 \times 3 \times \sqrt{80}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate $\sqrt{80}$.

$$
\sqrt{80} = 8.9443
$$

**Step 4:** Final multiplication.

$$
\text{SS} = 6.15 \times 8.9443 \approx 55.01 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5: Round up.**

$$
\text{SS} = 56 \text{ units}
$$

**Interpretation:** The company must maintain a buffer of **56 units** to achieve a 97.5% probability of meeting demand during lead time. In AI-driven systems, this value is recalculated **hourly** based on streaming demand data.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing AI with ML:** AI is the broader concept; ML is a *subset*. Writing "AI = ML" will lose a mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert MAPE to percentage:** Students often give MAPE as 0.0461 instead of 4.61%. Always multiply by 100%.
> 3. **Rounding safety stock DOWN:** Safety stock must ALWAYS be rounded **up** to the next integer. Rounding down violates the service-level guarantee.
> 4. **Using absolute value bars in markdown tables:** This breaks table rendering. Use $\vert x \vert$ notation in LaTeX.
> 5. **Generic answers on Reinforcement Learning:** KTU examiners want **State, Action, Reward** framework explicitly mentioned.
> 6. **Ignoring units in RMSE:** RMSE must always be reported with its unit (e.g., "7.42 tons" not just "7.42").

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - **AI** = the broad field of intelligent machines; **ML** = a subset of AI that learns from data.
> - Three ML types: **Supervised** (labeled), **Unsupervised** (unlabeled), **Reinforcement** (reward-based).
> - **MAPE** measures average percentage error; **RMSE** penalizes large errors quadratically.
> - **LSTM** is preferred for sequential/time-series data (demand forecasting).
> - **Random Forest** is robust for classification & regression on mixed datasets.
> - **RL** outperforms static **EOQ** in dynamic, uncertain demand environments.
> - **Safety stock** formula: $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{\mu_d}$ — always round up.
> - Key SCM applications: demand forecasting, inventory optimization, route planning, predictive maintenance, supplier risk scoring.
> - AI/ML enables **real-time, self-correcting** supply chains — the foundation of **Industry 4.0**.
> - Always tie algorithmic choice to the **business problem**, not the other way around.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forecasting Models for Supply Chains Including Seasonal Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET502 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forecasting Models for Supply Chains Including Seasonal Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Forecasting** in supply chain management is the systematic process of estimating future demand for products or services by analyzing historical data, market intelligence, and quantitative models. It is the foundational input for virtually every operational decision in a supply chain — including inventory planning, capacity scheduling, procurement, production planning, distribution, and transportation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Forecasting is the estimation of future demand using mathematical/statistical models applied to historical time-series data, enabling proactive supply chain decisions. It converts *historical demand signals* into *forward-looking operational plans*.

According to Chopra & Meindl's *Supply Chain Management* framework (the standard KTU reference for PCIET502), forecasting serves as the **primary interface between demand and supply planning**, where errors propagate downstream as the **bullwhip effect**.

### 1.2 Classification of Forecasting Methods

Forecasting models are broadly classified into two families:

| Category | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Qualitative Methods** | Based on judgment, expert opinion, Delphi technique, market research | New product launches, no historical data |
| **Quantitative Methods** | Based on mathematical/statistical models applied to historical data | Mature products with reliable history |

> [!NOTE]
> Quantitative forecasting is the **core focus of Module 2** under the KTU 2024 scheme and includes time-series models, causal/regression models, and—critically—**seasonal models**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Weather Predictor"

Imagine you are a **Kerala monsoon farmer** deciding when to harvest and how much to stockpile. You don't plant rice based on guesswork — you study:
- The **last 10 years' rainfall** (time-series data),
- The **pattern of monsoon onset** (seasonality),
- The **yearly trend** of rainfall intensity (trend),
- **Sudden floods or droughts** (irregular/random component).

Forecasting in supply chains is identical in spirit:
- **Time-series data** = historical demand records.
- **Seasonality** = predictable swings (e.g., Onam sales spike every August–September, winter apparel peaks in December).
- **Trend** = long-term growth or decline.
- **Irregular component** = random noise (e.g., a one-day flash sale, supply disruption).

Just as a good weather forecast is the difference between a successful harvest and a ruined crop, a good demand forecast is the difference between an efficient supply chain and a costly inventory nightmare.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Forecasting Accuracy)

These metrics determine *how good* a forecast is:

- **MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation):** Average of absolute forecast errors.
- **MSE (Mean Squared Error):** Average of squared errors — penalizes large errors heavily.
- **MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error):** Average percentage error — scale-independent.
- **Tracking Signal (TS):** Cumulative forecast bias measure used for forecast control.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask students to *choose the right metric*. Use **MAD** for inventory decisions, **MSE** when large errors are unacceptable, and **MAPE** to compare accuracy across products with different scales.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Time-Series Components

A time-series $D_t$ (demand at time $t$) is decomposed into four components:

$$
D_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t \quad \text{(Additive Model)}
$$

$$
D_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t \quad \text{(Multiplicative Model)}
$$

Where:
- $T_t$ = **Trend Component** (long-term direction).
- $S_t$ = **Seasonal Component** (predictable periodic fluctuation).
- $C_t$ = **Cyclical Component** (multi-year economic/business cycles).
- $I_t$ = **Irregular/Random Component** (unexplained noise).

> [!NOTE]
> The **multiplicative model** is preferred when seasonal variation grows proportionally with the trend (e.g., Diwali sales increase *and* the base demand is also growing). The **additive model** is used when seasonal swings are roughly constant in absolute terms.

### 2.2 Classification of Quantitative Forecasting Models

#### 2.2.1 Naïve Forecasting
The simplest method — next period's forecast equals the last observed demand:
$$
F_{t+1} = D_t
$$
- Used as a **benchmark**; surprisingly competitive for stable series.

#### 2.2.2 Simple Moving Average (SMA)
Averages the last $n$ observations:
$$
F_{t+1} = \frac{D_t + D_{t-1} + D_{t-2} + \cdots + D_{t-n+1}}{n} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} D_{t-i}
$$
- Smooths out random fluctuations.
- Reacts **slowly** to changes (more lag with larger $n$).
- Gives **equal weight** to all $n$ observations.

#### 2.2.3 Weighted Moving Average (WMA)
Assigns different weights to recent vs. older observations:
$$
F_{t+1} = w_1 D_t + w_2 D_{t-1} + w_3 D_{t-2} + \cdots + w_n D_{t-n+1}
$$
- Constraint: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$
- Recent data gets higher weight (e.g., $w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2$).

#### 2.2.4 Single Exponential Smoothing (SES)
Recursively updates the forecast using a smoothing constant $\alpha \in [0, 1]$:
$$
F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t
$$
- **High $\alpha$ (close to 1):** Reacts quickly to changes but is noisy.
- **Low $\alpha$ (close to 0):** Smooth, but slow to react.
- Optimal $\alpha$ minimizes MAD/MSE on historical data.

#### 2.2.5 Holt's Two-Parameter Linear Method (Trend)
For series with **trend but no seasonality**:
$$
L_t = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}) \quad \text{(Level equation)}
$$

$$
T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1} \quad \text{(Trend equation)}
$$

$$
F_{t+p} = L_t + p \cdot T_t \quad \text{(Forecast } p \text{ periods ahead)}
$$

- $L_t$ = smoothed level.
- $T_t$ = smoothed trend.
- $\alpha, \beta \in [0,1]$ are smoothing constants.

#### 2.2.6 Holt-Winters' Seasonal Method (Triple Exponential Smoothing)
For series with **trend AND seasonality**. Two variants exist:

**Multiplicative Seasonal Model:**
$$
L_t = \alpha \frac{D_t}{S_{t-s}} + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})
$$

$$
T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}
$$

$$
S_t = \gamma \frac{D_t}{L_t} + (1-\gamma)S_{t-s}
$$

$$
F_{t+p} = (L_t + p \cdot T_t) \cdot S_{t+p-s}
$$

**Additive Seasonal Model:**
$$
L_t = \alpha(D_t - S_{t-s}) + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})
$$

$$
T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}
$$

$$
S_t = \gamma(D_t - L_t) + (1-\gamma)S_{t-s}
$$

$$
F_{t+p} = L_t + p \cdot T_t + S_{t+p-s}
$$

Where $s$ = length of seasonal cycle (e.g., $s=4$ for quarterly, $s=12$ for monthly), and $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are smoothing constants.

### 2.3 Seasonal Index Calculation (Classical Decomposition)

The seasonal index captures *how much* a particular period deviates from the average.

**Step-by-step method:**

1. Compute the **centered moving average** of length $s$ to remove seasonality.
2. Compute the **ratio** (multiplicative) or **difference** (additive) of actual demand to/from the centered moving average — this isolates $S_t \times I_t$.
3. **Group** these ratios by season (e.g., all Q1 values together, all Q2 values, etc.).
4. **Average** the ratios within each group.
5. **Normalize** so that the average index equals 1.0 (multiplicative) or 0.0 (additive):
   - For multiplicative: divide each group average by the mean of all group averages.
   - For additive: subtract the mean of all group averages from each.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sum of multiplicative seasonal indices = $s$ (number of seasons). Sum of additive seasonal indices = 0.

### 2.4 Forecast Accuracy Metrics (Detailed)

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert D_t - F_t \vert
$$

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (D_t - F_t)^2
$$

$$
\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{D_t - F_t}{D_t} \right\vert \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Bias (CFE)} = \sum_{t=1}^{n}(D_t - F_t)
$$

$$
\text{Tracking Signal (TS)} = \frac{\text{CFE}}{\text{MAD}}
$$

- If $\vert \text{TS} \vert > 4$ (commonly used control limit) for several periods → forecast is **biased** and needs adjustment.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — these are the only formulas tested in KTU exams on this topic.**

| Model | Core Equation | When to Use | Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Naïve | $F_{t+1} = D_t$ | Benchmark; stable series | None |
| Simple Moving Average | $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} D_{t-i}$ | Stationary, no trend/seasonality | Window $n$ |
| Weighted Moving Average | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i D_{t-i+1}$ | Recent data more relevant | Weights $w_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| Single Exp. Smoothing | $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ | Stationary, no trend | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ |
| Holt's Linear | $L_t = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1}+T_{t-1})$; $F_{t+p} = L_t + pT_t$ | Trend, no seasonality | $\alpha, \beta$ |
| Holt-Winters Multiplicative | $F_{t+p} = (L_t + pT_t) \cdot S_{t+p-s}$ | Trend + proportional seasonality | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, s$ |
| Holt-Winters Additive | $F_{t+p} = L_t + pT_t + S_{t+p-s}$ | Trend + constant seasonality | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, s$ |
| MAD | $\text{MAD} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \vert D_t - F_t \vert$ | Inventory control | Lower = better |
| MAPE | $\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \vert (D_t-F_t)/D_t \vert \times 100\%$ | Cross-product comparison | Lower = better |
| Tracking Signal | $\text{TS} = \text{CFE} / \text{MAD}$ | Bias detection | $\vert \text{TS} \vert \leq 4$ acceptable |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Inventory Management:** EOQ and safety stock formulas depend on forecast accuracy; a 10% MAPE reduction can cut inventory carrying cost by 3–5%.
- **Production Scheduling:** Holt's method drives aggregate production planning (APP) for 6–18 month horizons.
- **Retail & E-commerce:** Holt-Winters multiplicative is industry standard for fashion, FMCG, and electronics (highly seasonal demand).
- **Workforce Planning:** Seasonal forecasts drive hiring surges (e.g., logistics companies hiring for festive season).
- **Procurement & Sourcing:** Long-lead-time items (semiconductors, specialty chemicals) require Holt's or causal models for procurement contracts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Example 1 — Simple Moving Average (SMA)

**Problem:** A retail store recorded the following monthly demand (in units) for a product over 6 months:

| Month $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand $D_t$ | 100 | 110 | 95 | 120 | 105 | 115 |

Compute a **3-month Simple Moving Average** forecast for months 4, 5, 6, and the **MAD**.

**Step 1: Recall the SMA formula.**

$$
F_{t+1} = \frac{D_t + D_{t-1} + D_{t-2}}{3}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $F_4$ (forecast for month 4).**

$$
F_4 = \frac{D_3 + D_2 + D_1}{3} = \frac{95 + 110 + 100}{3} = \frac{305}{3} \approx 101.67 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $F_5$ (forecast for month 5).**

$$
F_5 = \frac{D_4 + D_3 + D_2}{3} = \frac{120 + 95 + 110}{3} = \frac{325}{3} \approx 108.33 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $F_6$ (forecast for month 6).**

$$
F_6 = \frac{D_5 + D_4 + D_3}{3} = \frac{105 + 120 + 95}{3} = \frac{320}{3} \approx 106.67 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5: Compute forecast errors $e_t = D_t - F_t$.**

| Month $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 120 | 101.67 | +18.33 | 18.33 |
| 5 | 105 | 108.33 | −3.33 | 3.33 |
| 6 | 115 | 106.67 | +8.33 | 8.33 |

**Step 6: Compute MAD.**

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{18.33 + 3.33 + 8.33}{3} = \frac{29.99}{3} \approx 10.00 \text{ units}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 3-month SMA forecast has a MAD of 10 units. The store should maintain at least this much safety buffer to handle average forecast error.

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — Single Exponential Smoothing (SES)

**Problem:** Using the same demand data, apply SES with $\alpha = 0.3$ and initial forecast $F_1 = D_1 = 100$. Compute $F_2, F_3, \ldots, F_6$ and the MAD.

**Step 1: Recall the SES formula.**

$$
F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t
$$

**Step 2: Compute $F_2$.**

$$
F_2 = \alpha D_1 + (1-\alpha) F_1 = (0.3)(100) + (0.7)(100) = 100.00
$$

**Step 3: Compute $F_3$.**

$$
F_3 = (0.3)(110) + (0.7)(100) = 33 + 70 = 103.00
$$

**Step 4: Compute $F_4$.**

$$
F_4 = (0.3)(95) + (0.7)(103) = 28.5 + 72.1 = 100.60
$$

**Step 5: Compute $F_5$.**

$$
F_5 = (0.3)(120) + (0.7)(100.6) = 36 + 70.42 = 106.42
$$

**Step 6: Compute $F_6$.**

$$
F_6 = (0.3)(105) + (0.7)(106.42) = 31.5 + 74.494 = 105.99 \approx 106.00
$$

**Step 7: Forecast for month 7 (forward projection).**

$$
F_7 = (0.3)(115) + (0.7)(106.00) = 34.5 + 74.20 = 108.70 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 8: Compute MAD across $t=2$ to $6$.**

| $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 110 | 100.00 | +10.00 | 10.00 |
| 3 | 95 | 103.00 | −8.00 | 8.00 |
| 4 | 120 | 100.60 | +19.40 | 19.40 |
| 5 | 105 | 106.42 | −1.42 | 1.42 |
| 6 | 115 | 106.00 | +9.00 | 9.00 |

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{10 + 8 + 19.4 + 1.42 + 9}{5} = \frac{47.82}{5} \approx 9.56 \text{ units}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> With $\alpha = 0.3$, MAD (9.56) is **lower** than the SMA's MAD (10.00). SES adapts better to recent shifts, but for this short series, the difference is small. For longer series, $\alpha$ should be optimized to minimize MAD/MSE.

---

### 3.3 Example 3 — Seasonal Index Calculation (Multiplicative Model, $s = 4$ Quarters)

**Problem:** A company has 3 years of quarterly demand data:

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2022 | 100 | 120 | 140 | 110 |
| 2023 | 110 | 130 | 155 | 115 |
| 2024 | 120 | 140 | 170 | 125 |

Compute the **multiplicative seasonal indices** for each quarter.

**Step 1: Compute the 4-period centered moving average (CMA).**

The standard centered MA for even $s=4$ is the average of two consecutive 4-period MAs:

$$
\text{CMA}_t = \frac{0.5 \cdot D_{t-2} + D_{t-1} + D_t + D_{t+1} + 0.5 \cdot D_{t+2}}{4}
$$

**For Q2 of 2022 (t = Q2 2022):**

$$
\text{CMA}_{\text{Q2 2022}} = \frac{0.5(100) + 120 + 140 + 110 + 0.5(\text{Q3 2021})}{4}
$$

> We don't have Q3 2021, so we compute CMA starting from Q3 2022.

**CMA for Q3 2022:**

$$
\text{CMA}_{\text{Q3 2022}} = \frac{0.5(120) + 140 + 110 + 110 + 0.5(130)}{4} = \frac{60 + 140 + 110 + 110 + 65}{4} = \frac{485}{4} = 121.25
$$

**CMA for Q4 2022:**

$$
\text{CMA}_{\text{Q4 2022}} = \frac{0.5(140) + 110 + 110 + 130 + 0.5(140)}{4} = \frac{70 + 110 + 110 + 130 + 70}{4} = \frac{490}{4} = 122.50
$$

**CMA for Q1 2023:**

$$
\text{CMA}_{\text{Q1 2023}} = \frac{0.5(110) + 110 + 130 + 155 + 0.5(120)}{4} = \frac{55 + 110 + 130 + 155 + 60}{4} = \frac{510}{4} = 127.50
$$

Continuing in this manner for all 10 interior points:

| Period | $D_t$ | CMA | Ratio $D_t / \text{CMA}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q3 2022 | 140 | 121.25 | 1.1546 |
| Q4 2022 | 110 | 122.50 | 0.8980 |
| Q1 2023 | 110 | 127.50 | 0.8627 |
| Q2 2023 | 130 | 132.50 | 0.9811 |
| Q3 2023 | 155 | 136.25 | 1.1376 |
| Q4 2023 | 115 | 140.00 | 0.8214 |
| Q1 2024 | 120 | 145.00 | 0.8276 |
| Q2 2024 | 140 | 150.00 | 0.9333 |
| Q3 2024 | 170 | 155.00 | 1.0968 |
| Q4 2024 | 125 | — | — |

**Step 2: Group the ratios by quarter and average them.**

- **Q1 ratios:** 0.8627, 0.8276 → Average = $(0.8627 + 0.8276)/2 = 0.8452$
- **Q2 ratios:** 0.9811, 0.9333 → Average = $(0.9811 + 0.9333)/2 = 0.9572$
- **Q3 ratios:** 1.1546, 1.1376, 1.0968 → Average = $(1.1546 + 1.1376 + 1.0968)/3 = 1.1297$
- **Q4 ratios:** 0.8980, 0.8214 → Average = $(0.8980 + 0.8214)/2 = 0.8597$

**Step 3: Normalize the indices so they sum to $s = 4$.**

Mean of averages: $(0.8452 + 0.9572 + 1.1297 + 0.8597)/4 = 3.7918/4 = 0.9480$

$$
S_i^{\text{normalized}} = \frac{S_i^{\text{avg}}}{0.9480}
$$

- $S_{\text{Q1}} = 0.8452 / 0.9480 = 0.8916$
- $S_{\text{Q2}} = 0.9572 / 0.9480 = 1.0097$
- $S_{\text{Q3}} = 1.1297 / 0.9480 = 1.1917$
- $S_{\text{Q4}} = 0.8597 / 0.9480 = 0.9068$

**Verification:** $0.8916 + 1.0097 + 1.1917 + 0.9068 = 3.9998 \approx 4.00$ ✓

**Final Multiplicative Seasonal Indices:**

| Quarter | Seasonal Index | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 | **0.8916** | Demand is ~10.8% below average in Q1 |
| Q2 | **1.0097** | Demand is ~1.0% above average in Q2 |
| Q3 | **1.1917** | Demand is ~19.2% above average in Q3 (peak) |
| Q4 | **0.9068** | Demand is ~9.3% below average in Q4 |

> [!TIP]
> These indices can now be used to deseasonalize data for trend analysis, or to **reseasonalize** a trend forecast: $F^{\text{final}}_{t} = T_t \times S_t$.

---

### 3.4 Example 4 — Deseasonalization and Trend Forecast

**Problem:** Using the seasonal indices from Example 3, deseasonalize the 2024 demand and fit a linear trend.

**Step 1: Deseasonalize using $D_t^{\text{deseasonalized}} = D_t / S_t$.**

- Q1 2024: $120 / 0.8916 = 134.59$
- Q2 2024: $140 / 1.0097 = 138.65$
- Q3 2024: $170 / 1.1917 = 142.65$
- Q4 2024: $125 / 0.9068 = 137.85$

**Step 2: Fit a linear regression $T_t = a + b \cdot t$ on deseasonalized data.**

Using least-squares (omitted detailed calculation for brevity; KTU students may use normal equations or a calculator):

Slope $b \approx 1.5$/quarter; Intercept $a \approx 132$ (at $t=1$).

**Step 3: Forecast deseasonalized Q1 2025 ($t = 13$).**

$$
T_{13} = 132 + 1.5 \times 13 = 132 + 19.5 = 151.5
$$

**Step 4: Reseasonalize using Q1 index.**

$$
F_{\text{Q1 2025}} = 151.5 \times 0.8916 \approx 135.08 \text{ units}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End-to-end seasonal forecasting pipeline:** Raw data → Compute CMA → Derive seasonal indices → Deseasonalize → Fit trend → Forecast trend → Reseasonalize → Final forecast.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Selection Matrix — Which Model to Choose?

| Data Characteristic | Recommended Model | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| No trend, no seasonality, stationary | **Naïve / SMA / SES** | Simplicity, no overfitting |
| Trend, no seasonality | **Holt's Linear** | Captures both level and slope |
| Trend + proportional seasonality | **Holt-Winters Multiplicative** | Handles growing seasonal swings |
| Trend + constant seasonality | **Holt-Winters Additive** | Seasonal swing is roughly constant |
| Strong cyclic patterns (multi-year) | **Causal/Regression + Seasonal Indices** | Requires exogenous economic indicators |
| Very short series ($n < 10$) | **Naïve or SMA** | Avoid overfitting; insufficient data for parameters |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Forecasting Process — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Historical Demand Data] --> B[Clean and Preprocess Data]
    B --> C{Identify Time-Series Components}
    C -->|Trend Present| D[Apply Trend Model: Holt]
    C -->|Seasonality Present| E[Compute Seasonal Indices]
    C -->|Both Present| F[Apply Holt-Winters]
    C -->|Neither| G[Apply SES or SMA]
    D --> H[Generate Forecast]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Accuracy Metrics: MAD, MAPE, TS]
    I --> J{Acceptable Accuracy?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Deploy Forecast to Supply Chain]
    J -->|No| L[Tune Parameters: alpha, beta, gamma]
    L --> H
    K --> M[Monitor Tracking Signal]
    M -->|TS exceeds limits| N[Re-train Model]
    N --> A
```

### 4.2 Time-Series Decomposition — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Time Series D sub t] --> B[Decomposition Engine]
    B --> C[Trend Component T sub t]
    B --> D[Seasonal Component S sub t]
    B --> E[Cyclical Component C sub t]
    B --> F[Irregular Component I sub t]
    C --> G[Additive or Multiplicative Combiner]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Reconstructed Series D sub t]
    H --> I[Forecasting Engine]
    I --> J[Forecast F sub t+1]
    J --> K[Accuracy Evaluation MAD, MAPE]
    K --> L[Decision: Adjust Parameters or Deploy]
```

### 4.3 Holt-Winters Multiplicative — Internal Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: D sub t] --> B[Level Update: L sub t]
    B --> C[Trend Update: T sub t]
    C --> D[Seasonal Update: S sub t]
    D --> E[Storage: Lagged Values L sub t-1, T sub t-1, S sub t-s]
    E --> F[Forecast Engine: F sub t+p = L + pT times S]
    F --> G[Output: Forecasted Demand]
    G --> H[Feedback to Error Computation]
    H --> I[Parameter Optimizer: alpha, beta, gamma]
    I --> B
```

### 4.4 Forecast Error Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Actual Demand D sub t] --> B[Compare with Forecast F sub t]
    B --> C[Error e sub t = D - F]
    C --> D[Update MAD]
    C --> E[Update CFE - Cumulative]
    D --> F[Tracking Signal TS = CFE / MAD]
    F --> G{TS in range minus 4 to plus 4?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Forecast Acceptable - Continue]
    G -->|No| I[Forecast Biased - Trigger Re-training]
    I --> J[Adjust Smoothing Constants or Model Type]
    J --> K[Re-run Forecast]
    K --> H
```

### 4.5 Model Selection Decision Matrix (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Analyze Historical Data] --> B{Trend?}
    B -->|No| C{Seasonality?}
    B -->|Yes| D{Seasonality?}
    C -->|No| E[Use Naive or SES or SMA]
    C -->|Yes| F[Use Seasonal Decomposition plus SES]
    D -->|No| G[Use Holt Linear]
    D -->|Yes| H{Constant or Proportional Swings?}
    H -->|Constant| I[Use Holt-Winters Additive]
    H -->|Proportional| J[Use Holt-Winters Multiplicative]
    E --> K[Validate with MAD and MAPE]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Accuracy Acceptable?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Deploy Model]
    L -->|No| N[Re-examine Data or Try Alternative Model]
    N --> A
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Q: Define forecasting. List any two qualitative methods of forecasting used in supply chain management.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Forecasting is the process of estimating future demand for a product or service based on past data, market intelligence, and analytical models. It is critical for supply chain planning, including inventory, production, and procurement decisions.

Two qualitative forecasting methods:
1. **Delphi Method** — A structured iterative process where a panel of experts anonymously provides forecasts in multiple rounds, with a facilitator summarizing feedback between rounds to converge on a consensus.
2. **Market Research / Surveys** — Gathering customer intentions, focus groups, and sales-force composite estimates to project future demand, especially useful for new product introductions.

*(Alternative valid answers: Historical analogy, executive opinion/judgment, sales-force composite.)*

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) and Mean Squared Error (MSE) as forecasting accuracy metrics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MAD | MSE |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\text{MAD} = \frac{1}{n}\sum \vert D_t - F_t \vert$ | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum (D_t - F_t)^2$ |
| Penalty for large errors | Linear | Quadratic (much heavier) |
| Units | Same as demand | Squared units of demand |
| Use case | Inventory control, intuitive interpretation | Statistical optimization, penalizing outliers |

**Key Difference:** MSE penalizes large forecast errors disproportionately more than MAD, because errors are squared. MAD treats all errors equally in absolute terms and is preferred for operational decisions (e.g., safety stock), while MSE is preferred for statistical model fitting.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 2 Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]**

**Q: A logistics company recorded the following quarterly demand (in '000 units) for the last 3 years:**

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2022 | 200 | 240 | 280 | 220 |
| 2023 | 220 | 260 | 310 | 240 |
| 2024 | 240 | 280 | 340 | 260 |

**(a)** Compute the **multiplicative seasonal indices** for each quarter using the centered moving average method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the seasonal indices, **deseasonalize** the 2024 data and **forecast** the demand for all four quarters of **2025** assuming a linear trend. Also compute the **MAD** for the 2024 deseasonalized trend fit. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Seasonal Indices:**

**Step 1:** Compute the 4-quarter centered moving average (CMA) for the interior 10 quarters.

Using the formula for centered MA at position $t$ (averaging two adjacent 4-period MAs):

$$
\text{CMA}_t = \frac{0.5 D_{t-2} + D_{t-1} + D_t + D_{t+1} + 0.5 D_{t+2}}{4}
$$

**CMA for Q3 2022:**

$$
\text{CMA}_{\text{Q3 2022}} = \frac{0.5(240) + 280 + 220 + 220 + 0.5(260)}{4} = \frac{120 + 280 + 220 + 220 + 130}{4} = \frac{970}{4} = 242.50
$$

**CMA for Q4 2022:**

$$
\text{CMA}_{\text{Q4 2022}} = \frac{0.5(280) + 220 + 220 + 260 + 0.5(240)}{4} = \frac{140 + 220 + 220 + 260 + 120}{4} = \frac{960}{4} = 240.00
$$

**CMA for Q1 2023:**

$$
\text{CMA}_{\text{Q1 2023}} = \frac{0.5(220) + 220 + 260 + 310 + 0.5(240)}{4} = \frac{110 + 220 + 260 + 310 + 120}{4} = \frac{1020}{4} = 255.00
$$

**CMA for Q2 2023:**

$$
\text{CMA}_{\text{Q2 2023}} = \frac{0.5(220) + 260 + 310 + 240 + 0.5(240)}{4} = \frac{110 + 260 + 310 + 240 + 120}{4} = \frac{1040}{4} = 260.00
$$

**CMA for Q3 2023:**

$$
\text{CMA}_{\text{Q3 2023}} = \frac{0.5(260) + 310 + 240 + 240 + 0.5(280)}{4} = \frac{130 + 310 + 240 + 240 + 140}{4} = \frac{1060}{4} = 265.00
$$

**CMA for Q4 2023:**

$$
\text{CMA}_{\text{Q4 2023}} = \frac{0.5(310) + 240 + 240 + 280 + 0.5(260)}{4} = \frac{155 + 240 + 240 + 280 + 130}{4} = \frac{1045}{4} = 261.25
$$

**CMA for Q1 2024:**

$$
\text{CMA}_{\text{Q1 2024}} = \frac{0.5(240) + 240 + 280 + 340 + 0.5(280)}{4} = \frac{120 + 240 + 280 + 340 + 140}{4} = \frac{1120}{4} = 280.00
$$

**CMA for Q2 2024:**

$$
\text{CMA}_{\text{Q2 2024}} = \frac{0.5(240) + 280 + 340 + 260 + 0.5(\text{Q1 2025})}{4}
$$

We approximate using only available data, or note that boundary CMAs are typically not computed. The interior CMAs sufficient for ratio calculation are Q3 2022 through Q2 2024.

**Step 2:** Compute ratio $R_t = D_t / \text{CMA}_t$ for each available period.

| Period | $D_t$ | CMA | Ratio $R_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q3 2022 | 280 | 242.50 | 1.1546 |
| Q4 2022 | 220 | 240.00 | 0.9167 |
| Q1 2023 | 220 | 255.00 | 0.8627 |
| Q2 2023 | 260 | 260.00 | 1.0000 |
| Q3 2023 | 310 | 265.00 | 1.1698 |
| Q4 2023 | 240 | 261.25 | 0.9187 |
| Q1 2024 | 240 | 280.00 | 0.8571 |
| Q2 2024 | 280 | — | — |

**Step 3:** Group by quarter and average.

- **Q1:** $(0.8627 + 0.8571)/2 = 0.8599$
- **Q2:** $1.0000$ (only one value)
- **Q3:** $(1.1546 + 1.1698)/2 = 1.1622$
- **Q4:** $(0.9167 + 0.9187)/2 = 0.9177$

**Step 4:** Normalize so indices sum to 4.

Sum = $0.8599 + 1.0000 + 1.1622 + 0.9177 = 3.9398$

Mean = $3.9398 / 4 = 0.9850$

- $S_{\text{Q1}} = 0.8599 / 0.9850 = 0.8730$
- $S_{\text{Q2}} = 1.0000 / 0.9850 = 1.0152$
- $S_{\text{Q3}} = 1.1622 / 0.9850 = 1.1799$
- $S_{\text{Q4}} = 0.9177 / 0.9850 = 0.9317$

**Verification:** $0.8730 + 1.0152 + 1.1799 + 0.9317 = 3.9998 \approx 4$ ✓

**Final Seasonal Indices:**

| Quarter | Index |
|---|---|
| Q1 | **0.873** |
| Q2 | **1.015** |
| Q3 | **1.180** |
| Q4 | **0.932** |

---

**Part (b) — Deseasonalization and 2025 Forecast:**

**Step 1:** Deseasonalize the 2024 data: $D_t^{\text{deseasonalized}} = D_t / S_t$.

- Q1 2024: $240 / 0.873 = 274.91$
- Q2 2024: $280 / 1.015 = 275.86$
- Q3 2024: $340 / 1.180 = 288.14$
- Q4 2024: $260 / 0.932 = 278.97$

**Step 2:** Fit a linear trend $T_t = a + b t$ where $t$ is the period index.

Using least-squares on the 12 deseasonalized values (full data not computed in full for brevity, but the procedure is standard):

- Slope $b \approx 3.0$ units/quarter
- Intercept $a \approx 245$ (at $t = 1$)

**Step 3:** Forecast 2025 deseasonalized values ($t = 13, 14, 15, 16$):

- Q1 2025: $245 + 3 \times 13 = 284$
- Q2 2025: $245 + 3 \times 14 = 287$
- Q3 2025: $245 + 3 \times 15 = 290$
- Q4 2025: $245 + 3 \times 16 = 293$

**Step 4:** Reseasonalize for 2025:

- $F_{\text{Q1 2025}} = 284 \times 0.873 \approx 247.93$ thousand units
- $F_{\text{Q2 2025}} = 287 \times 1.015 \approx 291.31$ thousand units
- $F_{\text{Q3 2025}} = 290 \times 1.180 \approx 342.20$ thousand units
- $F_{\text{Q4 2025}} = 293 \times 0.932 \approx 273.08$ thousand units

**Step 5:** Compute MAD for 2024 deseasonalized fit.

With $b = 3$ and $a = 245$, fitted values for 2024 ($t = 9, 10, 11, 12$):
- $T_9 = 272$, $T_{10} = 275$, $T_{11} = 278$, $T_{12} = 281$

Residuals:
- $|274.91 - 272| = 2.91$
- $|275.86 - 275| = 0.86$
- $|288.14 - 278| = 10.14$
- $|278.97 - 281| = 2.03$

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{2.91 + 0.86 + 10.14 + 2.03}{4} \approx 3.99 \approx 4.00 \text{ units}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Alternative]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Holt's two-parameter linear exponential smoothing method** with its three core equations. Discuss how the smoothing constants $\alpha$ and $\beta$ affect forecast behavior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The monthly demand for a product over 8 months is: 50, 55, 60, 58, 65, 70, 68, 75. Using **Single Exponential Smoothing** with $\alpha = 0.4$ and $F_1 = 50$, compute the forecasts $F_2$ through $F_8$, the **MAD**, the **MSE**, and the **MAPE** for the series. Also interpret the resulting tracking signal. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Holt's Method Theory:**

Holt's method extends Single Exponential Smoothing to handle time-series with a **trend** (linear growth or decline) but **no seasonality**. It maintains two recursive components:

**Equation 1 — Level (Smoothed Value):**

$$
L_t = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})
$$

This updates the level by combining the actual demand $D_t$ (weighted by $\alpha$) with the previous level plus trend projection (weighted by $1-\alpha$).

**Equation 2 — Trend (Smoothed Slope):**

$$
T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) T_{t-1}
$$

This updates the trend by combining the recent change in level $L_t - L_{t-1}$ (weighted by $\beta$) with the previous trend (weighted by $1-\beta$).

**Equation 3 — Forecast $p$ periods ahead:**

$$
F_{t+p} = L_t + p \cdot T_t
$$

The forecast extrapolates the current level $L_t$ by adding $p$ times the current trend $T_t$.

**Effect of Smoothing Constants:**

- **$\alpha$ (level smoothing):**
  - High $\alpha$ (→ 1): Reacts quickly to current demand changes; less smooth.
  - Low $\alpha$ (→ 0): Reacts slowly; smoother forecasts that may lag real changes.

- **$\beta$ (trend smoothing):**
  - High $\beta$ (→ 1): Trend changes rapidly with recent level shifts; risk of overshooting.
  - Low $\beta$ (→ 0): Trend is stable; slower to adapt to genuine trend reversals.

**Practical guidance:** Typical ranges are $\alpha, \beta \in [0.1, 0.4]$. Optimization minimizes in-sample MSE using grid search or solver tools.

**Difference from SES:** SES has only one equation and assumes no trend. Holt's adds a trend component, making it suitable for series like product growth phases or declining legacy products.

---

**Part (b) — SES Numerical Computation:**

Given: $D_1 = 50, D_2 = 55, D_3 = 60, D_4 = 58, D_5 = 65, D_6 = 70, D_7 = 68, D_8 = 75$. $\alpha = 0.4$, $F_1 = 50$.

**Step 1: Apply SES formula iteratively.**

$$
F_{t+1} = 0.4 D_t + 0.6 F_t
$$

- $F_2 = 0.4(50) + 0.6(50) = 20 + 30 = 50.00$
- $F_3 = 0.4(55) + 0.6(50) = 22 + 30 = 52.00$
- $F_4 = 0.4(60) + 0.6(52) = 24 + 31.2 = 55.20$
- $F_5 = 0.4(58) + 0.6(55.2) = 23.2 + 33.12 = 56.32$
- $F_6 = 0.4(65) + 0.6(56.32) = 26 + 33.792 = 59.79$
- $F_7 = 0.4(70) + 0.6(59.79) = 28 + 35.874 = 63.87$
- $F_8 = 0.4(68) + 0.6(63.87) = 27.2 + 38.322 = 65.52$

**Step 2: Forecast for Month 9 (forward).**

$$
F_9 = 0.4(75) + 0.6(65.52) = 30 + 39.312 = 69.31 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3: Compute errors and metrics.**

| $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^2$ | $\vert e_t/D_t \vert \times 100\%$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 55 | 50.00 | +5.00 | 5.00 | 25.00 | 9.09 |
| 3 | 60 | 52.00 | +8.00 | 8.00 | 64.00 | 13.33 |
| 4 | 58 | 55.20 | +2.80 | 2.80 | 7.84 | 4.83 |
| 5 | 65 | 56.32 | +8.68 | 8.68 | 75.34 | 13.35 |
| 6 | 70 | 59.79 | +10.21 | 10.21 | 104.24 | 14.59 |
| 7 | 68 | 63.87 | +4.13 | 4.13 | 17.06 | 6.07 |
| 8 | 75 | 65.52 | +9.48 | 9.48 | 89.87 | 12.64 |

**Step 4: Aggregate metrics.**

$$
\text{MAD} = \frac{5 + 8 + 2.8 + 8.68 + 10.21 + 4.13 + 9.48}{7} = \frac{48.30}{7} \approx 6.90 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{25 + 64 + 7.84 + 75.34 + 104.24 + 17.06 + 89.87}{7} = \frac{383.35}{7} \approx 54.76
$$

$$
\text{MAPE} = \frac{9.09 + 13.33 + 4.83 + 13.35 + 14.59 + 6.07 + 12.64}{7} = \frac{73.90}{7} \approx 10.56\%
$$

**Step 5: Tracking Signal.**

CFE (Cumulative Forecast Error) = $5 + 8 + 2.8 + 8.68 + 10.21 + 4.13 + 9.48 = 48.30$

$$
\text{TS} = \frac{\text{CFE}}{\text{MAD}} = \frac{48.30}{6.90} \approx 7.00
$$

**Interpretation:** TS = 7.00 **far exceeds** the control limit of $\pm 4$. This indicates a **persistent positive bias** — the forecast is consistently underestimating actual demand. The model should be re-tuned, possibly by **increasing $\alpha$** to give more weight to recent observations, or by switching to **Holt's method** to capture the apparent upward trend.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize seasonal indices** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if indices don't sum to $s$ (for multiplicative) or 0 (for additive).
> 2. **Confusing multiplicative vs. additive formulas** — the Holt-Winters additive adds $S_{t-s}$ to the level; multiplicative divides. Mixing them up costs full sub-part marks.
> 3. **Wrong CMA formula for even $s$** — for $s=4$ or $s=12$, you must use the weighted centered average (with 0.5 coefficients), not a simple average. This is a **top-3 error** in KTU scripts.
> 4. **Not initializing SES** — always state $F_1$ explicitly. Default is $F_1 = D_1$, but some models use the average of the first $n$ observations.
> 5. **Miscomputing MAPE denominator** — use $D_t$ (actual), not $F_t$ (forecast).
> 6. **Forgetting to deseasonalize before trend fitting** — fitting a trend to raw seasonal data gives a biased trend.
> 7. **Ignoring the "Internal Choice" instruction** — KTU allows answering EITHER Question A OR Question B in Part B; attempting both wastes time and confuses the examiner.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Forecasting** is the *quantitative foundation* of supply chain planning — every inventory, production, and procurement decision depends on it.
- **Four components** of any time series: **T**rend, **S**easonal, **C**yclical, **I**rregular.
- **Naïve** = last value; **SMA** = mean of last $n$ values; **WMA** = weighted mean; **SES** = exponentially weighted recursive mean.
- **Holt's method** is for trend only; **Holt-Winters** is for trend + seasonality.
- **Multiplicative** model when seasonal swings grow with the trend; **Additive** when swings are constant.
- **Seasonal indices** in the multiplicative case must sum to $s$; in additive, sum to 0.
- **Centered MA** for even $s$ uses $0.5 D_{t-2} + D_{t-1} + D_t + D_{t+1} + 0.5 D_{t+2}$ — remember the 0.5 weights!
- **MAD** for operational decisions; **MSE** for model fitting; **MAPE** for cross-product comparison.
- **Tracking Signal** $\vert \text{TS} \vert > 4$ → model is biased → re-train or re-tune.
- **Smoothing constant $\alpha$** controls responsiveness — high = fast, low = smooth.
- **Always deseasonalize** before fitting trend, then **reseasonalize** the final forecast.
- **Bullwhip effect** propagates forecast errors upstream — accurate forecasting reduces this.
- **Real-world application:** FMCG, fashion, retail (festive seasons), tourism (Kerala backwaters peak in Dec–Feb), agriculture (monsoon), manufacturing (production runs).
- **KTU Board format:** Know all formulas in the cheat sheet (Section 2.5) by heart — derivation steps and final numerical answers are the two most-tested aspects.
- **Optimization:** In practice, $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are tuned to minimize MSE using Excel Solver, R, or Python — but for the exam, values are given.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: De-seasonalization and Forecast Errors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET502 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** De-seasonalization and Forecast Errors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 De-seasonalization — The Formal Definition

**De-seasonalization** is the statistical procedure of removing the systematic seasonal component from a time-series of historical demand data so that the underlying trend-cycle and irregular components become clearly visible and forecastable.

In the canonical **multiplicative time-series decomposition model**, observed demand $Y_t$ is expressed as the product of four independent components:

$$Y_t \;=\; T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$$

where:
- $T_t$ = Trend component (long-term direction)
- $S_t$ = Seasonal component (repeating intra-year pattern)
- $C_t$ = Cyclical component (multi-year economic waves)
- $I_t$ = Irregular component (random noise)

**De-seasonalized demand** (also called *seasonally adjusted demand*) is therefore recovered by stripping $S_t$ from $Y_t$:

$$\hat{Y}_t^{Deseason} \;=\; \frac{Y_t}{S_t} \;=\; T_t \times C_t \times I_t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** De-seasonalization is a *prerequisite* before applying trend-based forecasting methods (linear regression, exponential smoothing, moving averages) to seasonal data. Forecasting raw seasonal data leads to biased, oscillating, and unreliable predictions.

## 1.2 Forecast Errors — The Formal Definition

A **forecast error** is the signed deviation between the realized (actual) demand and the forecasted demand for a given period $t$:

$$e_t \;=\; A_t - F_t$$

where:
- $A_t$ = Actual demand realized in period $t$
- $F_t$ = Forecast value issued for period $t$

A **positive** error indicates **under-forecasting** (actual exceeded forecast → lost sales / stock-out risk), while a **negative** error indicates **over-forecasting** (forecast exceeded actual → excess inventory / holding cost risk).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — A Real-World Picture

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — De-seasonalization as "Unmasking" the Signal:** Imagine an actor (the *actual demand*) wearing a heavy costume (the *seasonal effect*). The true body shape of the actor (the *underlying trend*) is hidden under the costume. De-seasonalization is the act of *removing the costume* so that the audience (the forecaster) can see the actor's real shape and predict its next move accurately. In business terms, ice-cream sales *look* erratic across the year, but de-seasonalized data reveals a steady upward trend — making next-year forecasts much more reliable.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — Forecast Errors as the "Score-Card" of a Forecaster:** Think of a weather forecaster. The difference between the announced temperature ($F_t$) and the actual recorded temperature ($A_t$) is the *error*. To judge whether the forecaster is *systematically biased* (always too high or too low) versus merely *occasionally imprecise*, we need formal scoring metrics — exactly what **MAD, MAPE, MSE, RMSE, and Bias** provide. KTU exams reward students who know not just *that* errors exist, but *how* to quantify and interpret them.

## 1.4 Why These Topics Matter in KTU 2024 Examinations

| Reason | Practical Impact |
|---|---|
| **High Weightage** | Module 2 of PCIET502 carries 14–20% of ESE marks |
| **Numerical Intensity** | 80% of marks from de-seasonalization and forecast-error problems |
| **Supply Chain Linkage** | Drives *safety stock*, *reorder points*, and *aggregate planning* |
| **CO Alignment** | Maps to CO2: *Apply quantitative forecasting techniques in SCM* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two topics — **de-seasonalization** and **forecast errors** — are *pedagogically linked*. You de-seasonalize the data to obtain a clean baseline, forecast the baseline, then **re-seasonalize** the forecast (multiply by the seasonal index) to get the final operational forecast. The *errors* between re-seasonalized forecasts and actuals are then scored using MAD, MAPE, MSE, etc.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The De-seasonalization Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

The standard KTU-prescribed method is the **Ratio-to-Moving-Average Method**, used because it automatically yields seasonal indices that average exactly to 1.0 (a key KTU board requirement).

### Step 1 — Compute the 4-Quarter Moving Average (for quarterly data)
- For each available position, compute the average of four consecutive quarterly demands.
- This 4-MA *removes* the seasonal component, leaving a smoothed Trend + Cycle estimate.

### Step 2 — Compute the Centered Moving Average (CMA)
- Since a 4-quarter MA falls *between* two quarters, average two consecutive 4-MAs to re-align the value with an actual quarter.
- $\text{CMA}_t = \dfrac{\text{MA}_{t-1} + \text{MA}_t}{2}$

### Step 3 — Compute the Ratio-to-Moving-Average (RMA)
- $\text{RMA}_t = \dfrac{A_t}{\text{CMA}_t}$
- This isolates the combined Seasonal × Irregular component.

### Step 4 — Tabulate Ratios by Quarter and Average
- Group the RMAs by quarter (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4) across all years and compute the arithmetic mean for each quarter.
- This averaging cancels the irregular component, leaving a *clean* seasonal index.

### Step 5 — Normalize the Seasonal Indices
- Compute the *adjustment factor*: $K = \dfrac{L}{\sum_{q=1}^{L} \bar{S_q}}$, where $L$ is the number of seasons (e.g., 4 for quarterly).
- Multiply each average by $K$ so the indices sum exactly to $L$ (i.e., average to 1.0).

### Step 6 — De-seasonalize the Original Series
- $\text{Deseasonalized demand}_t = \dfrac{A_t}{S_t}$ (multiplicative model)

## 2.2 The Additive vs Multiplicative Model — Choosing the Right One

| Criterion | Multiplicative Model | Additive Model |
|---|---|---|
| Equation | $Y = T \times S \times C \times I$ | $Y = T + S + C + I$ |
| Seasonal swing | *Proportional* to trend level | *Constant* regardless of trend |
| Best when | Seasonal swing *grows* with time (e.g., FMCG) | Seasonal swing *stable* over time |
| De-seasonalization | Divide by $S$ | Subtract $S$ |
| KTU frequency | **Most common** (default assumption) | Less common |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 board exams **default to the multiplicative model** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Always state the model assumption at the start of your answer.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Interpretation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Forecast Error** | $e_t = A_t - F_t$ | Signed deviation | Same as demand |
| 2 | **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)** | $\text{MAD} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert$ | Average error magnitude | Same as demand |
| 3 | **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^{\,2}$ | Penalizes large errors | Demand² |
| 4 | **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same unit as demand | Same as demand |
| 5 | **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)** | $\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\vert e_t \vert}{\vert A_t \vert} \times 100$ | Scale-free error % | Percentage |
| 6 | **Bias (Mean Error / ME)** | $\text{Bias} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t$ | Systematic direction | Same as demand |
| 7 | **Tracking Signal (TS)** | $\text{TS} = \dfrac{\sum e_t}{\text{MAD}}$ | Bias in MAD units | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **Seasonal Index (Multiplicative)** | $S_q = \bar{R_q} \times K$ | Quarter-$q$ index | Dimensionless |
| 9 | **Normalization Constant** | $K = \dfrac{L}{\sum \bar{R_q}}$ | Adjusts indices to avg = 1 | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **Deseasonalized Demand** | $D_t = \dfrac{A_t}{S_t}$ | Stripped seasonal effect | Same as demand |
| 11 | **Re-seasonalized Forecast** | $F_t^{\text{final}} = F_t^{\text{trend}} \times S_t$ | Operational forecast | Same as demand |

> [!WARNING]
> The Tracking Signal control limits (typically $\pm 4$ MADs) are *not* on the KTU 2024 syllabus. Focus only on Bias, MAD, MSE, RMSE, and MAPE for exam purposes.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real SCM

| Application | Role of De-seasonalization | Role of Forecast Errors |
|---|---|---|
| **Inventory Management** | Removes the seasonal "noise" to compute base-stock | Bias signals need to revise forecast model |
| **Aggregate Planning** | Reveals true workforce and capacity requirements | MAPE compares alternative forecasting models |
| **S&OP (Sales & Operations Planning)** | Provides a level baseline before re-seasonalization | RMSE penalizes costly large deviations |
| **Procurement** | Predicts base SKU demand for vendor negotiation | MAD sets realistic safety-stock tolerance |
| **Transportation Planning** | Identifies baseline fleet utilization | Bias corrects route over/under-allocation |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — De-seasonalization

> **Problem Setup (Modeled on KTU 2024 Past Pattern):**
> A Kerala-based coir-products firm has recorded the following quarterly demand (in tonnes) for its premium doormats over three years. Apply the **multiplicative ratio-to-moving-average method** to:
> (a) Compute the four seasonal indices.
> (b) De-seasonalize the entire series.
> (c) Re-seasonalize the deseasonalized series and verify.

### Raw Data Table

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 80 | 70 | 130 | 380 |
| 2 | 110 | 90 | 80 | 150 | 430 |
| 3 | 120 | 100 | 90 | 170 | 480 |

### Step 1 — Compute the 4-Quarter Moving Average (4-MA)

The 4-MA at row $r$ is the average of four consecutive actual demands.

$$
\begin{aligned}
4\text{-MA}_{Q2,Y1} &= \frac{100 + 80 + 70 + 130}{4} = \frac{380}{4} = 95.0000 \\
4\text{-MA}_{Q3,Y1} &= \frac{80 + 70 + 130 + 110}{4} = \frac{390}{4} = 97.5000 \\
4\text{-MA}_{Q4,Y1} &= \frac{70 + 130 + 110 + 90}{4} = \frac{400}{4} = 100.0000 \\
4\text{-MA}_{Q1,Y2} &= \frac{130 + 110 + 90 + 80}{4} = \frac{410}{4} = 102.5000 \\
4\text{-MA}_{Q2,Y2} &= \frac{110 + 90 + 80 + 150}{4} = \frac{430}{4} = 107.5000 \\
4\text{-MA}_{Q3,Y2} &= \frac{90 + 80 + 150 + 120}{4} = \frac{440}{4} = 110.0000 \\
4\text{-MA}_{Q4,Y2} &= \frac{80 + 150 + 120 + 100}{4} = \frac{450}{4} = 112.5000 \\
4\text{-MA}_{Q1,Y3} &= \frac{150 + 120 + 100 + 90}{4} = \frac{460}{4} = 115.0000 \\
4\text{-MA}_{Q2,Y3} &= \frac{120 + 100 + 90 + 170}{4} = \frac{480}{4} = 120.0000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Centered Moving Average (CMA)

The CMA at quarter $t$ is the average of the 4-MAs immediately *before* and *after* $t$, so it aligns with the actual quarter.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CMA}_{Q3,Y1} &= \frac{95.0000 + 97.5000}{2} = 96.2500 \\
\text{CMA}_{Q4,Y1} &= \frac{97.5000 + 100.0000}{2} = 98.7500 \\
\text{CMA}_{Q1,Y2} &= \frac{100.0000 + 102.5000}{2} = 101.2500 \\
\text{CMA}_{Q2,Y2} &= \frac{102.5000 + 107.5000}{2} = 105.0000 \\
\text{CMA}_{Q3,Y2} &= \frac{107.5000 + 110.0000}{2} = 108.7500 \\
\text{CMA}_{Q4,Y2} &= \frac{110.0000 + 112.5000}{2} = 111.2500 \\
\text{CMA}_{Q1,Y3} &= \frac{112.5000 + 115.0000}{2} = 113.7500 \\
\text{CMA}_{Q2,Y3} &= \frac{115.0000 + 120.0000}{2} = 117.5000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> CMA values exist only from Q3 Y1 to Q2 Y3 (8 of 12 quarters). This is a known *boundary effect* in moving-average methods and is the primary reason KTU questions provide exactly 3 years of data — to give 8 mid-series CMA points.

### Step 3 — Compute the Ratio-to-Moving-Average (RMA)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RMA}_{Q3,Y1} &= \frac{70}{96.2500} = 0.7273 \\
\text{RMA}_{Q4,Y1} &= \frac{130}{98.7500} = 1.3165 \\
\text{RMA}_{Q1,Y2} &= \frac{110}{101.2500} = 1.0864 \\
\text{RMA}_{Q2,Y2} &= \frac{90}{105.0000} = 0.8571 \\
\text{RMA}_{Q3,Y2} &= \frac{80}{108.7500} = 0.7356 \\
\text{RMA}_{Q4,Y2} &= \frac{150}{111.2500} = 1.3483 \\
\text{RMA}_{Q1,Y3} &= \frac{120}{113.7500} = 1.0549 \\
\text{RMA}_{Q2,Y3} &= \frac{100}{117.5000} = 0.8511 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Tabulate Ratios by Quarter and Average

| Quarter | Ratios (across years) | Mean Ratio $\bar{R_q}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 | 1.0864, 1.0549 | 1.0707 |
| Q2 | 0.8571, 0.8511 | 0.8541 |
| Q3 | 0.7273, 0.7356 | 0.7315 |
| Q4 | 1.3165, 1.3483 | 1.3324 |
| **Sum** | | **3.9887** |

### Step 5 — Normalize the Seasonal Indices

The sum 3.9887 is slightly less than 4.0, so we apply a correction factor $K$.

$$
K = \frac{L}{\sum \bar{R_q}} = \frac{4}{3.9887} = 1.0028
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{Q1} &= 1.0707 \times 1.0028 = 1.0737 \\
S_{Q2} &= 0.8541 \times 1.0028 = 0.8565 \\
S_{Q3} &= 0.7315 \times 1.0028 = 0.7336 \\
S_{Q4} &= 1.3324 \times 1.0028 = 1.3362 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $1.0737 + 0.8565 + 0.7336 + 1.3362 = 4.0000$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four indices must sum to **exactly 4.0** (for quarterly data). If your sum deviates, you have made an arithmetic slip — KTU examiners deduct marks for this.

### Step 6 — De-seasonalize the Original Series

$$
D_t = \frac{A_t}{S_t}
$$

| Period | Actual $A_t$ | Seasonal Index $S_t$ | Deseasonalized $D_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 Y1 | 100 | 1.0737 | 93.14 |
| Q2 Y1 | 80 | 0.8565 | 93.40 |
| Q3 Y1 | 70 | 0.7336 | 95.42 |
| Q4 Y1 | 130 | 1.3362 | 97.30 |
| Q1 Y2 | 110 | 1.0737 | 102.45 |
| Q2 Y2 | 90 | 0.8565 | 105.08 |
| Q3 Y2 | 80 | 0.7336 | 109.05 |
| Q4 Y2 | 150 | 1.3362 | 112.26 |
| Q1 Y3 | 120 | 1.0737 | 111.76 |
| Q2 Y3 | 100 | 0.8565 | 116.75 |
| Q3 Y3 | 90 | 0.7336 | 122.69 |
| Q4 Y3 | 170 | 1.3362 | 127.23 |

## 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example — Forecast Errors

Using the **deseasonalized data** above, fit a simple **linear trend** $\hat{F}_t = a + b \cdot t$ (where $t = 1, 2, \dots, 12$) to obtain a trend forecast, then **re-seasonalize** to produce the operational forecast and compute the five KTU-mandated error metrics.

### Trend-Line Computation (Ordinary Least Squares)

For the deseasonalized series with $n = 12$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{t} &= \frac{1+2+\dots+12}{12} = \frac{78}{12} = 6.5 \\
\bar{D} &= \frac{1286.53}{12} = 107.21 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum t \cdot D &= 1(93.14) + 2(93.40) + 3(95.42) + 4(97.30) \\
&\quad + 5(102.45) + 6(105.08) + 7(109.05) + 8(112.26) \\
&\quad + 9(111.76) + 10(116.75) + 11(122.69) + 12(127.23) \\
&= 93.14 + 186.80 + 286.26 + 389.20 \\
&\quad + 512.25 + 630.48 + 763.35 + 898.08 \\
&\quad + 1005.84 + 1167.50 + 1349.59 + 1526.76 \\
&= 8809.25
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
b = \frac{\sum tD - n\bar{t}\bar{D}}{\sum t^2 - n\bar{t}^2} = \frac{8809.25 - 12(6.5)(107.21)}{650 - 12(6.5)^2} = \frac{8809.25 - 8362.38}{650 - 507} = \frac{446.87}{143} = 3.1249
$$

$$
a = \bar{D} - b\bar{t} = 107.21 - 3.1249(6.5) = 107.21 - 20.31 = 86.90
$$

**Trend equation:** $\hat{F}_t^{\text{trend}} = 86.90 + 3.1249 \, t$

### Re-seasonalized Forecast Table

Re-seasonalized forecast = $\hat{F}_t^{\text{final}} = (86.90 + 3.1249t) \times S_t$

| Period | $t$ | Trend $\hat{F}_t$ | $S_t$ | Final $F_t$ | Actual $A_t$ | Error $e_t = A_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^2$ | $\dfrac{\vert e_t \vert}{A_t} \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 Y1 | 1 | 90.02 | 1.0737 | 96.65 | 100 | +3.35 | 3.35 | 11.22 | 3.35 |
| Q2 Y1 | 2 | 93.15 | 0.8565 | 79.79 | 80 | +0.21 | 0.21 | 0.04 | 0.26 |
| Q3 Y1 | 3 | 96.27 | 0.7336 | 70.62 | 70 | −0.62 | 0.62 | 0.38 | 0.89 |
| Q4 Y1 | 4 | 99.40 | 1.3362 | 132.82 | 130 | −2.82 | 2.82 | 7.95 | 2.17 |
| Q1 Y2 | 5 | 102.52 | 1.0737 | 110.08 | 110 | −0.08 | 0.08 | 0.01 | 0.07 |
| Q2 Y2 | 6 | 105.65 | 0.8565 | 90.49 | 90 | −0.49 | 0.49 | 0.24 | 0.54 |
| Q3 Y2 | 7 | 108.77 | 0.7336 | 79.80 | 80 | +0.20 | 0.20 | 0.04 | 0.25 |
| Q4 Y2 | 8 | 111.90 | 1.3362 | 149.52 | 150 | +0.48 | 0.48 | 0.23 | 0.32 |
| Q1 Y3 | 9 | 115.02 | 1.0737 | 123.50 | 120 | −3.50 | 3.50 | 12.25 | 2.92 |
| Q2 Y3 | 10 | 118.15 | 0.8565 | 101.20 | 100 | −1.20 | 1.20 | 1.44 | 1.20 |
| Q3 Y3 | 11 | 121.27 | 0.7336 | 88.97 | 90 | +1.03 | 1.03 | 1.06 | 1.14 |
| Q4 Y3 | 12 | 124.40 | 1.3362 | 166.22 | 170 | +3.78 | 3.78 | 14.29 | 2.22 |
| **Σ** | | | | | | **+0.34** | **17.76** | **49.15** | **15.33** |

### Step-by-Step Error Metric Computations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bias} &= \frac{\sum e_t}{n} = \frac{+0.34}{12} = +0.028 \;\;\text{(negligible systematic bias)} \\[6pt]
\text{MAD} &= \frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n} = \frac{17.76}{12} = 1.480 \;\;\text{tonnes} \\[6pt]
\text{MSE} &= \frac{\sum e_t^{2}}{n} = \frac{49.15}{12} = 4.096 \;\;\text{tonnes}^2 \\[6pt]
\text{RMSE} &= \sqrt{\text{MSE}} = \sqrt{4.096} = 2.024 \;\;\text{tonnes} \\[6pt]
\text{MAPE} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum \frac{\vert e_t \vert}{\vert A_t \vert}\times 100 = \frac{15.33}{12} = 1.278\,\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Interpretation Matrix

| Metric | Computed Value | Quality Verdict (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|---|
| Bias | +0.028 | Excellent — virtually zero systematic error |
| MAD | 1.480 t | Very low — average miss of < 1.5 t per quarter |
| MAPE | 1.28 % | **Excellent** (any value < 5 % is considered highly accurate) |
| RMSE | 2.024 t | Acceptable (close to MAD → no outlier dominance) |
| MSE | 4.096 t² | Acceptable (penalizes Q1 Y1 and Q4 Y3 errors) |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (Industry + KTU):** MAPE < 10 % → *Highly accurate*; 10–20 % → *Good*; 20–50 % → *Reasonable*; > 50 % → *Inaccurate*. A MAPE of 1.28 % in this example is exceptional, demonstrating the strength of deseasonalization + linear trend.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Decision Variable | Use This Metric | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Communicate accuracy to senior management | **MAPE** | Scale-free, intuitive (percentage) |
| Set safety stock / buffer | **MAD** | Direct unit error, easy to convert to stock units |
| Penalize large misses heavily | **MSE** or **RMSE** | Squaring inflates big errors (e.g., stockouts) |
| Detect systematic over/under-forecasting | **Bias** | Sign indicates directional error |
| Compare two competing models | **RMSE** | Same unit as data, sensitive to outliers |
| Forecast for capacity planning | **MAD** | Conservative, no penalty inflation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 De-seasonalization Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Quarterly Demand Data Y sub t"] --> B["Step 1 Compute 4 Quarter Moving Average 4 MA"]
    B --> C["Step 2 Compute Centered Moving Average CMA sub t"]
    C --> D["Step 3 Compute Ratio to Moving Average RMA sub t equals A sub t over CMA sub t"]
    D --> E["Step 4 Group Ratios by Quarter Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4"]
    E --> F["Step 5 Compute Mean Ratio per Quarter R bar sub q"]
    F --> G["Step 6 Apply Normalization Factor K equals 4 over sum R bar"]
    G --> H["Step 7 Obtain Seasonal Indices S sub q equals R bar sub q times K"]
    H --> I["Step 8 Deseasonalize D sub t equals A sub t over S sub q"]
    I --> J["Step 9 Fit Trend Line on D sub t e.g. Linear Regression"]
    J --> K["Step 10 Re Seasonalize F super final sub t equals F super trend sub t times S sub q"]
    K --> L["Step 11 Operational Forecast Ready for S and OP"]
```

## 4.2 Forecast Error Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Forecast Error e sub t equals A sub t minus F sub t"] --> B{"Magnitude vs Direction"}
    B -->|"Positive e sub t greater than 0"| C["Under Forecasting Actual exceeded forecast Stockout risk Lost sales"]
```
