**Problem (KTU Style):** A retailer sells **10,000 units/year** of a product. Each unit costs **₹50**. Ordering cost is **₹200/order**. Holding cost rate is **20 %/year**. Find EOQ, # orders/year, cycle time, and minimum total cost.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute H.**
$H = h \cdot P = 0.20 \times 50 = ₹10/\text{unit·yr}$

**Step 2 — Apply EOQ formula.**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{10}} = \sqrt{400{,}000} = 632.46 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of orders per year.**

$$
N^{*} = \frac{10{,}000}{632.46} = 15.81 \approx 16 \text{ orders/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Cycle time.**

$$
T^{*} = \frac{1}{N^{*}} = \frac{1}{15.81} = 0.0633 \text{ yr} \approx 23.1 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum relevant cost.**

$$
\text{TRC}_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200 \times 10} = \sqrt{40{,}000{,}000} = ₹6{,}324.56/\text{yr}
$$

**Step 6 — Total annual cost including material.**

$$
\text{TAC} = 6{,}324.56 + (50 \times 10{,}000) = ₹5{,}06{,}324.56/\text{yr}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — EPQ

**Problem:** A factory produces a part at **p = 50,000 units/yr**, demand is **d = 10,000 units/yr**, setup cost **S = ₹3,000/order**, holding cost **H = ₹5/unit·yr**. Find EPQ and compare with EOQ.

**Solution:**

$$
Q^{*}_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 3{,}000}{5}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{50{,}000}{50{,}000 - 10{,}000}} = \sqrt{12{,}000{,}000} \cdot \sqrt{1.25} = 3464.1 \times 1.1180 = 3873.0 \text{ units}
$$

For comparison, the simple EOQ (p → ∞) would have been:
$Q^{*}_{EOQ} = \sqrt{12{,}000{,}000} = 3464.1 \text{ units}$

The EPQ is **11.8 % larger** because production cannot keep up with demand instantaneously.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — All-Units Quantity Discount

**Problem:** A wholesaler faces the following price schedule:

| Tier | Quantity Range | Unit Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 – 999 | 10.00 |
| 2 | 1000 – 1999 | 9.80 |
| 3 | 2000 and above | 9.60 |

Annual demand **D = 5,000 units**, ordering cost **S = ₹100/order**, holding rate **h = 25 %/yr**.

**Step 1 — Compute H for each tier.**
- H₁ = 0.25 × 10.00 = ₹2.50
- H₂ = 0.25 × 9.80 = ₹2.45
- H₃ = 0.25 × 9.60 = ₹2.40

**Step 2 — Compute candidate EOQ starting from lowest price tier.**

$$
Q_{3}^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 100}{2.40}} = \sqrt{416{,}666.67} = 645.5 \text{ units}
$$

**645.5 is NOT in Tier 3 (needs ≥ 2000) → infeasible.** Set Q = 2000 (breakpoint of Tier 3).

$$
\text{TAC}(Q=2000, P=9.60) = \frac{5000}{2000}(100) + \frac{2000}{2}(2.40) + 9.60(5000) = 250 + 2400 + 48{,}000 = ₹50{,}650
$$

**Step 3 — Move to Tier 2.**

$$
Q_{2}^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 100}{2.45}} = 638.9 \text{ units}
$$

**638.9 is NOT in Tier 2 (needs ≥ 1000) → infeasible.** Set Q = 1000.

$$
\text{TAC}(Q=1000, P=9.80) = \frac{5000}{1000}(100) + \frac{1000}{2}(2.45) + 9.80(5000) = 500 + 1225 + 49{,}000 = ₹50{,}725
$$

**Step 4 — Tier 1.**

$$
Q_{1}^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 100}{2.50}} = 632.5 \text{ units}
$$

**632.5 IS in Tier 1 (≤ 999) → feasible.**

$$
\text{TAC}(Q=632.5, P=10.00) = \frac{5000}{632.5}(100) + \frac{632.5}{2}(2.50) + 10(5000) = 790.6 + 790.6 + 50{,}000 = ₹51{,}581
$$

**Step 5 — Compare.**

| Tier | Q | TAC (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 632.5 | 51,581 |
| 2 | 1000 | 50,725 |
| **3** | **2000** | **50,650** ← Minimum |

**Optimal order: Q\* = 2000 units at ₹9.60/unit.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Although EOQ is *mathematically* minimum-cost, the *all-units discount* often makes a larger order (at a breakpoint) the global optimum. Always compare across tiers — never stop at the lowest-tier EOQ.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inventory Behaviour Over Time — Basic EOQ

The diagram below captures the **saw-tooth inventory profile** under instantaneous replenishment.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Start of Cycle - Inventory Q] --> B[Steady Consumption - Demand d per day]
    B --> C[End of Cycle - Inventory 0]
    C --> D[Instantaneous Reorder - Q units arrive]
    D --> A
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Inventory starts at **Q**, depletes linearly under constant demand **d** over cycle time **T = Q/d**, drops to **0**, and an instantaneous replenishment of Q units restores the level — and the cycle repeats.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Cycle Inventory Selection

This block-level functional architecture shows how a manager chooses the appropriate cycle inventory model based on operational context.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Planning Problem: How much to order?]) --> Q1{Is production in-house or external supplier?}
    Q1 -- External / Instantaneous --> A1[Apply Basic EOQ - Wilson Model]
    Q1 -- Internal / Gradual Production --> A2[Apply EPQ / Production Lot Size]
    A1 --> Q2{Does supplier offer price breaks?}
    A2 --> Q3{Does supplier offer price breaks?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> B1[Apply All Units or Incremental Discount Model]
    Q2 -- No --> B2[Use Q* from EOQ directly]
    Q3 -- Yes --> B2b[Apply All Units or Incremental Discount with EPQ]
    Q3 -- No --> B2c[Use Q* from EPQ directly]
    B1 --> End([Optimal Order Quantity Q*])
    B2 --> End
    B2b --> End
    B2c --> End
    style Start fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style End fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.3 Cost Curve Behaviour — Total Cost vs. Order Quantity

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Order Quantity Q on x axis] --> B[Total Cost on y axis]
    B --> C[Ordering Cost - Hyperbola DS over Q - Decreases]
    B --> D[Holding Cost - Straight line QH over 2 - Increases]
    B --> E[Total Relevant Cost - U shaped Convex]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Optimum Q* at Minimum Point]
    E --> G[Intersection Property - Ordering Cost = Holding Cost at Q*]
    style F fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Insight:** The convex U-shape is the *visual signature* of any well-posed EOQ problem. The minimum lies exactly where the ordering-cost hyperbola and the holding-cost straight line intersect.

---

## 4.4 EPQ Inventory Profile — Trapezoidal Build-up

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Start Production - Inventory 0] --> P1[Inventory Rises at Rate p minus d for Q over p Time]
    P1 --> P2[Production Stops - Max Inventory I max = Q times 1 minus d over p]
    P2 --> P3[Inventory Falls at Rate d until Cycle Ends]
    P3 --> P4[New Production Run Begins]
    P4 --> P1
    style P2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

This trapezoidal cycle is the geometric signature of EPQ, in contrast to the triangular saw-tooth of basic EOQ.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define **cycle inventory**. Why does a firm choose to carry cycle inventory even though holding inventory is costly?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Cycle inventory** is the average inventory a firm holds as a result of producing or ordering in lots larger than the immediate requirement; mathematically, average cycle inventory = **Q/2**, where **Q** is the order quantity. Firms deliberately carry cycle inventory to **exploit economies of scale** — larger orders reduce the per-unit ordering (or setup) and transportation cost, even though they raise the per-unit holding cost. The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) determines the lot size that **balances these two opposing cost components** and minimises the total relevant cost. *(3 marks — full marks for clear definition + cost trade-off explanation.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the **three core cost components** considered in the classical EOQ model and write the total relevant cost function.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three core cost components are:
1. **Annual Material (Purchase) Cost = P·D** — independent of Q, often excluded from optimisation.
2. **Annual Ordering (Setup) Cost = (D/Q) · S** — decreases as Q increases.
3. **Annual Holding (Carrying) Cost = (Q/2) · H** — increases as Q increases.

The **Total Relevant Cost function** is:

$$
\text{TRC}(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}
$$

*(1 mark for each component, 1 mark for the formula. Use H = h·P explicitly if a discount question follows.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 carries 14 marks. You must attempt **one** of the two alternatives below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *EOQ + Quantity Discount Application*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> A company uses **24,000 units/year** of a raw material. The unit price is normally **₹20**, ordering cost is **₹150 per order**, and the holding cost is **25 % of unit price per year**.
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Determine the **EOQ**, the number of orders per year, the cycle time, and the **minimum total relevant cost**.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] The supplier now offers an **all-units quantity discount** of ₹19 per unit for orders of 3,000 or more. Should the company accept the discount offer? Justify with a cost comparison.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Holding cost H.**
$H = h \cdot P = 0.25 \times 20 = ₹5/\text{unit·yr}$

**Step 2 — EOQ.**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150}{5}} = \sqrt{1{,}440{,}000} = 1200 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of orders per year.**

$$
N^{*} = \frac{24{,}000}{1200} = 20 \text{ orders/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Cycle time.**

$$
T^{*} = \frac{1}{N^{*}} = 0.05 \text{ yr} = 18.25 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum total relevant cost.**

$$
\text{TRC}_{\min} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150 \times 5} = \sqrt{36{,}000{,}000} = ₹6{,}000/\text{yr}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification via intersection property.**
At Q = 1200: Ordering cost = (24,000/1200)·150 = ₹3,000 ✓ Holding cost = (1200/2)·5 = ₹3,000 ✓
Both equal — confirms optimum. *(1 mark for verification step.)*

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Recompute H at discounted price.**
$H_{\text{new}} = 0.25 \times 19 = ₹4.75/\text{unit·yr}$

**Step 2 — Candidate EOQ at discount.**

$$
Q^{*}_{\text{new}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150}{4.75}} = \sqrt{1{,}515{,}789.5} = 1231.3 \text{ units}
$$

**1231.3 < 3000** → not eligible for discount. Adjust Q to breakpoint: **Q = 3000**.

**Step 3 — Total Annual Cost at the discount.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TAC}(Q=3000) &= \frac{24{,}000}{3000}(150) + \frac{3000}{2}(4.75) + 19(24{,}000) \\
&= 1200 + 7125 + 456{,}000 = ₹464{,}325
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — TAC without discount at EOQ = 1200.**

$$
\text{TAC}(Q=1200) = \frac{24{,}000}{1200}(150) + \frac{1200}{2}(5) + 20(24{,}000) = 3000 + 3000 + 480{,}000 = ₹486{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Decision.**

| Policy | Q | TAC (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| No discount | 1200 | 486,000 |
| **Discount accepted** | **3000** | **464,325** ← Minimum |

**Savings = ₹21,675/year.** The company **should accept** the discount offer and place orders of 3,000 units.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *EPQ / Production Lot-Size Application*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
> A manufacturing firm produces a component at the rate of **40,000 units/year**. Annual demand is steady at **8,000 units/year**. Setup cost per production run is **₹2,500**, and the holding cost is **₹8 per unit per year**.
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Compute the **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)**, the maximum inventory, the production run time, and the **minimum total relevant cost**.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Compare the EPQ result with the basic EOQ. Explain in words why the EPQ is larger (or smaller) than the EOQ, and the operational significance of this difference.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Step 1 — Substitute into EPQ formula.**

$$
Q^{*}_{EPQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{p-d}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000 \times 2500}{8}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{40{,}000}{40{,}000 - 8000}}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate.**

$$
= \sqrt{5{,}000{,}000} \cdot \sqrt{1.25} = 2236.07 \times 1.1180 = 2500 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum inventory.**

$$
I_{\max} = 2500 \left(1 - \frac{8000}{40{,}000}\right) = 2500 \times 0.8 = 2000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4 — Production run time.**

$$
t_{p} = \frac{Q}{p} = \frac{2500}{40{,}000} = 0.0625 \text{ yr} \approx 22.8 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum total relevant cost.**

$$
\text{TRC}_{\min,EPQ} = \sqrt{2DSH\left(1 - \frac{d}{p}\right)} = \sqrt{2 \times 8000 \times 2500 \times 8 \times 0.8} = \sqrt{256{,}000{,}000} = ₹16{,}000/\text{yr}
$$

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute basic EOQ (p → ∞).**

$$
Q^{*}_{EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000 \times 2500}{8}} = \sqrt{5{,}000{,}000} = 2236.07 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare.**

| Metric | EOQ (p → ∞) | EPQ (p = 40,000) | % Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Optimal lot size | 2236 | 2500 | +11.8 % |
| Max inventory | 1118 | 2000 | +78.9 % |
| Min TRC (₹) | 20,000 | 16,000 | −20 % |

**Step 3 — Explanation.**
The EPQ is **larger than the basic EOQ** by a factor of $\sqrt{p/(p-d)} = \sqrt{1.25} = 1.118$. Operationally, this is because the basic EOQ assumes *instantaneous* replenishment, which would require a production rate approaching infinity. In reality, production is **gradual** — at rate p = 40,000 against demand d = 8,000. Because production cannot outpace demand by an infinite amount, the firm must produce a **larger batch** to make each setup worthwhile. At the same time, the **maximum inventory** is *less* than Q (it is 0.8·Q here) because inventory is *depleted simultaneously* with production. This simultaneous depletion **reduces the average holding cost** by the factor $(1 - d/p)$, and the **total minimum cost** is correspondingly lower than the basic EOQ minimum cost. *(Full 7 marks requires numerical comparison + conceptual interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — most common 2-mark loss; always state units before plugging into a formula.
> 2. **Forgetting material cost (PD)** in quantity-discount problems — TAC is mandatory, not TRC alone.
> 3. **Wrong "feasibility check"** in discounts — a candidate EOQ *outside* its price tier is **infeasible**; use the tier's *breakpoint* instead.
> 4. **Confusing EPQ average inventory with EOQ** — EPQ average = (Q/2)(1 − d/p), not Q/2.
> 5. **Skipping the second-derivative test** — KTU examiners *do* award a mark for verifying the minimum.
> 6. **Rounding Q to an integer too early** — keep one decimal until the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Application of Cycle Inventory Models**

- **Cycle inventory** = Q/2 on average; exists because firms exploit **economies of scale** in ordering/production.
- **Basic EOQ** formula (Wilson): $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$. Valid only under deterministic, constant, instantaneous-replenishment assumptions.
- **Holding cost** must be in the *same time unit* as demand — convert D, S, or H to a common basis first.
- The **intersection property** at the optimum: annual ordering cost = annual holding cost = $\sqrt{DSH/2}$.
- The minimum total relevant cost is $\sqrt{2DSH}$; total annual cost adds the material cost **P·D**.
- The **5.96 % rule**: doubling the EOQ raises total cost by ~6 % — a classic conceptual question.
- **EPQ / Production Lot Size**: $Q^{*}_{EPQ} = \sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot \sqrt{p/(p-d)}$; max inventory is $Q(1 - d/p)$.
- The **trapezoidal inventory profile** is the geometric fingerprint of EPQ; the **saw-tooth triangle** is the fingerprint of basic EOQ.
- **All-units quantity discount**: always start from the lowest-price tier, check feasibility, fall back to the breakpoint if infeasible, then **compare TACs across all tiers**.
- **Incremental quantity discount**: compute TC *within each tier's* Q range using the tier's average unit price.
- **Reorder Point (ROP)** for cycle stock = d × L; independent of Q.
- The **power-of-two rule** ($Q = 2^{k} \cdot d$) is a practical MRP rounding heuristic — KTU accepts it as a valid "engineering simplification."
- **Multiechelon extension**: when multiple items share a supplier, joint replenishment uses the **least common multiple** of individual EOQ cycle times to coordinate orders.
- **Sanity check** for any EOQ: the answer must lie *between* the lowest and highest price-break quantities; if not, the breakpoints are the only relevant candidates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discounting Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET502 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Discounting Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DISCOUNTING MODELS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Discounting Models** are quantitative financial decision-making tools used in Supply Chain and Logistics Management that convert future cash flows (inflows and outflows) into their equivalent **present-day monetary values** using a specified **discount rate (cost of capital / required rate of return)**. These models help managers decide whether an investment, project, or procurement strategy is financially viable when benefits accrue over multiple time periods.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, discounting models are the mathematical backbone for evaluating:
- Capital investment in warehouses, fleet, and automation
- **Quantity discount** and **cash discount** decisions in procurement
- Multi-period inventory and supplier selection problems
- Make-vs-buy, lease-vs-buy, and outsourcing decisions

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Intuition:** Imagine your grandfather offers you two choices today:  
> (a) Receive **₹1,00,000 right now**, OR  
> (b) Receive **₹1,21,000 after 2 years**.  
>  
> Naively, ₹1,21,000 looks bigger. But money today is more valuable than money tomorrow because you can invest today's money (e.g., in a fixed deposit at 10% per year) and grow it. If you invest ₹1,00,000 today at 10% compounded, it becomes ₹1,21,000 after 2 years — so both options are equal at a 10% discount rate.  
>  
> **Discounting** is the reverse process: it shrinks future money back to "today's rupees" so we can compare options on a common scale.

The mathematical act of shrinking future money is called **discounting**, and the percentage factor used is the **discount rate (r)**.

### 1.3 Why Discounting Models Matter in SCM

| SCM Decision | Why Discounting is Needed |
|---|---|
| Buying in bulk (quantity discounts) | Future holding costs vs. immediate purchase savings |
| Investing in a new warehouse | Multi-year cash flow spread over decades |
| Leasing vs. buying a truck | Stream of payments at different points in time |
| Just-in-time vs. safety stock | Holding cost savings accrue over many cycles |
| Supplier selection with credit terms | Cash discount now vs. paying later |

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Must Remember)

- **Time Value of Money (TVM):** A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow.
- **Discount Rate (r or i):** The opportunity cost of capital, expressed as a decimal (e.g., 10% = 0.10).
- **Cash Flow (CFₜ):** Net money received (positive) or paid (negative) at the end of period *t*.
- **Present Value (PV):** Today's worth of a future cash flow.
- **Future Value (FV):** Worth of a present cash flow at a future date.
- **Net Present Value (NPV):** Sum of PVs of all cash flows minus the initial investment.
- **Internal Rate of Return (IRR):** The discount rate at which NPV = 0.
- **Discounted Payback Period:** Time required to recover the initial investment using *discounted* cash flows.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Building Blocks

### 2.1.1 Single Cash Flow — Future Value (FV)
A sum **PV** invested today at compound interest rate **r** for **n** periods grows to:

$$FV = PV \times (1 + r)^n$$

The factor $(1+r)^n$ is the **Future Value Interest Factor (FVIF)**.

### 2.1.2 Single Cash Flow — Present Value (PV)
Rearranging the above, the present worth of a future sum **FV** received after **n** periods is:

$$PV = \frac{FV}{(1 + r)^n}$$

The factor $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^n}$ is the **Present Value Interest Factor (PVIF)**.

### 2.1.3 Uniform Series — Annuity
An **annuity** is a stream of equal cash flows *A* at the end of each period for *n* periods.

**Present Value of an Ordinary Annuity:**

$$PV_{annuity} = A \times \left[ \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r} \right]$$

The bracketed term is the **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)**.

**Future Value of an Ordinary Annuity:**

$$FV_{annuity} = A \times \left[ \frac{(1 + r)^n - 1}{r} \right]$$

### 2.1.4 Perpetuity
A perpetual stream of *A* every period (n → ∞):

$$PV_{perpetuity} = \frac{A}{r}$$

## 2.2 Net Present Value (NPV) — The King of Discounting Models

### 2.2.1 Definition
NPV is the algebraic sum of the present values of **all** cash inflows and outflows of a project, discounted at the firm's cost of capital.

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}$$

where:
- $CF_0$ = Initial investment (typically negative)
- $CF_t$ = Net cash flow in period *t* (for *t* = 1, 2, …, n)
- *r* = Discount rate (cost of capital)
- *n* = Project life in periods

### 2.2.2 Decision Rule

| NPV Value | Decision |
|---|---|
| **NPV > 0** | **Accept** the project — it adds value |
| **NPV = 0** | Indifferent — project meets required return exactly |
| **NPV < 0** | **Reject** — project destroys value |

> [!TIP]  
> KTU examiners often award full marks in 14-mark questions when the student states the decision rule explicitly along with the numerical computation.

### 2.2.3 Why NPV is the Preferred Model
- Accounts for the **time value of money**.
- Considers **all** cash flows over the entire project life.
- Uses the actual cost of capital as a benchmark.
- **Additivity rule**: NPV of (A + B) = NPV(A) + NPV(B), allowing clean comparison of mutually exclusive alternatives.

## 2.3 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

### 2.3.1 Definition
IRR is the discount rate at which the Net Present Value of a project becomes **zero**.

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + IRR)^t} = 0$$

### 2.3.2 Decision Rule
- **Accept** if **IRR > Cost of Capital (r)**
- **Reject** if **IRR < Cost of Capital (r)**

### 2.3.3 Calculation Method (when not directly solvable)
Use **interpolation** between two rates $r_1$ and $r_2$ where the NPV changes sign:

$$IRR = r_1 + \frac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2} \times (r_2 - r_1)$$

## 2.4 Profitability Index (PI)

$$PI = \frac{PV \ of \ Future \ Cash \ Flows}{Initial \ Investment} = 1 + \frac{NPV}{Initial \ Investment}$$

**Decision Rule:** Accept if **PI > 1**.

## 2.5 Discounted Payback Period
The number of years required to recover the initial investment from **discounted** cash flows (not just undiscounted).

## 2.6 SCM-Specific Discounting Models

### 2.6.1 Quantity Discount Model (All-Units Discount)
When a supplier offers a **lower unit price** if the buyer purchases above a certain quantity threshold, the buyer's total annual cost becomes:

$$TC(Q) = D \cdot P(Q) + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H + \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

where:
- *D* = Annual demand (units)
- *P(Q)* = Unit price (depends on quantity slab)
- *Q* = Order quantity
- *H* = Holding cost per unit per year
- *S* = Ordering cost per order

**Procedure:**
1. Compute EOQ at the **lowest price** first.
2. If EOQ falls in the lowest price slab → done.
3. If EOQ falls in a higher slab → compare total cost at:
   - The adjusted EOQ for that slab
   - The **minimum quantity** for that slab (price break)
4. Pick the option with **lowest total cost**.

### 2.6.2 Cash Discount Model (Incremental Discount)
A supplier offers terms like *"2/10 net 30"* — meaning a **2% discount** if paid within **10 days**, otherwise full payment in **30 days**.

**Effective annual cost of forgoing the discount:**

$$r_{effective} = \frac{d}{1 - d} \times \frac{365}{T}$$

where *d* = discount rate, *T* = extra days credit gained by not taking the discount.

If $r_{effective}$ > firm's cost of capital → **TAKE the discount** (borrow at higher rate from bank to pay early).
If $r_{effective}$ < cost of capital → skip the discount only if cash is short.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Future Value | $FV = PV(1+r)^n$ | Compounded growth |
| 2 | Present Value | $PV = FV / (1+r)^n$ | Discounting future money |
| 3 | PV of Annuity | $PV = A \cdot \dfrac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | Equal periodic receipts |
| 4 | FV of Annuity | $FV = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r}$ | Future worth of equal deposits |
| 5 | Perpetuity | $PV = A/r$ | Infinite stream of cash flows |
| 6 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} CF_t / (1+r)^t$ | Accept if NPV > 0 |
| 7 | IRR (definition) | $\sum CF_t / (1+IRR)^t = 0$ | Rate that makes NPV = 0 |
| 8 | IRR (interpolation) | $r_1 + \dfrac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2}(r_2 - r_1)$ | Approximate IRR |
| 9 | Profitability Index | $PI = 1 + NPV / CF_0$ | Accept if PI > 1 |
| 10 | EOQ | $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS / H}$ | Basic Wilson formula |
| 11 | Total Cost (with discount) | $TC = DP + (Q/2)H + (D/Q)S$ | Compare price-break options |
| 12 | Effective cost of skipping cash discount | $r_e = \dfrac{d}{1-d} \times \dfrac{365}{T}$ | Decide take or skip |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfall:** In KTU valuation, students often forget to use $\vert$ sign conventions correctly when NPV is negative. Always write the NPV formula with **explicit plus and minus signs** for cash flows.

## 2.8 Engineering & Real-World Utility

- **Logistics Network Design:** NPV is used to compare alternative warehouse locations over 20–30 year horizons.
- **Fleet Replacement:** IRR analysis decides whether replacing an old truck is more profitable than continuing to use it.
- **Vendor Financing Decisions:** Cash discount models drive working-capital policy in procurement.
- **Capital Budgeting in 3PLs:** Third-party logistics providers evaluate automation projects (e.g., ASRS) using NPV.
- **Sustainability Investments:** NPV of solar-powered warehouses vs. diesel alternatives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Present Value of an Ordinary Annuity

**Starting Point:** A stream of equal payments *A* at the end of periods 1, 2, 3, …, n. Each payment is discounted at rate *r*.

$$PV = \frac{A}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{A}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{A}{(1+r)^3} + \dots + \frac{A}{(1+r)^n}$$

Factor out the common term $A$:

$$PV = A \left[ \frac{1}{(1+r)} + \frac{1}{(1+r)^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{(1+r)^n} \right]$$

Let $x = \dfrac{1}{1+r}$. The bracket becomes a finite geometric series $x + x^2 + \dots + x^n$.

**Sum of a geometric series of n terms with first term x and common ratio x:**

$$S = x \cdot \frac{1 - x^n}{1 - x}$$

Substituting back $x = 1/(1+r)$:

$$S = \frac{1}{1+r} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{1 - \frac{1}{1+r}}$$

The denominator simplifies:

$$1 - \frac{1}{1+r} = \frac{(1+r) - 1}{1+r} = \frac{r}{1+r}$$

So:

$$S = \frac{1}{1+r} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r/(1+r)} = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

Therefore, the **Present Value of an Ordinary Annuity** is:

$$\boxed{PV_{annuity} = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}}$$

This is the most heavily tested formula in SCM discounting problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Discounted Payback Period Logic

For a project with initial investment $CF_0$ (negative) and positive discounted cash flows $DCF_t = CF_t / (1+r)^t$:

1. Compute cumulative discounted cash flow (CDCF) year by year.
2. The **discounted payback period** is the year in which CDCF turns non-negative.
3. If positive CDCF in year *k* and negative in year *(k–1)*, use linear interpolation:

$$\text{Discounted Payback} = (k-1) + \frac{|CDCF_{k-1}|}{DCF_k}$$

## 3.3 Solved Example 1 — NPV of a Warehouse Investment (Standard 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A logistics company is considering investing **₹20,00,000** in a new automated warehouse. The expected net cash inflows are:
- Year 1: ₹6,00,000
- Year 2: ₹7,00,000
- Year 3: ₹8,00,000
- Year 4: ₹6,00,000
- Year 5: ₹5,00,000

The cost of capital is **10% per annum**. Should the project be accepted?

**Step 1 — Identify cash flows and discount rate:**
$CF_0 = -20,00,000$; $r = 0.10$; cash inflows for years 1 to 5.

**Step 2 — Compute PVIF for each year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PVIF_1 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^1} = 0.9091 \\
PVIF_2 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^2} = 0.8264 \\
PVIF_3 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^3} = 0.7513 \\
PVIF_4 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^4} = 0.6830 \\
PVIF_5 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^5} = 0.6209
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute PV of each cash flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 6,00,000 \times 0.9091 = 5,45,455 \\
PV_2 &= 7,00,000 \times 0.8264 = 5,78,512 \\
PV_3 &= 8,00,000 \times 0.7513 = 6,01,045 \\
PV_4 &= 6,00,000 \times 0.6830 = 4,09,800 \\
PV_5 &= 5,00,000 \times 0.6209 = 3,10,460
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum of PVs of inflows:**

$$\sum PV = 5,45,455 + 5,78,512 + 6,01,045 + 4,09,800 + 3,10,460 = 24,45,272$$

**Step 5 — Subtract initial investment:**

$$NPV = 24,45,272 - 20,00,000 = +4,45,272$$

**Step 6 — Decision:** Since NPV > 0, **ACCEPT** the project.

## 3.4 Solved Example 2 — IRR by Interpolation

**Problem:** Initial investment ₹10,00,000; cash inflows: Year 1 = ₹4,00,000, Year 2 = ₹5,00,000, Year 3 = ₹4,00,000. Find IRR.

**Step 1 — Try r₁ = 10%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{10\%} &= -10,00,000 + \frac{4,00,000}{1.10} + \frac{5,00,000}{1.21} + \frac{4,00,000}{1.331} \\
&= -10,00,000 + 3,63,636 + 4,13,223 + 3,00,526 \\
&= +77,385
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Try r₂ = 15%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{15\%} &= -10,00,000 + \frac{4,00,000}{1.15} + \frac{5,00,000}{1.3225} + \frac{4,00,000}{1.5209} \\
&= -10,00,000 + 3,47,826 + 3,78,072 + 2,63,034 \\
&= -11,068
\end{aligned}
$$

NPV changes sign between 10% and 15% → IRR lies in this range.

**Step 3 — Interpolate:**

$$IRR = 10\% + \frac{77,385}{77,385 - (-11,068)} \times (15\% - 10\%)$$

$$IRR = 10\% + \frac{77,385}{88,453} \times 5\%$$

$$IRR = 10\% + 0.8748 \times 5\% = 10\% + 4.37\% \approx 14.37\%$$

**Decision:** If cost of capital is 12%, **accept** (IRR = 14.37% > 12%).

## 3.5 Solved Example 3 — Quantity Discount Decision (SCM-Specific)

**Problem:** Annual demand *D* = 10,000 units. Ordering cost *S* = ₹500 per order. Holding cost *H* = ₹20 per unit per year. Price schedule:

| Quantity Range | Unit Price (₹) |
|---|---|
| 1 – 999 | 50 |
| 1,000 – 1,999 | 48 |
| 2,000 and above | 45 |

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ at lowest price (₹50):**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10000 \times 500}{20}} = \sqrt{5,00,000} \approx 707 \text{ units}$$

This EOQ falls in the **first slab** (1–999) → feasible.

**Step 2 — Total cost at EOQ = 707, P = ₹50:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_1 &= 10000 \times 50 + \frac{707}{2} \times 20 + \frac{10000}{707} \times 500 \\
&= 5,00,000 + 7,070 + 7,072 \\
&= 5,14,142
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Check price-break options:**

*Option B: Q = 1,000 (price break):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_2 &= 10000 \times 48 + \frac{1000}{2} \times 20 + \frac{10000}{1000} \times 500 \\
&= 4,80,000 + 10,000 + 5,000 \\
&= 4,95,000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Option C: Q = 2,000 (price break):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_3 &= 10000 \times 45 + \frac{2000}{2} \times 20 + \frac{10000}{2000} \times 500 \\
&= 4,50,000 + 20,000 + 2,500 \\
&= 4,72,500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:** Lowest total cost is **₹4,72,500** at Q = 2,000. **Order 2,000 units per cycle.**

## 3.6 Solved Example 4 — Cash Discount Effective Cost

**Problem:** Supplier offers "1.5/10 net 45". Firm's cost of capital is 12%. Should the firm take the discount?

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**
- Discount *d* = 1.5% = 0.015
- Full payment period = 45 days
- Discount period = 10 days
- Extra days credit by skipping discount: *T* = 45 − 10 = 35 days

**Step 2 — Compute effective annual cost:**

$$r_e = \frac{0.015}{1 - 0.015} \times \frac{365}{35}$$

$$r_e = 0.01523 \times 10.4286 = 0.1588 = 15.88\%$$

**Step 3 — Decision:** $r_e = 15.88\%$ > cost of capital 12% → **TAKE the discount**. The firm should borrow at 12% to pay early and save 15.88% — net gain of ~3.88%.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Discounting Model

| Situation | Recommended Model | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Single project, multi-year cash flows | **NPV** | Gold standard, accounts for TVM and all flows |
| Ranking mutually exclusive projects of unequal size | **PI** | Normalizes NPV by investment size |
| Want to know "rate of return" % | **IRR** | Easy communication to non-finance managers |
| Need to know "how fast we recover cash" | **Discounted Payback** | Emphasizes liquidity and risk |
| Supplier offers lower price for larger Q | **Quantity Discount TC** | Direct SCM procurement decision |
| Supplier offers early-payment discount | **Effective cost of credit** | Compares discount vs. borrowing cost |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Discounting Process Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Cash Flows CF0 CF1 ... CFn] --> B[Determine Discount Rate r]
    B --> C{Choose Model}
    C -->|Single Project| D[Compute PV of Each CFt]
    C -->|Annuity| E[Apply PVAF Formula]
    C -->|Quantity Discount| F[Compute EOQ at Each Price Slab]
    C -->|Cash Discount| G[Compute Effective Annual Cost]
    D --> H[Sum PVs minus Initial Investment]
    E --> H
    F --> I[Compare Total Costs across Slabs]
    G --> J[Compare with Cost of Capital]
    H --> K{NPV Decision}
    K -->|NPV > 0| L[ACCEPT Project]
    K -->|NPV = 0| M[Indifferent]
    K -->|NPV < 0| N[REJECT Project]
    I --> O[Choose Minimum Total Cost Option]
    J --> P{Take Discount?}
    P -->|re > r| Q[TAKE Discount]
    P -->|re < r| R[Skip Discount if Cash Constrained]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — NPV Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        A1[Initial Investment CF0]
        A2[Annual Cash Inflows CF1 to CFn]
        A3[Discount Rate r percent]
        A4[Project Life n years]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS
        B1[Compute PVIF t = 1 divide 1 plus r to power t]
        B2[PVt = CFt times PVIFt]
        B3[Sum of PVs of Inflows]
        B4[NPV = Sum PVs minus CF0]
        B5[Compute PI = Sum PVs divided by CF0]
    end

    subgraph DECISION
        C1{NPV Sign Check}
        C2[Accept if NPV greater than 0]
        C3[Reject if NPV less than 0]
    end

    A1 --> B4
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B3 --> B5
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Comparison Map — Discounting Models in SCM Decision-Making

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[SCM Discounting Decisions] --> R1[Capital Investment]
    Root --> R2[Procurement]
    Root --> R3[Working Capital]

    R1 --> M1[NPV Model]
    R1 --> M2[IRR Model]
    R1 --> M3[Discounted Payback]

    R2 --> M4[Quantity Discount EOQ]
    R2 --> M5[All Units vs Incremental Discount]

    R3 --> M6[Cash Discount Effective Cost]
    R3 --> M7[Net Present Value of Credit Terms]

    M1 --> Out1[Accept if NPV greater than 0]
    M2 --> Out2[Accept if IRR greater than r]
    M3 --> Out3[Accept if Payback less than cutoff]
    M4 --> Out4[Choose Q with minimum TC]
    M5 --> Out5[Use incremental analysis]
    M6 --> Out6[Take discount if effective cost greater than r]
    M7 --> Out7[Maximize NPV of credit policy]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading Guide for Mermaid Diagrams:**  
> - `A --> B` means A flows to B (directed arrow).  
> - `{}` denotes decision nodes.  
> - `[]` denotes process or input blocks.  
> - `subgraph` groups logically related steps.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of Net Present Value (NPV) and state its decision rule in supply chain capital investment decisions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Points):**
1. **Definition (1.5 Marks):** NPV is the difference between the present value of cash inflows and the present value of cash outflows over the life of a project, discounted at the firm's cost of capital. It is computed as $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$.
2. **Decision Rule (1 Mark):** Accept the project if NPV > 0 (it adds value), reject if NPV < 0 (it destroys value), and remain indifferent if NPV = 0.
3. **SCM Relevance (0.5 Mark):** In SCM, NPV is used to evaluate investments in warehouses, fleets, automation, and IT systems where cash flows occur over many years.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between NPV and IRR as discounting models. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **NPV (1.25 Marks):** Gives the absolute monetary value added by a project in today's rupees. Decision: accept if NPV > 0.
2. **IRR (1.25 Marks):** Gives the percentage rate of return earned by the project. Decision: accept if IRR > cost of capital.
3. **Advantage of NPV (0.25 Mark):** Always consistent with the goal of shareholder wealth maximization; can be added across projects.
4. **Advantage of IRR (0.25 Mark):** Communicates the project's return as a percentage, which is easy for non-financial managers to understand.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

A logistics company is evaluating two mutually exclusive investment proposals for warehouse automation. The expected net cash flows (in ₹ lakhs) are:

| Year | Project X | Project Y |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (Initial Investment) | −50 | −70 |
| 1 | 15 | 20 |
| 2 | 18 | 24 |
| 3 | 20 | 25 |
| 4 | 18 | 22 |
| 5 | 12 | 15 |

The cost of capital is **12%** per annum.

**(a) Compute the NPV of both projects. Which project should be selected based on NPV?** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Compute the IRR of both projects and the Profitability Index (PI). Verify your selection and discuss any conflict between NPV and PI rankings.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — PVIF values at r = 12%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PVIF_1 &= 1 / (1.12)^1 = 0.8929 \\
PVIF_2 &= 1 / (1.12)^2 = 0.7972 \\
PVIF_3 &= 1 / (1.12)^3 = 0.7118 \\
PVIF_4 &= 1 / (1.12)^4 = 0.6355 \\
PVIF_5 &= 1 / (1.12)^5 = 0.5674
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Project X NPV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_X &= -50 + (15 \times 0.8929) + (18 \times 0.7972) + (20 \times 0.7118) \\
&\quad + (18 \times 0.6355) + (12 \times 0.5674) \\
&= -50 + 13.394 + 14.350 + 14.236 + 11.439 + 6.809 \\
&= -50 + 60.228 \\
&= \textbf{+10.228 lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Project Y NPV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_Y &= -70 + (20 \times 0.8929) + (24 \times 0.7972) + (25 \times 0.7118) \\
&\quad + (22 \times 0.6355) + (15 \times 0.5674) \\
&= -70 + 17.858 + 19.133 + 17.795 + 13.981 + 8.511 \\
&= -70 + 77.278 \\
&= \textbf{+7.278 lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:** $NPV_X = 10.228 > NPV_Y = 7.278$. **Select Project X.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — IRR of Project X (interpolation):**
- At r = 18%, try:
$NPV_X(18\%) = -50 + (15/1.18) + (18/1.3924) + (20/1.6430) + (18/1.9388) + (12/2.2878)$
$= -50 + 12.712 + 12.928 + 12.173 + 9.284 + 5.245 = +2.342$
- At r = 25%, try:
$NPV_X(25\%) = -50 + (15/1.25) + (18/1.5625) + (20/1.9531) + (18/2.4414) + (12/3.0518)$
$= -50 + 12.000 + 11.520 + 10.240 + 7.372 + 3.932 = -4.936$

**Interpolation:**

$$IRR_X = 18\% + \frac{2.342}{2.342 - (-4.936)} \times (25\% - 18\%)$$

$$IRR_X = 18\% + \frac{2.342}{7.278} \times 7\% = 18\% + 0.3218 \times 7\% \approx \textbf{20.25\%}$$

**Step 2 — IRR of Project Y (interpolation):**
- At r = 15%:
$NPV_Y(15\%) = -70 + (20/1.15) + (24/1.3225) + (25/1.5209) + (22/1.7490) + (15/2.0114)$
$= -70 + 17.391 + 18.147 + 16.438 + 12.579 + 7.457 = +2.012$
- At r = 20%:
$NPV_Y(20\%) = -70 + (20/1.20) + (24/1.44) + (25/1.728) + (22/2.0736) + (15/2.4883)$
$= -70 + 16.667 + 16.667 + 14.468 + 10.610 + 6.028 = -5.560$

**Interpolation:**

$$IRR_Y = 15\% + \frac{2.012}{2.012 - (-5.560)} \times 5\% = 15\% + \frac{2.012}{7.572} \times 5\% \approx \textbf{16.33\%}$$

**Step 3 — Profitability Index:**

$$PI_X = \frac{60.228}{50} = 1.205$$

$$PI_Y = \frac{77.278}{70} = 1.104$$

**Step 4 — Verification and Conflict Discussion:**

- **NPV ranking:** X > Y (select X).
- **IRR ranking:** X (20.25%) > Y (16.33%) — **no conflict**.
- **PI ranking:** X (1.205) > Y (1.104) — **no conflict**.
- All three methods agree: **Select Project X** because it has higher NPV, higher IRR, and higher PI.
- **Note on PI conflict:** If there were a conflict (e.g., Y has higher PI but lower NPV), the NPV rule should prevail because NPV measures absolute value addition, while PI normalizes by investment size and is unreliable for ranking mutually exclusive projects with different scales.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

A retail supply chain procures **20,000 units annually** of a fast-moving consumer good. The ordering cost is **₹1,000 per order** and the holding cost is **₹25 per unit per year**. A supplier offers the following **all-units quantity discount** schedule:

| Quantity Slab (units) | Unit Price (₹) |
|---|---|
| 1 – 499 | 100 |
| 500 – 999 | 95 |
| 1,000 and above | 90 |

**(a) Compute the basic Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) and determine whether the discount is attractive. Show all calculations.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Compare total annual costs at the EOQ and at each price break. Recommend the optimal order quantity with full justification.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — EOQ Formula:**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 20000 \times 1000}{25}} = \sqrt{1,600,000} = 1264.91 \approx 1265 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Check slab:** EOQ = 1265 lies in the slab **"1,000 and above"** at unit price ₹90.

**Step 3 — Conclusion of Part (a):** Since the EOQ already falls in the highest-discount slab, the discount is potentially attractive — but we must verify total cost before deciding, because the higher quantity increases holding cost.

**Step 4 — Sanity check on inventory turns:** Annual turns = 20000/1265 ≈ 15.8, which is operationally reasonable.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Total cost formula:**

$$TC(Q) = D \cdot P + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H + \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

**Step 2 — Total cost at EOQ = 1265, P = ₹90:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_1 &= 20000 \times 90 + \frac{1265}{2} \times 25 + \frac{20000}{1265} \times 1000 \\
&= 18,00,000 + 15,812.5 + 15,810 \\
&= \textbf{₹18,31,623}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total cost at Q = 1,000 (boundary of highest slab), P = ₹90:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_2 &= 20000 \times 90 + \frac{1000}{2} \times 25 + \frac{20000}{1000} \times 1000 \\
&= 18,00,000 + 12,500 + 20,000 \\
&= \textbf{₹18,32,500}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total cost at the price break of Q = 500 (slab 500–999), P = ₹95:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_3 &= 20000 \times 95 + \frac{500}{2} \times 25 + \frac{20000}{500} \times 1000 \\
&= 19,00,000 + 6,250 + 40,000 \\
&= \textbf{₹19,46,250}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total cost at Q = 499 (lowest slab boundary), P = ₹100:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_4 &= 20000 \times 100 + \frac{499}{2} \times 25 + \frac{20000}{499} \times 1000 \\
&= 20,00,000 + 6,237.5 + 40,080 \\
&= \textbf{₹20,46,318}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Cost comparison summary:**

| Option | Q | Unit Price (₹) | Total Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (EOQ) | 1,265 | 90 | **18,31,623** ✅ |
| 2 (Break) | 1,000 | 90 | 18,32,500 |
| 3 (Mid slab) | 500 | 95 | 19,46,250 |
| 4 (Low slab) | 499 | 100 | 20,46,318 |

**Step 7 — Recommendation:** The **minimum total cost is ₹18,31,623** at **Q = 1,265 units**. Therefore, the optimal order quantity is **1,265 units per order**, taking advantage of the ₹90 unit price. This yields annual savings of ₹20,46,318 − ₹18,31,623 = **₹2,14,695** compared to the lowest-slab option.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Discounting Questions (KTU Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign on $CF_0$:** Always write $-50$ (or equivalent) clearly; losing the sign gives the wrong NPV.
> 2. **Mixing discount rate and inflation rate:** Discount rate = cost of capital. Do not confuse with WPI/CPI inflation.
> 3. **Quantity discount — wrong candidate Q:** Students often compare only EOQ vs. price break, forgetting to also check EOQ at the next-lower slab. KTU expects **all** candidates to be tabulated.
> 4. **IRR interpolation — using wrong sign:** If $NPV_1 > 0$ and $NPV_2 < 0$, the formula is $r_1 + \frac{NPV_1}{NPV_1 - NPV_2}(r_2 - r_1)$. Using $NPV_1 - NPV_2$ with both negative will give nonsense.
> 5. **Not stating the decision rule explicitly:** KTU markers award 1 mark for clearly stating the accept/reject criterion.
> 6. **Cash discount — using 360 days:** Most KTU problems use **365 days**; check the question.
> 7. **Annuity problems — using end-of-period vs. beginning:** If the question says "at the beginning of each year", the annuity due factor is $(1+r) \times PVAF_{ordinary}$; missing this loses full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Top 15 Must-Remember Points on Discounting Models:**
> 1. **Time value of money** is the foundation — a rupee today > a rupee tomorrow.
> 2. **Discount rate (r)** is the cost of capital; use the firm's hurdle rate, not bank rate.
> 3. **NPV formula:** $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} CF_t / (1+r)^t$; accept if NPV > 0.
> 4. **PVIF** = $1/(1+r)^t$; **FVIF** = $(1+r)^t$.
> 5. **PV of annuity** = $A \cdot [1 - (1+r)^{-n}] / r$.
> 6. **FV of annuity** = $A \cdot [(1+r)^n - 1] / r$.
> 7. **Perpetuity** = $A/r$.
> 8. **IRR** = rate that makes NPV = 0; use **linear interpolation** when not directly solvable.
> 9. **PI** = PV of inflows / Initial Investment; accept if PI > 1.
> 10. **Discounted Payback** = year in which cumulative discounted cash flow first becomes non-negative.
> 11. **Quantity discount decision rule:** Compute total cost at EOQ and at each price-break quantity; pick minimum TC.
> 12. **Cash discount effective annual cost** = $\dfrac{d}{1-d} \times \dfrac{365}{T}$; take the discount if this > cost of capital.
> 13. **Annuity due** (beginning of period) = ordinary annuity × (1+r).
> 14. **Reinvestment assumption:** NPV assumes cash flows are reinvested at the discount rate; IRR assumes reinvestment at the IRR (often unrealistic — favour NPV for high-stakes SCM decisions).
> 15. **Mutually exclusive projects:** Always trust **NPV** over IRR/PI when rankings conflict.

> [!TIP]  
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer in three sections — (i) **formula used**, (ii) **calculation table** with PVIFs, (iii) **decision rule + final recommendation**. This mirrors the KTU marking scheme and ensures you secure the method marks even if the final arithmetic has a small slip.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-item Inventory Models and Production-Consumption Models Related to Supply Chains Including Multi-Echelon Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET502 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Multi-item Inventory Models and Production-Consumption Models Related to Supply Chains Including Multi-Echelon Systems ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Multi-Item Inventory Models — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Multi-Item Deterministic Inventory Model** — A classical Operations Research extension of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) framework in which **two or more distinct stock-keeping units (SKUs)** are procured and stocked simultaneously within a shared resource environment (warehouse space, capital budget, or manpower), and the replenishment decision for each item is mathematically coupled through one or more binding constraints. **Key distinguishing features (KTU 2024 syllabus terminology):** - The objective function becomes the **sum of individual EOQ costs** across all items. - At least one shared constraint (typically floor space or capital) is active. - Solutions require constrained optimization, normally via the **Lagrange Multiplier** technique. - The classical independent EOQ is replaced by a **coordinated lot-sizing policy**. ### 1.2 Production-Consumption Model — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Production-Consumption Model (also called the Economic Production Quantity / EPQ model)** — A deterministic lot-sizing model in which the item is **produced internally at a finite production rate $p$** and **consumed (demanded) at rate $d$**, where $p > d$. Inventory therefore *accumulates during production* and *depletes during non-production* within the same replenishment cycle. Unlike the EOQ (where replenishment is instantaneous), the EPQ assumes **gradual replenishment**, which reduces the maximum inventory level and consequently the average holding cost. ### 1.3 Multi-Echelon Inventory Systems — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Multi-Echelon Inventory System** — A supply chain architecture in which inventory is held at **two or more sequential stages (echelons)** such as retailer → warehouse → distributor → manufacturer. The total system cost is the sum of holding, ordering, and shortage costs at each echelon, and **demand is propagated upstream** through orders, while material flows downstream. ### 1.4 Real-World Analogies (Intuition) > [!TIP] > **Analogy 1 — Multi-Item Model (The Shared Refrigerator):** > Imagine one refrigerator in a hostel shared by 50 students. Each student (item) has an optimal personal grocery quantity, but the *shelf space is limited*. You cannot independently optimize each student's stock — you must **coordinate** so that the *total volume of items* fits. This is exactly the multi-item constraint problem. > [!TIP] > **Analogy 2 — Production-Consumption Model (The Water Tap & Drain):** > Think of a water tank with a tap filling it at rate $p$ (production) and a hole at the bottom draining it at rate $d$ (consumption). Since $p > d$, the tank fills during "production time," then only drains during the rest of the cycle. The *maximum* water level (peak inventory) is *less* than the full quantity filled — exactly the EPQ behavior. > [!TIP] > **Analogy 3 — Multi-Echelon System (The Indian Postal Hierarchy):** > A letter posted in Kochi travels: Post Office → Sorting Hub → Regional Depot → National Depot → Delivery. Each stage holds "inventory" (letters in transit/stock). Total supply chain cost depends on inventory at **every** stage, not just the origin or destination. This is multi-echelon stock optimization. ### 1.5 Important Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Units | |---|---|---| | Annual demand | $D$ | units/year | | Order / setup cost | $A$ | ₹/order | | Holding cost per unit per year | $H$ | ₹/unit/year | | Production rate | $p$ | units/year | | Demand (consumption) rate | $d$ | units/year | | Storage space per unit | $a$ | m²/unit | | Total available storage space | $W$ | m² | | Optimal order quantity | $Q^{*}$ | units | | Optimal cycle time | $T^{*}$ | year | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Multi-Item Deterministic Model — Logical Decomposition **Step 1 — Independent EOQ Cost Formulation:** For a single item $i$, the total relevant cost per year is: $$TC_i(Q_i) = \frac{A_i D_i}{Q_i} + \frac{H_i Q_i}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Aggregate Cost Across All Items:** When $n$ items are stored together with no constraint, the optimal solution is the *sum of independent EOQs*: $$TC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{A_i D_i}{Q_i} + \frac{H_i Q_i}{2} \right)$$ **Step 3 — Introduction of Storage Constraint:** The classical unconstrained optimum often violates the warehouse capacity $W$. The active constraint becomes: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i Q_i \leq W$$ **Step 4 — Lagrange Multiplier Construction:** $$\mathcal{L}(Q_i, \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{A_i D_i}{Q_i} + \frac{H_i Q_i}{2} \right) + \lambda \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i Q_i - W \right)$$ **Step 5 — First-Order Necessary Conditions:** $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q_i} = -\frac{A_i D_i}{Q_i^{2}} + \frac{H_i}{2} + \lambda a_i = 0$$ Solving for $Q_i^{*}$: $$Q_i^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 A_i D_i}{H_i + 2 \lambda a_i}}$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation:** - When $\lambda = 0$ → unconstrained classical EOQ result. - When $\lambda > 0$ → storage constraint is binding; lot sizes are *reduced* relative to EOQ. - The multiplier $\lambda$ is found by back-substitution into the constraint. ### 2.2 Production-Consumption (EPQ) Model — Logical Decomposition **Step 1 — Cycle Structure:** Within cycle time $T$ (where $T = Q/d$), the system produces for time $t_1 = Q/p$ and idles for $t_2 = T - t_1$. **Step 2 — Maximum Inventory:** During production, inventory grows at net rate $(p - d)$. Maximum level reached at end of production: $$I_{\max} = (p - d) \cdot t_1 = Q \left( 1 - \frac{d}{p} \right)$$ **Step 3 — Average Inventory:** Since inventory rises linearly from $0$ to $I_{\max}$ and then falls back to $0$ in each cycle: $$\bar{I} = \frac{I_{\max}}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}\left( 1 - \frac{d}{p} \right)$$ **Step 4 — Cost per Unit Time:** $$TC(Q) = \frac{A D}{Q} + \frac{H Q}{2}\left( 1 - \frac{d}{p} \right)$$ **Step 5 — Optimization:** Setting $dTC/dQ = 0$ leads to the famous EPQ formula shown in the cheat sheet below. ### 2.3 Multi-Echelon Systems — Logical Decomposition **Echelon 1 — Clark-Scarf Decomposition:** For a serial $n$-echelon system with deterministic lead times, the cost function is **additive across echelons**, enabling decentralized optimization. Each echelon uses an independent base-stock or $(s, S)$ policy. **Echelon 2 — Installation vs. Echelon Stock:** - **Installation stock at echelon $i$** = physical on-hand inventory at stage $i$ alone. - **Echelon stock at echelon $i$** = installation stock at stage $i$ plus all stocks held *downstream* at stages $1, 2, \dots, i-1$. **Echelon 3 — Optimal Policy Insight:** For serial systems, the optimal echelon stock level *decreases* as we move upstream (toward the manufacturer). This is because demand uncertainty compounds upstream. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Constraint / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $EOQ_i = \sqrt{2 A_i D_i / H_i}$ | Independent EOQ per item | $\lambda = 0$ (unconstrained) | | 2 | $Q_i^{*} = \sqrt{2 A_i D_i / (H_i + 2 \lambda a_i)}$ | Constrained multi-item lot size | $\lambda > 0$ | | 3 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \cdot \sqrt{2 A_i D_i / (H_i + 2 \lambda a_i)} = W$ | Determining $\lambda$ | Solve by iteration | | 4 | $EPQ = \sqrt{2 A D / \left[ H \left(1 - d/p\right) \right]}$ | Economic Production Quantity | $p > d$ | | 5 | $T^{*} = \sqrt{2 A / \left[ H D \left(1 - d/p\right) \right]}$ | Optimal cycle time (years) | $T^{*} = Q^{*}/d$ | | 6 | $t_1^{*} = Q^{*}/p$ | Production run time per cycle | $t_1 < T$ | | 7 | $I_{\max} = Q^{*} \left(1 - d/p\right)$ | Maximum inventory | Always $< Q$ | | 8 | $TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 A D H \left(1 - d/p\right)}$ | Minimum annual cost | EPQ system | | 9 | $EI_i = II_i + II_{i-1} + \cdots + II_1$ | Echelon stock recursion | Multi-echelon | | 10 | $D_{i}^{echelon} = D$ | Demand propagated upstream | Serial chain | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Supply Chain Utility - **Manufacturing plants**: Optimal lot sizing when multiple SKUs share a common machine, raw material, or warehouse bay. - **Process industries** (chemicals, FMCG, cement): EPQ is the *default* model because production is continuous, not instantaneous. - **Distribution networks**: Multi-echelon analysis underpins Amazon's, Flipkart's, and Walmart's inventory placement decisions. - **Defense logistics**: Spare parts inventory for aircraft is a classic multi-echelon application (METRIC model). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Lagrange Multiplier Solution for Multi-Item Model with Storage Constraint **Problem Setup:** A firm procures $n$ items sharing a warehouse of total floor space $W$. Item $i$ has: - Annual demand $D_i$ (units/year) - Setup cost $A_i$ (₹/order) - Holding cost $H_i$ (₹/unit/year) - Floor space per unit $a_i$ (m²/unit) Find $Q_1, Q_2, \dots, Q_n$ that minimizes total annual cost subject to the warehouse capacity. **Step 1: Cost Function Assembly** $$TC(Q_1, Q_2, \dots, Q_n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \frac{A_i D_i}{Q_i} + \frac{H_i Q_i}{2} \right]$$ The first term is the **annual setup (ordering) cost** — frequency $D_i/Q_i$ times cost per setup $A_i$. The second term is the **annual holding cost** — average inventory $Q_i/2$ times holding cost per unit $H_i$. Unit purchase cost $C_i$ is omitted because it does not depend on $Q_i$. **Step 2: Constraint Specification** $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i Q_i \leq W$$ Equality holds at optimum (otherwise we could increase some $Q_i$ and reduce cost). **Step 3: Lagrangian Construction** $$\mathcal{L}(Q_1, \dots, Q_n, \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \frac{A_i D_i}{Q_i} + \frac{H_i Q_i}{2} \right] + \lambda \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i Q_i - W \right]$$ **Step 4: Partial Derivative with Respect to $Q_i$** $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q_i} = -\frac{A_i D_i}{Q_i^{2}} + \frac{H_i}{2} + \lambda a_i = 0$$ Solving explicitly: $$Q_i^{2} = \frac{2 A_i D_i}{H_i + 2 \lambda a_i}$$ $$\boxed{\;Q_i^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 A_i D_i}{H_i + 2 \lambda a_i}}\;}$$ **Step 5: Determining $\lambda$ via Back-Substitution** Substitute the optimal $Q_i^{*}$ into the binding constraint: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 A_i D_i}{H_i + 2 \lambda a_i}} = W$$ This equation is **non-linear in $\lambda$** and must be solved iteratively (Newton-Raphson, bisection, or trial-and-error). **Step 6: Numerical Illustration** > **Numerical Example.** Two items share 100 m² of warehouse space. > > | Item | $D_i$ | $A_i$ | $H_i$ | $a_i$ | $EOQ_i$ | > |---|---|---|---|---|---| > | 1 | 1000 | 100 | 5 | 1 | 200 | > | 2 | 500 | 50 | 4 | 2 | $\approx 112$ | > > Total unconstrained space = $1(200) + 2(112) = 424$ m² — exceeds $W = 100$. So $\lambda > 0$. > > Solving the iteration yields $\lambda \approx 0.85$, giving $Q_1^{*} \approx 144$ and $Q_2^{*} \approx 85$, occupying exactly 100 m². --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Economic Production Quantity (EPQ) Formula **Problem Setup:** A firm manufactures a product internally: - Production rate $p$ (units/year) - Demand rate $d$ (units/year), with $p > d$ - Setup cost $A$ per production run - Holding cost $H$ per unit per year - Production lot size $Q$ (units per run) Find $Q$ that minimizes the average annual cost. **Step 1: Cycle Time Derivation** Each cycle covers demand $Q$, so: $$T = \frac{Q}{d}$$ Within this cycle, production occurs for duration: $$t_1 = \frac{Q}{p}$$ **Step 2: Inventory Profile** During production: net accumulation rate = $(p - d)$. Therefore peak inventory: $$I_{\max} = (p - d) t_1 = (p - d) \cdot \frac{Q}{p} = Q \left( 1 - \frac{d}{p} \right)$$ During non-production (length $T - t_1$): inventory depletes at rate $d$, returning to zero. **Step 3: Average Inventory** The inventory-time graph is a triangle (rising then falling): $$\bar{I} = \frac{I_{\max}}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}\left( 1 - \frac{d}{p} \right)$$ **Step 4: Annual Cost Function** $$TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{A D}{Q}}_{\text{setup cost per year}} + \underbrace{\frac{H Q}{2}\left( 1 - \frac{d}{p} \right)}_{\text{holding cost per year}}$$ **Step 5: First-Order Condition** $$\frac{dTC}{dQ} = -\frac{A D}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2}\left( 1 - \frac{d}{p} \right) = 0$$ $$Q^{2} = \frac{2 A D}{H \left( 1 - d/p \right)}$$ $$\boxed{\;Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 A D}{H \left( 1 - d/p \right)}} = \frac{EOQ}{\sqrt{1 - d/p}}\;}$$ **Step 6: Second-Order Sufficiency** $$\frac{d^{2} TC}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2 A D}{Q^{3}} > 0 \quad \checkmark$$ The minimum is confirmed. **Step 7: Cost at Optimum** $$TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 A D H \left( 1 - d/p \right)}$$ --- ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Echelon Stock Position in Serial Multi-Echelon System **Problem Setup:** Consider a serial supply chain with $n$ echelons in series: Manufacturer → Warehouse → Retailer. **Step 1: Definition Recursion** For echelon $i$, define: - $II_i$ = Installation (on-hand) stock at stage $i$ - $EI_i$ = Echelon stock at level $i$ - $OI_i$ = Pipeline (in-transit) stock at stage $i$ The echelon stock is the cumulative downstream installation stock: $$EI_i = II_i + II_{i-1} + \cdots + II_1$$ **Step 2: Echelon Stock Position (ESP)** $$ESP_i = EI_i + \text{Pipeline stock approaching stage } i$$ **Step 3: Reorder Point at Echelon $i$** Under a base-stock policy with safety stock $SS_i$: $$s_i = D_i \cdot L_i + SS_i$$ where $L_i$ is the lead time to stage $i$. **Step 4: Cost Decomposition (Clark-Scarf Insight)** For a serial system, the total system cost can be written as an *unconstrained sum* of single-echelon costs by treating echelon stock as the decision variable. Each echelon then independently minimizes: $$TC_i = \frac{A_i D}{EI_i} + H_i \cdot \frac{EI_i}{2} + \text{stockout cost at stage } i$$ This is one of the most powerful results in supply chain theory because it **decomposes** a coupled multi-echelon problem into $n$ independent subproblems. --- ### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix | Aspect | EOQ Model | EPQ Model | Multi-Item Model | Multi-Echelon Model | |---|---|---|---|---| | Replenishment | Instantaneous | Finite rate $p$ | Instantaneous (multiple items) | Each echelon independent | | Decision Variable | $Q$ | $Q$ | $Q_1, \dots, Q_n$ | $EI_1, \dots, EI_n$ | | Peak Inventory | $Q$ | $Q(1 - d/p)$ | $Q_i$ per item | Varies by echelon | | Constraint | None | $p > d$ | Storage space $W$ | Demand propagation | | Solution Method | Calculus | Calculus | Lagrange multiplier | Decomposition | | Cost Reduction | Baseline | $\sqrt{1 - d/p}$ factor | $\lambda$-dependent | Echelon-by-echelon | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Multi-Item Inventory System (Shared Warehouse Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Supplier 1] --> V1[Item 1

[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the Economic Production Quantity (EPQ) model. State the formula for the optimal lot size and explain why it is smaller than the classical EOQ when production rate is finite.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The EPQ model determines the optimal production lot size when an item is *produced at a finite rate $p$* and *demanded at rate $d$*, with $p > d$. The optimal lot size is: $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 A D}{H \left( 1 - d/p \right)}}$$ The peak inventory is $I_{\max} = Q \left(1 - d/p\right)$, which is *less* than the EOQ peak of $Q$ because part of the lot is consumed even while production is ongoing. Since holding cost is incurred on a *smaller* average inventory, the optimal lot size is *larger* than EOQ (the denominator $\sqrt{1 - d/p} < 1$ effectively inflates $Q$). The classical EOQ is recovered in the limit $p \to \infty$, where $d/p \to 0$. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for explanation)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between **installation stock** and **echelon stock** in a multi-echelon inventory system. Why is the echelon stock concept important for supply chain analysis?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Installation Stock | Echelon Stock | |---|---|---| | Scope | On-hand inventory at a *single* stage | Inventory at a stage *plus* all downstream stages | | Notation | $II_i$ | $EI_i$ | | Recursion | $II_i$ directly observed | $EI_i = II_i + EI_{i-1}$ | | Decision policy | Local to the stage | Globally coordinated | **Importance of Echelon Stock:** 1. Enables **decomposition** of a coupled multi-echelon cost function into independent single-stage problems (Clark-Scarf). 2. Captures the **demand amplification effect** propagating upstream. 3. Provides the natural decision variable for base-stock and $(R, Q)$ policies in serial systems. 4. Used in advanced models like METRIC and DRP (Distribution Requirements Planning). *(3 marks: 1.5 for differentiation, 1.5 for importance)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Choose Either Option) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Multi-Item Inventory Focus **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** *Derive the optimal lot size formula for a multi-item inventory system subject to a single storage area constraint using the Lagrange multiplier method. Clearly state the necessary conditions and explain how the multiplier $\lambda$ is determined.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Objective Function:** For $n$ items, the total annual relevant cost is: $$TC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \frac{A_i D_i}{Q_i} + \frac{H_i Q_i}{2} \right]$$ **Step 2 — Constraint:** $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i Q_i = W \quad \text{(binding at optimum)}$$ **Step 3 — Lagrangian:** $$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \frac{A_i D_i}{Q_i} + \frac{H_i Q_i}{2} \right] + \lambda \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i Q_i - W \right]$$ **Step 4 — First-Order Conditions (one per item):** $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q_i} = -\frac{A_i D_i}{Q_i^{2}} + \frac{H_i}{2} + \lambda a_i = 0$$ Solving: $$Q_i^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 A_i D_i}{H_i + 2 \lambda a_i}}$$ **Step 5 — Determining $\lambda$:** Substitute into constraint: $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \sqrt{\frac{2 A_i D_i}{H_i + 2 \lambda a_i}} = W$$ Iteratively solved (e.g., bisection or Newton-Raphson). **Step 6 — Verification of Second-Order Sufficiency:** $\partial^{2} \mathcal{L}/\partial Q_i^{2} = 2 A_i D_i / Q_i^{3} > 0$, hence minimum. **Interpretation:** As $\lambda \to 0$, $Q_i^{*} \to \sqrt{2 A_i D_i / H_i}$, recovering the unconstrained EOQ. *(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1–4, 2 for Step 5, 1 for sufficiency and interpretation)* --- **(b)** *A firm stocks two items (X and Y) in a warehouse of total capacity 400 m². The data are given below:* | Item | $D_i$ (units/yr) | $A_i$ (₹/order) | $H_i$ (₹/unit/yr) | $a_i$ (m²/unit) | |---|---|---|---|---| | X | 5000 | 200 | 8 | 1.5 | | Y | 3000 | 150 | 6 | 1.0 | *Using the Lagrange multiplier approach, determine the optimal order quantities and verify the constraint.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Unconstrained EOQs:** $$EOQ_X = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 200 \times 5000}{8}} = \sqrt{250000} = 500 \text{ units}$$ $$EOQ_Y = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 150 \times 3000}{6}} = \sqrt{150000} \approx 387.3 \text{ units}$$ **Step 2 — Check Storage:** Space required = $1.5(500) + 1.0(387.3) = 750 + 387.3 = 1137.3$ m² Since $1137.3 > 400$, the constraint is binding. So $\lambda > 0$. **Step 3 — Optimal Lot Size Formulas:** $$Q_X = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 200 \times 5000}{8 + 2 \lambda \times 1.5}} = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}000{,}000}{8 + 3\lambda}}$$ $$Q_Y = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 150 \times 3000}{6 + 2 \lambda \times 1.0}} = \sqrt{\frac{900{,}000}{6 + 2\lambda}}$$ **Step 4 — Iterative Solution:** Constraint: $1.5 \, Q_X + 1.0 \, Q_Y = 400$ **Trial $\lambda = 5$:** $Q_X = \sqrt{2{,}000{,}000 / 23} \approx 295$, $Q_Y = \sqrt{900{,}000 / 16} \approx 237$ Space $= 1.5(295) + 237 = 442.5 + 237 = 679.5$ → too high. **Trial $\lambda = 20$:** $Q_X = \sqrt{2{,}000{,}000 / 68} \approx 172$, $Q_Y = \sqrt{900{,}000 / 46} \approx 140$ Space $= 1.5(172) + 140 = 258 + 140 = 398$ → very close. **Trial $\lambda = 21$:** $Q_X = \sqrt{2{,}000{,}000 / 71} \approx 168$, $Q_Y = \sqrt{900{,}000 / 48} \approx 137$ Space $= 1.5(168) + 137 = 252 + 137 = 389$ → slightly low. **Trial $\lambda \approx 20.3$: (interpolated)** $Q_X \approx 170$, $Q_Y \approx 139$ Space $= 1.5(170) + 139 = 255 + 139 = 394$ → near-optimal. Accept $\lambda^{*} \approx 20.3$, $Q_X^{*} \approx 170$, $Q_Y^{*} \approx 139$. **Step 5 — Verification:** - Setup cost (annual) = $200 \times 5000/170 + 150 \times 3000/139 \approx 5882 + 3237 = 9119$ ₹ - Holding cost = $8 \times 170/2 + 6 \times 139/2 = 680 + 417 = 1097$ ₹ - **Total $\approx 10{,}216$ ₹/year**, with space used $\approx 394 \leq 400$ ✓ *(7 marks: 1 EOQ calculation, 1 constraint check, 3 iterations, 1 verification, 1 final answer)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — EPQ + Multi-Echelon Focus **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** *Explain the Economic Production Quantity (EPQ) model with a finite production rate. Derive the optimal production lot size, the cycle time, the production run time, and the minimum total cost. State clearly the assumptions of the model.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Demand rate $d$ is constant and known. 2. Production rate $p$ is finite, constant, and $p > d$. 3. Setup cost $A$ per production run is fixed. 4. Holding cost $H$ per unit per year is linear. 5. No stockouts permitted. 6. Replenishment is gradual (production occurs over time $t_1$). **Derivation:** Cycle time $T = Q/d$, production time $t_1 = Q/p$. Peak inventory $I_{\max} = Q(1 - d/p)$, average inventory $\bar{I} = Q(1-d/p)/2$. Annual cost: $$TC(Q) = \frac{A D}{Q} + \frac{H Q}{2}\left( 1 - \frac{d}{p} \right)$$ Setting $dTC/dQ = 0$: $$-\frac{A D}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2}\left( 1 - \frac{d}{p} \right) = 0$$ $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 A D}{H (1 - d/p)}}$$ Cycle time: $$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{d} = \sqrt{\frac{2 A}{H D (1 - d/p)}}$$ Production run time: $$t_1^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{p} = \frac{d}{p} \cdot T^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 A D}{H p (p - d)}}$$ Minimum cost: $$TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 A D H \left( 1 - d/p \right)}$$ **Recovery of EOQ:** As $p \to \infty$, $d/p \to 0$, and $Q^{*} \to \sqrt{2 A D / H}$. *(7 marks: 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 1 each for cycle time and minimum cost)* --- **(b)** *A manufacturing firm produces a component at rate $p = 3000$ units/year. Annual demand is $d = 1000$ units/year. Setup cost is $A =$ ₹600 per run, and holding cost is $H =$ ₹3 per unit per year. Determine:* - *(i) the optimal production lot size,* - *(ii) the optimal cycle time,* - *(iii) the production run time per cycle,* - *(iv) the maximum inventory level, and* - *(v) the minimum total annual cost.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $D = d = 1000$, $p = 3000$, $A = 600$, $H = 3$. **Step 1 — Compute $1 - d/p$:** $$1 - \frac{d}{p} = 1 - \frac{1000}{3000} = 1 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3}$$ **Step 2 — Optimal Lot Size:** $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 600 \times 1000}{3 \times (2/3)}} = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}200{,}000}{2}} = \sqrt{600{,}000} \approx 774.6 \text{ units}$$ *(Round to $Q^* \approx 775$ units)* **Step 3 — Optimal Cycle Time:** $$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{d} = \frac{774.6}{1000} \approx 0.7746 \text{ year} \approx 9.3 \text{ months}$$ **Step 4 — Production Run Time per Cycle:** $$t_1^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{p} = \frac{774.6}{3000} \approx 0.2582 \text{ year} \approx 3.1 \text{ months}$$ **Step 5 — Maximum Inventory:** $$I_{\max} = Q^{*} \left( 1 - \frac{d}{p} \right) = 774.6 \times \frac{2}{3} \approx 516.4 \text{ units}$$ **Step 6 — Minimum Annual Cost:** $$TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 \times 600 \times 1000 \times 3 \times \frac{2}{3}} = \sqrt{2{,}400{,}000} \approx 1549 \text{ ₹/year}$$ **Verification by Components:** - Setup cost $= 600 \times 1000 / 774.6 \approx 774.6$ ₹ - Holding cost $= 3 \times 516.4 / 2 \approx 774.6$ ₹ - Sum $\approx 1549$ ₹ ✓ *(7 marks: 1.5 each for parts (i)–(iv), 1 for part (v) with verification)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):** > > 1. **Using EOQ instead of EPQ when $p$ is given finite** — Examiner deducts 2–3 marks outright. > 2. **Forgetting the factor $(1 - d/p)$** in peak inventory formula $I_{\max}$. > 3. **Not verifying that the storage constraint is actually binding** — if $\sum a_i EOQ_i \leq W$, then $\lambda = 0$ and you must state that the unconstrained EOQ is optimal. > 4. **Mishandling units** — $D$ in units/year, $H$ in ₹/unit/year, $A$ in ₹/order. Mixing units is a 1-mark deduction. > 5. **In Lagrange iterations**: not showing *at least 2 trials* with intermediate values. Examiner expects visible iteration work. > 6. **Conflating echelon and installation stock** — a classic 2-mark trap in multi-echelon questions. > 7. **Forgetting KKT condition** $\lambda \geq 0$ (multiplier must be non-negative in a minimization with a $\leq$ constraint). > 8. **Skipping the second-order sufficiency check** $\partial^{2} TC/\partial Q^{2} > 0$. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3:** > > ✅ Multi-item model exists *because* of shared constraints (space, capital, labor). > ✅ Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$ acts as a **shadow price** for the binding constraint. > ✅ When constraint is non-binding, $\lambda = 0$ and classical EOQs apply. > ✅ EPQ peak inventory is **always** less than EOQ peak because $d > 0$ during production. > ✅ EPQ optimal lot size is **larger** than EOQ by a factor $1/\sqrt{1 - d/p}$. > ✅ EPQ assumes $p > d$ strictly; otherwise the model is infeasible (production can't meet demand). > ✅ Minimum cost under EPQ is *lower* than under EOQ (because holding cost is lower). > ✅ Multi-echelon decomposition (Clark-Scarf) converts a coupled problem into $n$ independent subproblems. > ✅ Echelon stock $EI_i = II_i + II_{i-1} + \cdots + II_1$ — always cumulative. > ✅ Optimal echelon stock **decreases** upstream (toward manufacturer). > ✅ In serial multi-echelon, demand information flows *up*, material flows *down*. > ✅ Pipeline stock = in-transit stock = $D \cdot L$ where $L$ is lead time. > ✅ Reorder point in multi-echelon: $s_i = D \cdot L_i + \text{safety stock}_i$. > ✅ Numerical EPQ problems: always compute $1 - d/p$ *first* as a separate step. > ✅ Lagrange iterations must be shown explicitly in the answer sheet — silent skipping loses marks.

topics.title: Solutions for Safety Inventory Models in Continuous Review and Periodic Review Cases of Single and Multi-items with Probabilistic Demand Included in Multi-Echelon Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET502 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solutions for Safety Inventory Models in Continuous Review and Periodic Review Cases of Single and Multi-items with Probabilistic Demand Included in Multi-Echelon Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Safety Inventory?

**Safety Inventory** (also called *safety stock* or *buffer stock*) is the extra quantity of inventory held beyond the expected demand during lead time, designed to protect a supply chain against the **uncertainty** inherent in demand and supply. When demand is deterministic and lead time is fixed, theoretically no safety stock is required. However, in real KTU-level supply chain problems, both demand and lead time exhibit **stochastic (probabilistic)** behavior, making safety inventory a non-negotiable buffer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Safety inventory is the quantity of stock maintained to absorb demand and supply variability during the replenishment lead time so that a pre-specified customer service level is achieved. It is the difference between the **Reorder Point (r)** and the **Expected Demand during Lead Time (EDLT)**.

### 1.2 The Two Classical Review Systems

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly identifies two paradigms for managing safety stock:

**A) Continuous Review System — (Q, r) Model**
* Inventory position is monitored **continuously** (every transaction).
* When inventory position falls to the **reorder point (r)**, a fixed order quantity **Q** is placed.
* Safety stock is the buffer added to expected lead-time demand: $SS = r - \mu_{DLT}$.

**B) Periodic Review System — (R, S) or (P, T) Model**
* Inventory position is reviewed periodically at fixed intervals of length **R**.
* An order is placed to raise the inventory position up to the **order-up-to level S**.
* Safety stock is the buffer added to expected demand over (Review Period + Lead Time): $SS = S - \mu_{D(R+L)}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine you are cooking dinner for guests arriving in 30 minutes. You have already marinated enough chicken for **6 people** (expected demand). However, since friends sometimes bring extra friends, you **additionally** keep 2 extra pieces of chicken marinated. Those 2 extra pieces are your **safety stock**. The "reorder point" in this analogy is the moment you start cooking (your lead time). For a *continuous review* household, you keep peeking into the fridge; for a *periodic review* household, you check the fridge only once every hour.

### 1.3 Multi-Item & Multi-Echelon Context

* **Multi-item:** Safety stock decisions across SKUs are interdependent when a **budget constraint**, **shared warehouse space**, or **joint service-level objective** exists.
* **Multi-echelon:** Safety stock decisions are made at multiple stages — supplier → central warehouse → regional warehouse → retailer. The optimal placement and sizing of safety stock across echelons becomes a network-wide optimization problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metrics used in this Module:**
> * **Cycle Service Level (CSL)** — Type-1 Service Level: probability of no stockout per replenishment cycle. Unit: **%**.
> * **Fill Rate (β)** — Type-2 Service Level: fraction of demand met from stock. Unit: **%**.
> * **Lead Time (L)** — time between order placement and receipt. Unit: **days/weeks**.
> * **Reorder Point (r)** — inventory position that triggers replenishment. Unit: **units**.
> * **Order-Up-To Level (S)** — target inventory position in periodic review. Unit: **units**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Continuous Review (Q, r) Model with Probabilistic Demand

In the classical (Q, r) model under stochastic demand $D$ during lead time $L$, the **Inventory Position (IP)** is monitored every period. A replenishment order of size $Q$ is triggered when $IP \leq r$.

The demand during lead time is itself a **random variable** $DLT$ (Demand during Lead Time). To protect against stockouts, we set the reorder point as:

$$r = \mu_{DLT} + SS$$

**Key Theoretical Logic:**
* **Step 1:** Identify that lead-time demand $DLT$ is a sum of $L$ independent daily demands.
* **Step 2:** Compute mean and variance of $DLT$.
* **Step 3:** Choose service level → find $z$-value from standard normal table.
* **Step 4:** Safety Stock = $z \times \sigma_{DLT}$.
* **Step 5:** $r = \mu_{DLT} + z \cdot \sigma_{DLT}$.

**Why?** Because the reorder point must be high enough so that, with probability $(1-\alpha)$, the actual demand $DLT$ does not exceed $r$, where $\alpha$ is the allowable stockout probability.

### 2.2 Periodic Review (R, S) Model with Probabilistic Demand

In the periodic review system, the inventory is checked every $R$ units of time, and an order is placed to bring the inventory position up to $S$. Demand occurs over the **protection interval** $(R + L)$:

$$S = \mu_{D(R+L)} + SS$$

**Key Theoretical Logic:**
* **Step 1:** Determine the protection interval: it spans the review period **plus** the lead time.
* **Step 2:** Compute mean and standard deviation of demand over $(R + L)$.
