<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*"Define the Bullwhip Effect. List any TWO major causes of the Bullwhip Effect in a supply chain."*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution)

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
The Bullwhip Effect is the phenomenon in which the variability of orders placed by upstream supply chain members is greater than the variability of sales to end customers. It is measured by the ratio $\dfrac{\operatorname{Var}(Q)}{\operatorname{Var}(D)} \geq 1$.

**Two Major Causes (1.5 Marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Demand Signal Processing** — Each stage uses past orders to forecast, amplifying small demand changes.
2. **Order Batching** — Periodic, large-quantity orders (EOQ) create saw-tooth patterns upstream.

*(Alternative accepted causes: Price Fluctuations, Shortage Gaming.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"What is Risk Pooling? State the Square-Root Law of inventory."*

#### Model Answer

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Risk Pooling is the strategic consolidation of inventory from multiple locations into a centralized facility so that demand variability is averaged out, reducing the total safety stock required.

**Square-Root Law (1.5 Marks):**
When $n$ independent demand sources are pooled, the consolidated safety stock reduces by a factor of $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$ compared to holding decentralized inventory:

$$\dfrac{SS_{\text{pooled}}}{SS_{\text{decentralized}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice between two questions (A or B) per module is permitted.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Choice A]**

> *A retail chain has 4 regional warehouses. Each warehouse serves an independent demand zone with $\mu = 200$ units/day, $\sigma = 50$ units/day, and lead time $L = 9$ days. The firm uses a service factor $z = 2.05$ (98% service level).*
>
> **(a)** Calculate the total safety stock under decentralized operation and the total safety stock if all four warehouses are consolidated into one central warehouse. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain FOUR major causes of the Bullwhip Effect and suggest ONE specific strategy for each cause to mitigate it. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Safety Stock Computation (7 Marks)

**Decentralized Case (3.5 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_{\text{decentralized}} \;&=\; n \cdot z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{L} \\[4pt]
&=\; 4 \times 2.05 \times 50 \times \sqrt{9} \\[4pt]
&=\; 4 \times 2.05 \times 50 \times 3 \\[4pt]
&=\; 1230 \;\text{units}
\end{aligned}$$

**Centralized Case (3.5 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_{\text{pooled}} \;&=\; z \cdot \sqrt{n} \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{L} \\[4pt]
&=\; 2.05 \times \sqrt{4} \times 50 \times 3 \\[4pt]
&=\; 2.05 \times 2 \times 50 \times 3 \\[4pt]
&=\; 615 \;\text{units}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Pooling reduces safety stock from 1230 units to 615 units — a saving of **615 units (50%)**, consistent with the $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{4}} = 0.5$ factor.

---

### Part (b) — Four Causes and Their Mitigations (7 Marks)

| # | Cause of Bullwhip Effect (Marks) | Specific Mitigation Strategy (Marks) |
|---|-----------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Demand Signal Processing** (≈1.5 M) | Adopt **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting & Replenishment)** and share POS data with upstream partners (≈0.25 M) |
| 2 | **Order Batching / EOQ** (≈1.5 M) | Switch to **continuous replenishment, VMI (Vendor Managed Inventory)**, or smaller, more frequent orders (≈0.25 M) |
| 3 | **Price Fluctuations / Forward Buying** (≈1.5 M) | Adopt **EDLP (Every Day Low Pricing)** instead of frequent trade promotions (≈0.25 M) |
| 4 | **Shortage Gaming / Rationing** (≈1.5 M) | Allocate scarce supply based on **past sales records** rather than on inflated orders (≈0.25 M) |

**Concluding Sentence (Bonus):** Combining these mitigations with **Risk Pooling** can lower total supply-chain inventory cost by up to 50–70%.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Choice B]**

> *(a) Define the Bullwhip Effect and derive the condition under which order variance exceeds demand variance, using the variance-additivity identity. (7 marks)*
>
> *(b) A firm operates 16 independent retail outlets. Each outlet has demand mean $\mu = 80$ units/day and standard deviation $\sigma = 20$ units/day. Lead time $L = 16$ days, $z = 2.33$. Compute the safety stock with and without pooling, and comment on the practical implication. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Conceptual Derivation (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Bullwhip Effect is the systematic amplification of demand variability as we move upstream in the supply chain, quantified by the Bullwhip Ratio $BR = \dfrac{\operatorname{Var}(Q)}{\operatorname{Var}(D)} \geq 1$, where $Q$ is the order quantity and $D$ is the end-customer demand.

**Derivation (5 Marks):**
Using the order-up-to policy $Q_t = \hat{D}_t^{L+p} + z\hat{\sigma}_D^{L+p}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}(Q_t) \;&=\; \operatorname{Var}\!\left(\hat{D}_t^{L+p}\right) \;+\; z^{2}\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\hat{\sigma}_D^{L+p}\right) \;+\; 2z\,\operatorname{Cov}\!\left(\hat{D}_t^{L+p},\, \hat{\sigma}_D^{L+p}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Since:
- $\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\hat{D}_t^{L+p}\right) \geq 0$,
- $z^{2}\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\hat{\sigma}_D^{L+p}\right) \geq 0$,
- $2z\,\operatorname{Cov}(\ldots) \geq 0$ (because the forecast and its standard error are positively correlated — higher forecasts imply higher perceived risk),

we conclude $\operatorname{Var}(Q_t) \geq \operatorname{Var}(D)$, which mathematically proves the bullwhip condition.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Without Pooling (3.5 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_{\text{decentralized}} \;&=\; n \cdot z \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{L} \\[4pt]
&=\; 16 \times 2.33 \times 20 \times \sqrt{16} \\[4pt]
&=\; 16 \times 2.33 \times 20 \times 4 \\[4pt]
&=\; 2982.4 \;\text{units}
\end{aligned}$$

**With Pooling (3.5 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_{\text{pooled}} \;&=\; z \cdot \sqrt{n} \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{L} \\[4pt]
&=\; 2.33 \times \sqrt{16} \times 20 \times 4 \\[4pt]
&=\; 2.33 \times 4 \times 80 \\[4pt]
&=\; 745.6 \;\text{units}
\end{aligned}$$

**Practical Implication (within 3.5 M):**
- Savings: $2982.4 - 745.6 = 2236.8$ units (a **75% reduction**).
- Theoretically, $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{16}} = 0.25$, confirming 75% reduction.
- **Implication:** Consolidating 16 outlets into one central warehouse enables the firm to maintain the same service level with one-quarter the inventory, freeing working capital and reducing warehousing overheads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take $\sqrt{L}$:** Students frequently write $L$ instead of $\sqrt{L}$ in safety stock formulas. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing centralized vs decentralized formulas:** The pooled formula uses $\sqrt{n}$ — *not* $n$ — as the multiplier. Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Omitting the demand-independence assumption:** Without independence, the variance of the sum is **not** $n\sigma^{2}$. Always state this assumption explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing Bullwhip Ratio with Coefficient of Variation:** $BR = \dfrac{\operatorname{Var}(Q)}{\operatorname{Var}(D)}$ while $c_v = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu}$ — these are *different* metrics.
> 5. **Listing causes without mitigations:** In 7-mark bullwhip questions, you must pair each cause with a *specific* remedy (VMI, EDLP, CPFR, etc.). Generic answers lose 2–3 marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Bullwhip Effect** = variance amplification as we move upstream in a supply chain.
- **Bullwhip Ratio** $BR = \dfrac{\operatorname{Var}(Q)}{\operatorname{Var}(D)} \geq 1$.
- **Four Causes — Mnemonic "DBPR":** Demand signal processing, Batching, Price fluctuation, Rationing.
- **Mitigations — "ICS-FAR":** Information sharing, Continuous replenishment/VMI, Stabilize pricing (EDLP), Fair allocation, Accurate forecasting, Risk pooling.
- **Risk Pooling** = consolidating inventory across multiple locations or periods.
- **Square-Root Law:** Pooled safety stock = $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$ of decentralized safety stock.
- **Pooled Std Dev:** $\sigma_{\text{pooled}} = \sqrt{n}\,\sigma$ (for i.i.d. demands).
- **Assumption:** Demand across locations must be **independent** (or negatively correlated) for pooling to work; positively correlated demands *destroy* the pooling benefit.
- **Service Level:** Pooling does *not* change $z$ — service level is preserved while inventory drops.
- **Practical Cutoff:** Pooling benefit diminishes after ~$n = 10$–$15$ locations due to transportation cost and lead-time penalty.
- **Trade-Off:** Risk pooling lowers inventory cost but increases transportation cost and *transportation lead time* — a classic inventory-vs-transportation trade-off in SCM design.
- **Real-World Examples:** Amazon FBA central warehouses, McDonald's centralized food prep, Zara's centralized distribution center in Spain.
- **Connection to Module 3:** Bullwhip causes inventory distortion; Risk Pooling (along with VMI and CPFR) is the textbook answer to mitigate it.

---

**END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — Bullwhip Effect and Risk Pooling**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education Aligned*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Transportation Networks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET502 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Understanding Transportation Networks

# 🟦 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transportation Network (Definition):**
> A **Transportation Network** is a system of interconnected nodes (facilities such as factories, warehouses, distribution centers, and customer locations) and links (routes, lanes, and modes of transportation) used to move products, information, and resources from origins to destinations in a supply chain. The network defines *where* facilities are located, *how* inventory is stored, and *how* product flow is routed to meet customer demand at the lowest total landed cost.

The transportation network is one of the **strategic pillars of supply chain design** because it directly affects:
- **Customer service level** (lead time, on-time delivery, product availability)
- **Total logistics cost** (transportation + inventory + facility + handling)
- **Sustainability footprint** (fuel, emissions, consolidation efficiency)
- **Operational flexibility** (resilience to demand or supply shocks)

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "How the Network is Like a Highway System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Kerala State Road Network"**
>
> Imagine Kerala's road map. Big cities like **Kochi, Trivandrum, and Kozhikode** are the **Hubs** (think of them as DC's). Small towns are **spoke nodes** (think of them as retailers/cross-docks). Two ways the government could connect them:
>
> 1. **Point-to-Point (Direct Shipping):** Build a separate road from *every* small town to *every* other small town. Expensive! Many small roads with low traffic each.
> 2. **Hub-and-Spoke (Consolidated Network):** Build good roads from small towns into the big hubs, and then big highways between hubs. Fewer long routes, fully loaded trucks, much cheaper per unit.
>
> A **Transportation Network** in supply chain is exactly the same choice — it asks: *Should we ship direct from factory to every customer, or consolidate and route through hubs?*

---

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Terminology (Highlighted)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Node** | Any facility in the network (plant, warehouse, DC, customer) |
| **Link** | A transportation lane between two nodes |
| **Lane** | A specific origin–destination pair shipped on a regular schedule |
| **Service Level** | Often measured as fill rate / on-time delivery / lead time |
| **Landed Cost** | Total cost = Transportation + Inventory + Warehousing + Handling |
| **SKU** | Stock Keeping Unit — unit of inventory |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** Examiners *love* asking the difference between **direct shipping, hub-and-spoke, and consolidated networks** — keep this triangle clear in your head.

---

## 1.4 Why Transportation Networks Matter (KTU 2024 CO Mapping)

| Course Outcome | How this topic satisfies it |
|---|---|
| **CO4** – Design transportation networks | Direct mapping — node-link trade-offs |
| **CO3** – Optimize logistics cost | Landed-cost balancing |
| **CO5** – Evaluate network alternatives | Hub-vs-direct vs hybrid |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🟩 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Building Blocks of a Transportation Network

A transportation network has **three structural decisions** (Chopra & Meindl framework, which is the KTU reference base):

1. **Facility location decision** — *Where* to place plants, warehouses, DCs
2. **Inventory positioning decision** — *What* to stock and at *which* node
3. **Transportation decision** — *How* to route the flow between nodes (the topic of this module)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **transportation decision** is what converts a static map of nodes into a *living, working* supply chain. The same set of facilities can support very different transportation networks.

---

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Network Designs

### (a) Direct Shipping Network

- Each supplier/plant ships **directly to each customer/DC** independently.
- **No consolidation hub.**
- Best when: customers are large, shipments are full truckload (FTL), or service is highly time-critical.

### (b) Hub-and-Spoke (Consolidated) Network

- All smaller flows go to a **central or regional hub**, where shipments are **consolidated, sorted, and forwarded** to their final destination.
- Best when: customer orders are small, transportation cost is high, inventory needs to be pooled.
- Reduces transportation cost by **increasing consolidation density**.

### (c) Hybrid / Multi-Tiered Network

- Mixes both — regional DCs use direct shipping, smaller customers routed through hubs.
- Most **real-world networks** fall here.

> [!TIP]
> A useful memory line: **"Direct = speed, Hub = cost, Hybrid = balance."**

---

## 2.3 Decision Trade-off: Cost of Transportation vs. Cost of Inventory

There is a fundamental **risk-pooling trade-off** when deciding the network:

- **More centralization** → Less safety stock, lower inventory cost, but **higher inbound/outbound transportation cost** (longer distances, more handling).
- **More decentralization** → Higher safety stock (inventory scattered at many nodes), higher inventory cost, but **shorter, cheaper, faster shipments**.

This is captured by the classic Chopra formula:

$$
T_{total} = T_{trans} + I_{inv} + F_{fac} + H_{handling}
$$

Where:
- $T_{trans}$ = total transportation cost
- $I_{inv}$ = total inventory cost
- $F_{fac}$ = facility cost (rent, depreciation, labour)
- $H_{handling}$ = material handling cost

> The **optimal transportation network** *minimizes* this total cost subject to a minimum acceptable service level.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Landed Cost | $T = T_t + I + F + H$ | All in ₹ / period | Compare two network designs |
| 2 | Number of links in a fully connected direct network | $L = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $n$ = number of nodes | Direct shipping topology |
| 3 | Hub-and-spoke link count | $L = n + \dfrac{k(k-1)}{2}$ | $n$ = spoke nodes, $k$ = hubs | Hub-and-spoke topology |
| 4 | Consolidation cost saving | $S = (c_{LTL} - c_{FTL}) \cdot V$ | $c_{LTL}$, $c_{FTL}$ = ₹/unit, $V$ = consolidated volume | Hub design |
| 5 | Effective distance with hub routing | $D_{eff} = d(O,H) + d(H,D)$ | O = origin, H = hub, D = destination | Routing through a hub |
| 6 | Truck utilization | $U = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{capacity}}$ | Fraction (0–1) | Consolidation gain |
| 7 | Break-even distance for direct vs hub | $d^{*} = \dfrac{F}{\Delta c \cdot V}$ | $F$ = fixed handling cost, $\Delta c$ = per-km cost diff | Network choice |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing the **number of links formula** in the exam, do **not** write $\vert n \vert$ or $\vert V \vert$. Use plain $n$ and $V$ to avoid markdown pipe conflicts and signal a well-written formula.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce giants** (Amazon, Flipkart) use hub-and-spoke with regional sort centers.
- **Cold chain logistics** (Kerala dairy, fish exports) uses hub-and-spoke because temperature-controlled vehicles must be *fully loaded* to justify cost.
- **Automotive OEMs** use direct shipping (just-in-time) to deliver large volumes to assembly plants.
- **FMCG companies** (HUL, ITC) use hybrid networks — large distributors direct, smaller retailers via hub.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🟧 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Lanes in a Direct vs. Hub-and-Spoke Network

This derivation is a **favourite KTU 14-mark sub-part** because it tests *structured mathematical reasoning* and a *practical conclusion*.

### Step 1 — Define the direct network

A direct, fully connected network with $n$ nodes allows a *distinct* transportation lane between every pair of nodes.

The number of unordered pairs is the combination:

$$
L_{direct} = \binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Reasoning:** Each of the $n$ nodes can connect to $(n-1)$ others. But pairing A→B is the same lane as B→A, so we divide by 2.

### Step 2 — Define the hub-and-spoke network

Let the network have:
- $n$ spoke nodes (customers / small facilities)
- $k$ hub nodes (regional consolidation centers)

Each spoke connects to **one or more hubs** (assume 1 hub for simplicity here):

$$
L_{spoke\text{-}to\text{-}hub} = n \cdot k
$$

The $k$ hubs are fully connected to each other:

$$
L_{hub\text{-}hub} = \dfrac{k(k-1)}{2}
$$

Total hub-and-spoke lanes:

$$
L_{hub} = n \cdot k + \dfrac{k(k-1)}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare for $n = 10$ customers, $k = 1$ hub

Direct network:

$$
L_{direct} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 9}{2} = 45 \text{ lanes}
$$

Hub-and-spoke (1 hub):

$$
L_{hub} = 10 \cdot 1 + \dfrac{1 \cdot 0}{2} = 10 \text{ lanes}
$$

> **Analytical insight:** The hub-and-spoke network has only **22%** of the lanes of the direct network, but each hub lane now carries *consolidated* volume — leading to FTL loads and lower per-unit transportation cost.

### Step 4 — Generalize the comparison

Compute the **ratio**:

$$
R = \dfrac{L_{hub}}{L_{direct}} = \dfrac{nk + \dfrac{k(k-1)}{2}}{\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}}
$$

For $k = 1$:

$$
R = \dfrac{n}{\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}} = \dfrac{2}{n-1}
$$

As $n \to \infty$, $R \to 0$, meaning **larger networks benefit more from consolidation** — a key real-world insight.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Break-Even Distance for Direct vs. Hub

A supplier can either ship direct to customer, or route through a hub that adds fixed handling cost $F$ per shipment but saves per-km cost on the long leg.

### Cost of direct shipping

$$
C_{direct} = c_d \cdot D
$$

Where $c_d$ = per-km direct shipping cost, $D$ = distance.

### Cost of hub shipping

$$
C_{hub} = F + c_h \cdot D
$$

Where $F$ = fixed handling cost at hub, $c_h$ = per-km cost from hub (lower because volume is consolidated).

### Equate to find break-even distance $D^*$

$$
F + c_h D^{*} = c_d D^{*}
$$

$$
F = (c_d - c_h) D^{*}
$$

$$
D^{*} = \dfrac{F}{c_d - c_h}
$$

> **Interpretation:** For distances shorter than $D^*$, **direct shipping is cheaper** (small shipments don't justify the handling fee). For distances longer than $D^*$, **hub routing wins** because the per-km savings on consolidation outweigh the fixed handling cost.

### Numerical Example

Suppose $F = 5000$ ₹/shipment, $c_d = 100$ ₹/km, $c_h = 70$ ₹/km.

$$
D^{*} = \dfrac{5000}{100 - 70} = \dfrac{5000}{30} \approx 166.67 \text{ km}
$$

So **within Kerala**, for any customer farther than **~167 km** from the plant, hub-and-spoke through a regional DC becomes more cost-effective.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose What

| Network Type | Order Size | Distance | Inventory Pooling | Service Need | Cost Driver |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Shipping** | Large (FTL) | Any | Not needed | Urgent / fragile | Time, not cost |
| **Hub-and-Spoke** | Small (LTL) | Long | Highly pooled | Cost-driven | Cost, not time |
| **Hybrid** | Mixed | Mixed | Partial pooling | Mixed | Balanced |

> [!TIP]
> **For the KTU exam:** When asked "Design a transportation network for an XYZ firm," always start by **classifying the firm** into the row above. Then justify your network choice using the **break-even distance** or **total landed cost** formula.

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Network Comparison (Numerical)

A firm has $n = 8$ customers and is evaluating direct vs. a single hub. Annual data:

| Item | Direct Network | Hub Network |
|---|---|---|
| Number of lanes | 28 | 8 |
| Average volume/lane | 1,000 units | 3,500 units |
| Cost per unit (₹) | 50 | 35 |
| Fixed hub cost (₹/yr) | 0 | 2,00,000 |
| Inventory cost (₹/yr) | 8,00,000 | 4,50,000 |

### Direct network total cost

$$
T_{direct} = 28 \times 1000 \times 50 + 0 + 8,00,000 = 14,00,000 + 8,00,000 = 22,00,000 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

### Hub network total cost

$$
T_{hub} = 8 \times 3500 \times 35 + 2,00,000 + 4,50,000 = 9,80,000 + 2,00,000 + 4,50,000 = 16,30,000 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

### Decision

$$
T_{hub} < T_{direct} \Rightarrow \text{Choose Hub-and-Spoke, saving } 5,70,000 \text{ ₹/yr (26\% reduction)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Hub wins due to **consolidation + inventory pooling**, even after paying fixed hub cost.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🟪 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Direct Shipping Network (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plant A1] -- Truck FTL --> B[Customer B1]
    A -- Truck FTL --> C[Customer B2]
    A -- Truck FTL --> D[Customer B3]
    A2[Plant A2] -- Truck FTL --> E[Customer B4]
    A2 -- Truck FTL --> F[Customer B5]
    A2 -- Truck FTL --> G[Customer B6]

    classDef plant fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2545;
    classDef cust fill:#ffe9c2,stroke:#a35d00,color:#5d2f00;
    class A,A2 plant;
    class B,C,D,E,F,G cust;
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each plant ships independently to each customer. **No intermediate node**. The number of lanes = 6 in this example. Each truck carries its **own customer's volume only** — poor consolidation.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Hub-and-Spoke Network (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Plant P1] -- Inflow --> H1[Hub Kochi]
    A2[Plant P2] -- Inflow --> H1
    A3[Plant P3] -- Inflow --> H1
    H1 -- Outflow FTL --> C1[Customer C1]
    H1 -- Outflow FTL --> C2[Customer C2]
    H1 -- Outflow FTL --> C3[Customer C3]
    H1 -- Outflow FTL --> C4[Customer C4]
    H1 -- Outflow FTL --> C5[Customer C5]
    H1 -- Outflow FTL --> C6[Customer C6]

    classDef plant fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2545;
    classDef hub fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1d6b1d,color:#0a3d0a;
    classDef cust fill:#ffe9c2,stroke:#a35d00,color:#5d2f00;
    class A1,A2,A3 plant;
    class H1 hub;
    class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6 cust;
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Plants ship to **one hub** in many small loads; the hub **consolidates, sorts, and re-loads** FTL trucks to each customer. Each outbound truck is full → **per-unit cost drops** even though each shipment visits a hub first.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Hub-and-Spoke with Multiple Hubs (Multi-Tiered)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plant P1] --> H1[Hub North]
    P2[Plant P2] --> H2[Hub South]
    H1 <--> H2
    H1 --> C1[Customer C1]
    H1 --> C2[Customer C2]
    H1 --> C3[Customer C3]
    H2 --> C4[Customer C4]
    H2 --> C5[Customer C5]
    H2 --> C6[Customer C6]

    classDef plant fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2545;
    classDef hub fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1d6b1d,color:#0a3d0a;
    classDef cust fill:#ffe9c2,stroke:#a35d00,color:#5d2f00;
    class P1,P2 plant;
    class H1,H2 hub;
    class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6 cust;
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a **multi-hub hub-and-spoke** — the two hubs are *inter-connected*, so cross-region shipments still consolidate. This is what Amazon and Flipkart run at scale.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Network Decision Logic Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Customer Order Profile] --> B{Order Size per Customer}
    B -- Large FTL --> C{Distance Long}
    B -- Small LTL --> D[Hub-and-Spoke]
    C -- Yes --> E[Direct Shipping Justified]
    C -- No --> F[Direct Shipping Sufficient]
    D --> G{Consolidation Gain}
    G -- High --> H[Choose Hub]
    G -- Low --> I[Re-evaluate: hybrid]
    E --> J[Final Network: Direct]
    F --> J
    H --> K[Final Network: Hub]
    I --> L[Final Network: Hybrid]

    classDef start fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#1f4e79;
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#a88700;
    classDef output fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1d6b1d;
    class A start;
    class B,C,G decision;
    class J,K,L output;
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **decision logic** the student should learn to draw in the exam whenever a "design the network" question appears.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🟥 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between direct shipping and hub-and-spoke transportation networks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Shipping | Hub-and-Spoke |
|---|---|---|
| Number of lanes | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $nk + \dfrac{k(k-1)}{2}$ |
| Consolidation | None | High — volume is pooled at hub |
| Per-unit cost | Higher (LTL loads) | Lower (FTL loads) |
| Inventory | Distributed at each node | Pooled at hub |
| Lead time | Shorter, direct | Slightly longer (extra hop) |
| Best for | Large orders, time-sensitive | Small orders, cost-sensitive |

> Direct shipping ships product *straight* from plant to customer without intermediate consolidation, while hub-and-spoke consolidates flows at a central hub and re-dispatches in full loads. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by the term "transportation lane" in supply chain networks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **transportation lane** is a **specific origin–destination pair** (e.g., Kochi plant → Bangalore DC) along which shipments move on a recurring schedule. A network is essentially a **set of lanes** connecting nodes. Lanes are characterized by:
- Mode of transport (road, rail, air, sea)
- Frequency (daily, weekly)
- Volume per shipment
- Cost per unit shipped

Lanes are the **decision variables** of the transportation network design problem — *which lanes to operate, with what mode, frequency, and carrier*. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **one** of the two questions.

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A firm supplies product from a single plant to 6 customer locations. The firm is evaluating two network designs:**
- **Option 1:** Direct shipping from plant to all 6 customers
- **Option 2:** A single hub where all shipments are consolidated and re-dispatched

**Annual data is given below:**

| Item | Direct | Hub |
|---|---|---|
| Number of lanes | 15 | 6 |
| Total annual volume | 1,80,000 units | 1,80,000 units |
| Cost per unit shipped | ₹ 60 | ₹ 42 |
| Fixed hub operating cost | — | ₹ 4,00,000 |
| Annual inventory carrying cost | ₹ 9,00,000 | ₹ 5,50,000 |

**(a)** Compute the **total landed cost** for each option. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Which option should the firm choose? Justify using the **break-even logic** and discuss **two practical risks** of the chosen network. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute transportation cost**

Direct:

$$
T_t^{direct} = 1,80,000 \times 60 = 1,08,00,000 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

Hub:

$$
T_t^{hub} = 1,80,000 \times 42 = 75,60,000 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Add fixed cost and inventory cost**

$$
T_{direct} = 1,08,00,000 + 0 + 9,00,000 = 1,17,00,000 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

$$
T_{hub} = 75,60,000 + 4,00,000 + 5,50,000 = 85,10,000 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Savings from hub**

$$
\Delta T = 1,17,00,000 - 85,10,000 = 31,90,000 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

> Hub saves **₹31.9 lakh/year (~27% reduction)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Recommendation**

The firm should **choose the hub-and-spoke network** because it yields a 27% lower landed cost.

**Step 2 — Break-even logic explanation**

Using the break-even distance formula

$$
D^{*} = \dfrac{F}{c_d - c_h}
$$

Here, $F = ₹ 4,00,000$ (hub fixed cost); $c_d = ₹ 60$/unit, $c_h = ₹ 42$/unit, so $\Delta c = 18$. The break-even volume is

$$
V^{*} = \dfrac{F}{c_d - c_h} = \dfrac{4,00,000}{18} \approx 22,222 \text{ units/yr}
$$

Since actual volume **1,80,000 >> 22,222**, hub consolidation is **heavily justified**.

**Step 3 — Two practical risks of hub**

1. **Hub failure risk:** If the hub is disrupted (flood, fire, strike), the *entire* outbound network collapses. Mitigation: keep safety stock or use a backup cross-dock.
2. **Longer lead time & handling damage:** Each shipment now has an extra handling stage, increasing lead time by 12–24 hours and adding risk of in-transit damage.

**(7 marks total)**

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **three structural design decisions** that constitute transportation network design. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a company with $n = 12$ customers, calculate and compare the number of lanes required in (i) a fully connected direct network, and (ii) a hub-and-spoke network with $k = 2$ hubs. Comment on the operational implications. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The three structural design decisions (Chopra & Meindl framework) are:

1. **Facility location decision** — *Where* to place plants, warehouses, and distribution centers. Driven by customer proximity, labour cost, tax incentives, and land cost.
2. **Inventory positioning decision** — *What* SKU to stock and at *which* node. Pools safety stock at fewer nodes to reduce inventory cost (risk-pooling effect).
3. **Transportation decision** — *How* to move product between nodes, by which mode, on which lane, and with what frequency. Includes direct vs hub choice, mode selection, and carrier selection.

The three are **interdependent**: changing facility location changes inventory needs, which changes optimal transportation network. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Direct network with $n = 12$**

$$
L_{direct} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = \dfrac{12 \times 11}{2} = 66 \text{ lanes}
$$

**Step 2 — Hub-and-spoke with $k = 2$ hubs**

Spoke-to-hub lanes:

$$
L_{spoke\text{-}hub} = n \cdot k = 12 \times 2 = 24
$$

Inter-hub lanes:

$$
L_{hub\text{-}hub} = \dfrac{k(k-1)}{2} = \dfrac{2 \times 1}{2} = 1
$$

Total:

$$
L_{hub} = 24 + 1 = 25 \text{ lanes}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison**

$$
R = \dfrac{L_{hub}}{L_{direct}} = \dfrac{25}{66} \approx 0.379
$$

> Hub-and-spoke needs only **38% of the lanes** of the direct network.

**Step 4 — Operational implications (any 2 for full marks)**

1. **Lower operational complexity** — fewer carriers, contracts, and tracking systems to manage.
2. **Better truck utilization** — outbound trucks are full because hub consolidates.
3. **Higher dependency on hubs** — risk concentration; needs backup planning.
4. **Inventory pooling** — less safety stock needed at spoke level because inventory is centralized.

**(7 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Always show the formula AND the substitution** in part-(a) of any 14-mark question. Students often write only the final number — examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks for skipped steps.
> 2. **Don't forget the fixed hub cost** when computing landed cost. The single most common mistake is comparing only per-unit shipping cost, which over-states the hub's cost.
> 3. In **break-even questions**, clearly state the assumption (e.g., *assuming linear cost*) — examiners reward explicit assumptions.
> 4. **Diagram questions:** Draw the **decision logic flowchart**, not just a route map. Flowcharts score higher because they show *thinking*, not just topology.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔁 Rapid Revision Checklist — "Transportation Networks"**
>
> - [ ] Transportation network = **nodes + links**, choosing *how* to move product
> - [ ] Three structural decisions: **facility location, inventory positioning, transportation**
> - [ ] **Direct shipping** → high service, high cost, no consolidation
> - [ ] **Hub-and-spoke** → low cost, pooled inventory, longer lead time
> - [ ] **Hybrid** → most real-world networks, balances cost and service
> - [ ] Direct network lanes: $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$
> - [ ] Hub-and-spoke lanes: $nk + \dfrac{k(k-1)}{2}$
> - [ ] **Total landed cost** = Transportation + Inventory + Facility + Handling
> - [ ] **Break-even distance** $D^{*} = \dfrac{F}{c_d - c_h}$ decides direct vs hub
> - [ ] Consolidation reduces per-unit cost by **filling trucks** (LTL → FTL)
> - [ ] **Inventory pooling** at hubs reduces safety stock via risk-pooling
> - [ ] Larger $n$ → hub advantage grows (ratio $\to \dfrac{2}{n-1}$ for $k=1$)
> - [ ] Always show both **the formula and the substitution** in 14-mark answers
> - [ ] Mention **practical risks** of the chosen network (hub failure, lead time) to score full marks

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trade-offs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET502 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trade-offs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Supply Chain Management (SCM)**, a **trade-off** is a managerial decision-making phenomenon in which improving one supply chain objective (e.g., responsiveness, service level, variety) inherently worsens another supply chain objective (e.g., cost, efficiency, inventory level). Mathematically, if we represent supply chain performance as a vector

$$
P = \big[ P_1, P_2, P_3, \dots, P_n \big]
$$

then a trade-off exists between any two components $P_i$ and $P_j$ when:

$$
\frac{\partial P_i}{\partial x_k} \cdot \frac{\partial P_j}{\partial x_k} < 0
$$

where $x_k$ is a decision variable (e.g., lot size, safety stock, lead time, transportation mode). This negative partial derivative relationship is the formal signature of a supply chain trade-off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Chopra & Meindl Framework):**  
> *"A trade-off is a balancing act between two or more competing supply chain drivers. The goal of supply chain design is to consciously identify, quantify, and manage these trade-offs so that the supply chain is appropriately matched to the product and customer needs."*

The most foundational trade-off in SCM is the **Responsiveness vs. Efficiency Trade-off**, which is the master trade-off from which all other trade-offs cascade.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are planning a **road trip from Kochi to Delhi**. You have two choices:

- **Option A (Responsive):** Fly. Reach in 3 hours but pay ₹15,000.
- **Option B (Efficient):** Drive. Takes 3 days but costs only ₹3,000 in fuel.

You **cannot have both** the speed of an airplane and the cost-effectiveness of a car. **This is a trade-off.** Choosing one means sacrificing the other.

Now apply the same logic to a **supply chain**:
- A company can keep **huge inventory** of finished goods (high responsiveness to customer demand) but will incur **massive holding costs** (low efficiency).
- Alternatively, it can keep **minimal inventory** (high efficiency) but customers may face **stockouts** (low responsiveness).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:**  
> Trade-offs are the **"laws of physics"** of supply chains. Just as Newton's law states that energy can be transformed but not destroyed, supply chain objectives can be reallocated but not simultaneously maximized. The art of supply chain design is to find the **optimal point of balance**.

## 1.3 Categories of Trade-offs in KTU Module 4

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically highlights the following trade-off categories:

1. **Inventory vs. Lead Time**
2. **Inventory vs. Transportation Cost**
3. **Transportation Cost vs. Lead Time**
4. **Lead Time vs. Product Variety**
5. **Product Variety vs. Inventory**
6. **Cost vs. Product Variety**
7. **Product Variety vs. Customer Experience**
8. **Cost vs. Customer Experience**
9. **Inventory vs. Customer Experience**
10. **Transportation Cost vs. Customer Experience**
11. **Lead Time vs. Customer Experience**

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** All eleven trade-offs listed above can be **derived from three fundamental drivers**: (1) Inventory, (2) Lead Time, and (3) Transportation Cost, each interacting with the two customer-facing drivers: **Product Variety** and **Customer Experience**. Memorize this **3 × 2 expansion grid** to answer any trade-off question in the KTU ESE.

## 1.4 The Two Strategic Extremes of Supply Chains

Every supply chain lies on a spectrum between two polar strategies:

| Extreme | Strategic Focus | Key Characteristics | Industry Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physically Efficient Supply Chain** | Low cost, high efficiency | Low inventory, slow transportation, minimal variety, long lead times | FMCG, commodity retail, Walmart |
| **Market-Responsive Supply Chain** | High service, agility | High inventory, fast transportation, high variety, short lead times | Fashion, electronics, Amazon Prime |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The choice between these two extremes depends on the **product's position on the innovative-imitative spectrum** and the **margins** involved. **Innovative products** (high margin, unpredictable demand) need responsive chains. **Imitative/functional products** (low margin, predictable demand) need efficient chains.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Trade-off: Responsiveness vs. Efficiency

The entire framework of supply chain trade-offs can be unified under one master concept. Every action taken in a supply chain either:

- **Increases responsiveness** (and decreases efficiency), OR
- **Increases efficiency** (and decreases responsiveness)

### 2.1.1 The Three Primary Supply Chain Drivers

The Chopra-Meindl framework identifies three primary drivers that affect responsiveness and efficiency:

**Driver 1: Inventory**
- **Mechanism:** Inventory acts as a buffer between supply and demand.
- **Effect on Responsiveness:** Higher inventory → Higher responsiveness (less stockouts, faster fulfillment).
- **Effect on Efficiency:** Higher inventory → Higher carrying cost → Lower efficiency.
- **Cost Implication:** Inventory Carrying Cost is typically **20% to 40%** of inventory value per year.

**Driver 2: Lead Time**
- **Mechanism:** Lead time is the time between order placement and order receipt.
- **Effect on Responsiveness:** Shorter lead time → Higher responsiveness.
- **Effect on Efficiency:** Shorter lead time requires expensive transportation modes (air vs. sea) → Lower efficiency.

**Driver 3: Transportation Cost**
- **Mechanism:** Transportation mode selection (air, road, rail, sea) creates trade-offs.
- **Effect on Responsiveness:** Fast transport (air) → High responsiveness.
- **Effect on Efficiency:** Fast transport → High cost → Low efficiency.

### 2.1.2 The Two Customer-Facing Drivers

**Driver 4: Product Variety (SKU count)**
- **Effect on Customer Experience:** More variety → Higher customer satisfaction.
- **Trade-off:** Variety forces higher inventory, longer lead times, and higher costs.

**Driver 5: Customer Experience (Service Level)**
- **Effect:** Defined by fill rate, on-time delivery, perfect-order metrics.
- **Trade-off:** Better service → Higher cost (more safety stock, faster transport).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Major Trade-off

### Trade-off 1: Inventory vs. Lead Time

- **Logic Step 1:** If a firm wants to reduce lead time, it must hold inventory at multiple decentralized locations close to customers.
- **Logic Step 2:** Decentralized inventory increases total system inventory (compared to one central warehouse).
- **Logic Step 3:** Therefore, **reducing lead time increases inventory cost**.
- **Decision Rule:** Use a quick-response (QR) supply chain only when the product has high margin and demand uncertainty.

### Trade-off 2: Inventory vs. Transportation Cost

- **Logic Step 1:** If a firm uses cheap, slow transportation (e.g., ocean shipping), the long transit time requires a **pipeline inventory** (in-transit stock) to meet demand.
- **Logic Step 2:** Pipeline inventory directly increases total inventory in the system.
- **Logic Step 3:** Therefore, **saving on transportation cost increases inventory cost**.
- **Decision Rule:** Use expensive transport (air) only for high-value, time-sensitive goods.

### Trade-off 3: Transportation Cost vs. Lead Time

- **Logic Step 1:** Air transport: ₹100/kg for 2-day delivery.
- **Logic Step 2:** Ocean transport: ₹10/kg for 30-day delivery.
- **Logic Step 3:** Lead time and transportation cost are inversely related.
- **Decision Rule:** For perishables (medicines, food), the trade-off favors air.

### Trade-off 4: Lead Time vs. Product Variety

- **Logic Step 1:** More product variety means the supplier has to set up production more frequently.
- **Logic Step 2:** Frequent setups increase the time to fulfill orders.
- **Logic Step 3:** Therefore, **increasing variety increases lead time** (unless buffered by inventory).

### Trade-off 5: Product Variety vs. Inventory

- **Logic Step 1:** Each SKU requires its own safety stock.
- **Logic Step 2:** With $N$ SKUs and identical demand variability, total safety stock scales as $\sqrt{N}$.
- **Logic Step 3:** Therefore, **increasing variety increases inventory cost**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables | Units | Trade-off Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}}$ | D=annual demand, S=order cost, H=holding cost/unit/yr | units | Balances ordering cost vs. holding cost |
| Total Relevant Cost at EOQ | $TC^* = \sqrt{2 D S H}$ | D, S, H | ₹ per year | Minimum cost achievable |
| Safety Stock Formula | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | z=service z-score, $\sigma_{LT}$=std dev of lead time demand | units | Higher service → more safety stock |
| Reorder Point | $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + SS$ | $\bar{d}$=avg daily demand, L=lead time | units | Triggers new order |
| Square Root Law for Variety | $SS_{N} = SS_1 \cdot \sqrt{N}$ | N=number of SKUs | units | More variety → more inventory |
| Transportation Cost per Unit | $TC_{unit} = \dfrac{R \cdot D_{ist}}{W_{eight}}$ | R=rate, Distance in km, Weight in kg | ₹/unit | Mode selection trade-off |
| Inventory Carrying Cost | $ICC = H \cdot \dfrac{Q}{2}$ | H, Q | ₹/year | Increases with Q |
| Annual Ordering Cost | $AOC = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S$ | D, Q, S | ₹/year | Decreases with Q |
| Fill Rate vs. Safety Stock (Type 1) | $FR = 1 - \dfrac{E[z_{\alpha}] \cdot \sigma_{LT}}{Q}$ | Q, $\sigma_{LT}$ | dimensionless | Service vs. cost |
| Break-Even Make vs. Buy | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_{c}}{C_{b} - V_{m}}$ | $F_c$=fixed cost, $C_b$=buy price, $V_m$=variable make cost | units | Strategic sourcing |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When answering numerical problems, always state the **trade-off interpretation** after computing the EOQ. Simply writing $Q^* = 100$ units is incomplete; you must say: *"The trade-off between ordering cost (decreasing in Q) and holding cost (increasing in Q) is optimized at Q = 100 units."*

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Kerala industries**, trade-off analysis is critical in:
- **Spice export supply chains** (Kochi port) — Trade-off between ocean shipping (cost) and air freight (speed) for cardamom and pepper.
- **Coir industry** — Trade-off between inventory holding and product variety in the global coir yarn market.
- **Marine fisheries** (Kerala coast) — Trade-off between ice storage (inventory) and faster transport to inland markets.
- **Cochin Shipyard supply chain** — Trade-off between local sourcing (efficiency) and imported components (variety).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental EOQ Trade-off (Ordering Cost vs. Holding Cost)

The classical economic order quantity (EOQ) is a perfect mathematical embodiment of a supply chain trade-off. We derive it below in full.

### Step 1: Define the Decision Variable and Assumptions

Let $Q$ be the order quantity (units per order). The relevant assumptions are:
- Annual demand $D$ is **constant and deterministic**.
- Order cost $S$ is **fixed per order** (independent of $Q$).
- Holding cost $H$ is **per unit per year** (linear in average inventory).
- No stockouts are permitted.
- Instantaneous replenishment (infinite supply rate).

### Step 2: Express Annual Ordering Cost ($AOC$)

The number of orders placed per year is $\dfrac{D}{Q}$. Each order costs $S$. Therefore:

$$
AOC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S
$$

The $AOC$ **decreases** as $Q$ increases (larger orders → fewer orders → less ordering cost).

### Step 3: Express Annual Holding Cost ($AHC$)

With instantaneous replenishment and constant demand, the inventory level declines linearly from $Q$ to $0$ over the order cycle. The **average inventory** is:

$$
\bar{I} = \frac{Q + 0}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}
$$

The annual holding cost is then:

$$
AHC(Q) = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H
$$

The $AHC$ **increases** as $Q$ increases (larger orders → more average inventory → more holding cost).

### Step 4: Express the Total Relevant Cost ($TC$)

$$
TC(Q) = AOC(Q) + AHC(Q) = \frac{D S}{Q} + \frac{H Q}{2}
$$

This is a convex function of $Q$, exhibiting the classic **U-shaped trade-off curve**.

### Step 5: Minimize $TC(Q)$ Using First-Order Condition

Take the first derivative with respect to $Q$ and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D S}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
$$

### Step 6: Solve Algebraically for $Q^*$

$$
\frac{H}{2} = \frac{D S}{Q^2}
$$

Multiply both sides by $Q^2$:

$$
\frac{H Q^2}{2} = D S
$$

Multiply both sides by $2$:

$$
H Q^2 = 2 D S
$$

Divide both sides by $H$:

$$
Q^2 = \frac{2 D S}{H}
$$

Take the positive square root (since $Q > 0$):

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}
$$

This is the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**.

### Step 7: Verify the Trade-off Optimality (Second-Order Condition)

$$
\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2 D S}{Q^3} > 0
$$

The second derivative is **positive**, confirming that $Q^*$ is indeed a **minimum** of total cost.

### Step 8: Substitute $Q^*$ Back into $TC(Q)$ to Find the Minimum Cost

$$
TC(Q^*) = \frac{D S}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \frac{H}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}
$$

First term:

$$
\frac{D S}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} = D S \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H}{2 D S}} = \sqrt{\frac{D^2 S^2 H}{2 D S}} = \sqrt{\frac{D S H}{2}}
$$

Second term:

$$
\frac{H}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} = \frac{H}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2 D S}}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{\sqrt{H} \cdot \sqrt{2 D S}}{2} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S H}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{D S H}{2}}
$$

Summing the two terms:

$$
TC^* = \sqrt{\frac{D S H}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{D S H}{2}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{D S H}{2}} = \sqrt{4 \cdot \frac{D S H}{2}} = \sqrt{2 D S H}
$$

Therefore, the minimum total relevant cost is:

$$
TC^* = \sqrt{2 D S H}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Trade-off Insight:** At the EOQ point $Q^*$, the **annual ordering cost equals the annual holding cost**. This is a beautiful manifestation of the trade-off optimality:
> 
> $$\frac{D S}{Q^*} = \frac{H Q^*}{2}$$
> 
> The ordering cost curve and the holding cost curve **intersect exactly at the optimum**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Square Root Law of Inventory (Variety Trade-off)

Suppose a firm introduces $N$ SKUs of a product, each with identical demand variability. Derive the relationship between variety and safety stock.

### Step 1: Base Safety Stock for 1 SKU

For a single SKU with standard deviation of lead time demand $\sigma_{LT}$, the safety stock for service level $z$ is:

$$
SS_1 = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}
$$

### Step 2: Demand Variability for $N$ SKUs

Assuming independence across SKUs, the total demand variability scales with the **square root of the number of SKUs** (a result from probability theory: the variance of the sum of independent random variables is the sum of variances, hence the standard deviation scales as $\sqrt{N}$):

$$
\sigma_{LT,N} = \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{N}
$$

### Step 3: Safety Stock for $N$ SKUs

$$
SS_N = z \cdot \sigma_{LT,N} = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{N} = SS_1 \cdot \sqrt{N}
$$

### Step 4: Trade-off Interpretation

The marginal cost of adding the $N$-th SKU to the safety stock is:

$$
\Delta SS_N = SS_1 \cdot (\sqrt{N} - \sqrt{N-1})
$$

which **decreases** as $N$ increases (the square root function has diminishing returns). This is why product variety is often "free" in the marginal sense once a baseline is set, but the absolute cost rises.

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Numerical Insight:** If $SS_1 = 100$ units, then $SS_{4} = 200$ units, $SS_{9} = 300$ units, $SS_{16} = 400$ units. Quadrupling the variety only doubles the safety stock — a counterintuitive but mathematically rigorous result that KTU examiners love to test.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Make vs. Buy Break-Even Quantity

The **Make vs. Buy** decision is one of the most strategic trade-offs in supply chain design.

### Step 1: Cost of Buying

If the firm buys from an external supplier at price $C_b$ per unit, the unit cost is:

$$
C_{buy} = C_b
$$

### Step 2: Cost of Making In-House

If the firm manufactures in-house, it incurs a fixed cost $F_c$ (setup, tooling, equipment depreciation) and a variable cost $V_m$ per unit (materials, labor, energy). For an annual volume $Q$:

$$
C_{make}(Q) = F_c + V_m \cdot Q
$$

### Step 3: Equate the Two Costs to Find Break-Even

Set the total cost of making equal to the total cost of buying:

$$
F_c + V_m \cdot Q_{BE} = C_b \cdot Q_{BE}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for $Q_{BE}$

$$
F_c = C_b \cdot Q_{BE} - V_m \cdot Q_{BE} = Q_{BE} \cdot (C_b - V_m)
$$

$$
Q_{BE} = \frac{F_c}{C_b - V_m}
$$

### Step 5: Trade-off Decision Rule

- If $Q < Q_{BE}$ → **Buy** (outsourcing is cheaper).
- If $Q > Q_{BE}$ → **Make** (in-house production is cheaper).
- The trade-off is between **fixed cost commitment** (high for make) and **per-unit cost** (high for buy).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Application:** This is a **frequently asked 14-mark question**. Always draw the cost-volume graph showing two lines: a horizontal line for buy and an upward-sloping line for make. The intersection is the break-even point.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: The Complete Trade-off Map

The following 5×5 matrix maps the major supply chain drivers against each other, with the trade-off direction indicated.

| Driver A $\downarrow$ vs. Driver B $\rightarrow$ | Inventory | Lead Time | Transportation Cost | Product Variety | Customer Experience |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inventory** | — | $\uparrow$ I requires $\uparrow$ LT (centralization) | $\downarrow$ TC requires $\uparrow$ Pipeline I | $\uparrow$ Variety requires $\uparrow$ I | $\uparrow$ Service requires $\uparrow$ I |
| **Lead Time** | $\downarrow$ LT requires $\downarrow$ I decentralization | — | $\downarrow$ LT requires $\uparrow$ TC (air) | $\uparrow$ Variety requires $\uparrow$ LT | $\downarrow$ LT improves CX |
| **Transportation Cost** | $\uparrow$ TC $\rightarrow$ $\downarrow$ Pipeline I | $\uparrow$ TC $\rightarrow$ $\downarrow$ LT | — | $\uparrow$ Variety fragments $\rightarrow$ $\uparrow$ TC | $\downarrow$ TC $\rightarrow$ $\downarrow$ CX |
| **Product Variety** | $\uparrow$ Variety $\rightarrow$ $\uparrow$ I | $\uparrow$ Variety $\rightarrow$ $\uparrow$ LT | $\uparrow$ Variety $\rightarrow$ $\uparrow$ TC | — | $\uparrow$ Variety $\rightarrow$ $\uparrow$ CX |
| **Customer Experience** | $\uparrow$ I $\rightarrow$ $\uparrow$ CX | $\downarrow$ LT $\rightarrow$ $\uparrow$ CX | $\uparrow$ TC $\rightarrow$ $\uparrow$ CX | $\uparrow$ Variety $\rightarrow$ $\uparrow$ CX | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Matrix:** The arrow notation "$\uparrow$ A requires $\uparrow$ B" means: *to increase A, you must also increase B*. This is the signature of a **positive trade-off** (both rise together, but at the cost of worsening the opposite cell). For example, "$\uparrow$ Inventory improves $\uparrow$ Customer Experience" but at the cost of efficiency.
