# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) and the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP).**

**Model Answer:**

The TSP involves a single salesman (or vehicle) who must visit a set of cities exactly once and return to the starting point, minimizing total distance. It has no capacity constraint.

The VRP, in contrast, involves a **fleet of vehicles** based at one or more depots, each with a **capacity constraint**, serving customers with specific demands. The objective is to determine the optimal assignment of customers to vehicles and the sequence in which each vehicle visits its assigned customers.

| Feature | TSP | VRP |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Vehicles | 1 (single salesman) | Multiple |
| Depot | Often not modeled | Central node (start/end) |
| Capacity | No constraint | Vehicle capacity $Q$ |
| Demand | Not considered | Each customer has $q_i$ |
| Complexity | NP-hard | NP-hard (more general) |
| Real-world use | Single-courier tours | Fleet dispatch |

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: What is the Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm? State the savings formula.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm** (1964) is a constructive heuristic used to solve the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP). It works by initially assigning each customer to a separate route from the depot, then iteratively merging pairs of routes that yield the maximum savings, subject to vehicle capacity constraints.

**Savings Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $d_{0i}$ is the distance from the depot to customer $i$, $d_{0j}$ is the distance from the depot to customer $j$, and $d_{ij}$ is the direct distance between customers $i$ and $j$. A higher $s_{ij}$ indicates that merging $i$ and $j$ on one route saves more distance.

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**A logistics company has a central warehouse (depot at node 0) that needs to deliver goods to 5 customers at nodes 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5. The vehicle capacity is 15 units. The distance matrix and customer demands are given below. Apply the Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm to determine the optimal vehicle routes and compute the total distance traveled.**

**Given:**

| Node | Demand (units) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 4 |
| 2 | 6 |
| 3 | 5 |
| 4 | 3 |
| 5 | 7 |

**Distance Matrix (km):**

| | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 8 | 12 | 18 | 22 | 30 |
| 1 | 8 | 0 | 10 | 14 | 20 | 28 |
| 2 | 12 | 10 | 0 | 6 | 14 | 22 |
| 3 | 18 | 14 | 6 | 0 | 8 | 16 |
| 4 | 22 | 20 | 14 | 8 | 0 | 10 |
| 5 | 30 | 28 | 22 | 16 | 10 | 0 |

#### Part (a): Compute the Savings and Rank Them — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute $s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$ for all pairs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{12} &= 8 + 12 - 10 = 10 \\
s_{13} &= 8 + 18 - 14 = 12 \\
s_{14} &= 8 + 22 - 20 = 10 \\
s_{15} &= 8 + 30 - 28 = 10 \\
s_{23} &= 12 + 18 - 6 = 24 \\
s_{24} &= 12 + 22 - 14 = 20 \\
s_{25} &= 12 + 30 - 22 = 20 \\
s_{34} &= 18 + 22 - 8 = 32 \\
s_{35} &= 18 + 30 - 16 = 32 \\
s_{45} &= 22 + 30 - 10 = 42
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Rank in Descending Order:**

| Rank | Pair | Savings |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (4, 5) | 42 |
| 2 | (3, 4) | 32 |
| 2 | (3, 5) | 32 |
| 4 | (2, 3) | 24 |
| 5 | (2, 4) | 20 |
| 5 | (2, 5) | 20 |
| 7 | (1, 3) | 12 |
| 8 | (1, 2) | 10 |
| 8 | (1, 4) | 10 |
| 8 | (1, 5) | 10 |

#### Part (b): Apply Iterative Merging and Compute Total Distance — 7 Marks

**Initial Routes (each customer separate):**
- R1: $0 \to 1 \to 0$ (load 4)
- R2: $0 \to 2 \to 0$ (load 6)
- R3: $0 \to 3 \to 0$ (load 5)
- R4: $0 \to 4 \to 0$ (load 3)
- R5: $0 \to 5 \to 0$ (load 7)

**Iteration 1: Merge (4, 5), savings 42**
- Both 4 and 5 are endpoints of their own routes.
- New load: $3 + 7 = 10 \leq 15$ ✓
- **Route A:** $0 \to 4 \to 5 \to 0$ (load 10)

**Iteration 2: Merge (3, 4), savings 32**
- Node 4 is an endpoint of Route A. Node 3 is on its own route.
- New load: $5 + 10 = 15 \leq 15$ ✓
- **Route A:** $0 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 0$ (load 15) — capacity fully utilized!

**Iteration 3: Merge (3, 5), savings 32**
- Both 3 and 5 are interior nodes of Route A. Cannot merge (not endpoints). **Reject.**

**Iteration 4: Merge (2, 3), savings 24**
- Node 3 is an endpoint of Route A. Node 2 is on its own route.
- New load: $15 + 6 = 21 > 15$ ✗ **Reject.**

**Iteration 5: Merge (2, 4), savings 20**
- Node 4 is interior of Route A. **Reject.**

**Iteration 6: Merge (2, 5), savings 20**
- Node 5 is an endpoint of Route A. Node 2 is on its own route.
- New load: $15 + 6 = 21 > 15$ ✗ **Reject.**

**Iteration 7: Merge (1, 3), savings 12**
- Node 3 is an endpoint of Route A. Node 1 is on its own route.
- New load: $15 + 4 = 19 > 15$ ✗ **Reject.**

**Iteration 8: Merge (1, 2), savings 10**
- Both 1 and 2 are endpoints of their own routes.
- New load: $4 + 6 = 10 \leq 15$ ✓
- **Route B:** $0 \to 1 \to 2 \to 0$ (load 10)

**Remaining pairs (1, 4) and (1, 5):** All involve interior nodes of Route A or would exceed capacity. **Reject.**

**Final Solution:**

**Route A:** $0 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 0$
- Distance: $d_{03} + d_{34} + d_{45} + d_{50} = 18 + 8 + 10 + 30 = 66$ km
- Load: 15 units (100% utilization)

**Route B:** $0 \to 1 \to 2 \to 0$
- Distance: $d_{01} + d_{12} + d_{20} = 8 + 10 + 12 = 30$ km
- Load: 10 units (66.7% utilization)

**Total Distance: $66 + 30 = 96$ km**
**Total Vehicles Used: 2**

**Comparison with Initial Solution (5 direct routes):**
- Initial distance: $2(d_{01} + d_{02} + d_{03} + d_{04} + d_{05}) = 2(8+12+18+22+30) = 180$ km
- Distance saved: $180 - 96 = 84$ km (**46.67%** reduction)

---

### QUESTION B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 14 Marks

**A courier service needs to find the shortest delivery route from the main office (node A) to deliver packages to 5 locations (B, C, D, E, F) and return to the main office. The distance matrix is given below.**

**Distance Matrix (km):**

| | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 |
| B | 10 | 0 | 12 | 18 | 22 | 26 |
| C | 15 | 12 | 0 | 8 | 14 | 20 |
| D | 20 | 18 | 8 | 0 | 6 | 12 |
| E | 25 | 22 | 14 | 6 | 0 | 8 |
| F | 30 | 26 | 20 | 12 | 8 | 0 |

#### Part (a): Solve using the Nearest Neighbor Heuristic starting from A — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Start at A. Nearest unvisited node = B (10 km). Tour: A → B, distance = 10.**

**Step 2: At B. Unvisited: C(12), D(18), E(22), F(26). Nearest = C (12 km). Tour: A → B → C, distance = 10 + 12 = 22.**

**Step 3: At C. Unvisited: D(8), E(14), F(20). Nearest = D (8 km). Tour: A → B → C → D, distance = 22 + 8 = 30.**

**Step 4: At D. Unvisited: E(6), F(12). Nearest = E (6 km). Tour: A → B → C → D → E, distance = 30 + 6 = 36.**

**Step 5: At E. Unvisited: F(8). Visit F. Tour: A → B → C → D → E → F, distance = 36 + 8 = 44.**

**Step 6: Return to A from F. Tour: A → B → C → D → E → F → A, distance = 44 + 30 = 74 km.**

**Nearest Neighbor Result: 74 km** (Tour: A-B-C-D-E-F-A)

#### Part (b): Try an alternative starting node and compare with a different heuristic — 7 Marks

**Alternative: Start at C. Apply Nearest Neighbor.**

**Step 1:** At C, nearest unvisited: D (8 km). Tour: C → D, distance = 8.

**Step 2:** At D, nearest unvisited: E (6 km). Tour: C → D → E, distance = 14.

**Step 3:** At E, nearest unvisited: F (8 km). Tour: C → D → E → F, distance = 22.

**Step 4:** At F, nearest unvisited: A (30 km). Tour: C → D → E → F → A, distance = 52.

**Step 5:** At A, nearest unvisited: B (10 km). Tour: C → D → E → F → A → B, distance = 62.

**Step 6:** Return to C. Tour: C → D → E → F → A → B → C, distance = 62 + 12 = 74 km.

**Alternative Nearest Neighbor Result: 74 km** (same total due to symmetry, but different tour: A-B-C-D-E-F-A rotated).

**Brute Force Check (small $n$, feasible to enumerate):**

Number of distinct tours for $n=6$: $(6-1)!/2 = 60$. We can check a promising one: A → B → C → D → E → F → A = 74 (already computed). Let us try A → C → E → D → F → B → A:
- $d_{AC} + d_{CE} + d_{ED} + d_{DF} + d_{FB} + d_{BA}$
- $= 15 + 14 + 6 + 12 + 26 + 10 = 83$ km (worse)

Try A → F → E → D → C → B → A:
- $d_{AF} + d_{FE} + d_{ED} + d_{DC} + d_{CB} + d_{BA}$
- $= 30 + 8 + 6 + 8 + 12 + 10 = 74$ km (tied)

The optimal appears to be **74 km**, achievable by multiple tours. The Nearest Neighbor Heuristic found this optimum in this case.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Return to Depot" step:** Nearest Neighbor gives a tour but you MUST add the final edge back to the start. Many students lose 2 marks by forgetting this.
> 2. **Capacity check in Clarke-Wright:** Always verify the combined demand does not exceed vehicle capacity AFTER every merge. Marking a route as "valid" without capacity check = -3 marks.
> 3. **Endpoint requirement:** In Clarke-Wright merging, both customers must be **endpoints** of their respective routes. Many students try to merge interior nodes, which is invalid.
> 4. **Predecessor in Dijkstra:** When updating distances, always update the predecessor (π). A correct shortest path tree without π reconstruction = -2 marks.
> 5. **Sign convention in savings formula:** The formula is $d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$, NOT $d_{0i} + d_{0j} + d_{ij}$. Using wrong sign produces negative savings and fails the algorithm.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Routing (Module 4)**

- **Routing** = determining the optimal sequence of customer visits assigned to vehicles, subject to constraints.
- **TSP** = single vehicle, visits each city once, returns to start, NP-hard.
- **VRP** = multiple vehicles, depot-based, capacity constraints.
- **CVRP** = VRP with explicit vehicle capacity $Q$.
- **VRPTW** = VRP with time windows $[e_i, l_i]$ for each customer.
- **VRPPD** = VRP with both pickup and delivery demands.
- **TSP Tours Count** = $(n-1)!/2$ (cyclic equivalence, direction equivalence).
- **Dijkstra's Algorithm:** single-source shortest path, non-negative weights, $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ or $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ with heap.
- **Bellman-Ford:** handles negative weights, $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$, detects negative cycles.
- **Floyd-Warshall:** all-pairs shortest path, $O(\vert V \vert^3)$, recurrence: $d^{(k)}_{ij} = \min(d^{(k-1)}_{ij}, d^{(k-1)}_{ik} + d^{(k-1)}_{kj})$.
- **Clarke-Wright Savings:** $s_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$; merge routes in descending savings order, respecting capacity and endpoint conditions.
- **Nearest Neighbor Heuristic:** greedy, start at depot, always visit nearest unvisited node, return to depot.
- **Load Utilization Ratio (LUR):** $\frac{\text{Actual Load}}{Q} \times 100\%$ — measure of vehicle efficiency.
- **Total Transportation Cost:** $C = c_f \cdot m + c_v \cdot D$ where $c_f$ = fixed cost/vehicle, $c_v$ = variable cost/km.
- **NP-hardness:** Both TSP and VRP are NP-hard, meaning no polynomial-time exact algorithm is known for large instances — hence the need for heuristics and metaheuristics.
- **Routing Hierarchy:** Strategic (network design) → Tactical (VRP) → Operational (dispatch).
- **Real-world Applications:** Amazon delivery, FedEx dispatch, KSRTC bus routing, garbage collection, ambulance routing, food delivery (Swiggy/Zomato).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:**
> 1. Memorize the Clarke-Wright formula and its iterative procedure — it is the most frequently asked 14-mark question.
> 2. Practice Dijkstra's algorithm with at least 5-6 node graphs.
> 3. Understand the **difference** between TSP, VRP, CVRP, and VRPTW — definition questions are common.
> 4. Always carry out capacity checks in Clarke-Wright problems — skipping them costs heavy marks.
> 5. For numerical problems, present your work in **tabular form** (savings table, distance table) for clarity — examiners reward neat presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling and Sequencing in Transportation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET502 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scheduling and Sequencing in Transportation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Scheduling and Sequencing in Transportation?

**Scheduling** in transportation refers to the systematic determination of *when* each vehicle, trip, crew, or task should begin and end, typically represented as a time-phased plan within a defined planning horizon (daily, weekly, or monthly). It answers the question: **"At what time should each operation occur?"**

**Sequencing** refers to the determination of the *order* in which a set of jobs, trips, vehicles, or tasks must be processed through one or more service facilities (machines, terminals, depots, or drivers). It answers the question: **"In what order should the operations be performed?"**

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> *Scheduling* is the allocation of resources (vehicles, drivers, time slots) to tasks over time, while *Sequencing* is the determination of the optimal order in which a set of tasks should be processed to optimize one or more performance measures (makespan, idle time, flow time, tardiness, utilization).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The School Bus Problem

Imagine you are the **Transport In-Charge of a school** managing **6 bus routes** picking up students from different localities before 8:00 AM. You have **3 buses** and **6 drivers**.

- **Sequencing** = Deciding *which route* to assign to *which bus* first (i.e., ordering the trips).
- **Scheduling** = Deciding *at what departure time* each bus leaves the depot, when the driver takes a break, and when the bus returns.

If you sequence poorly (e.g., longest route first on a small bus), buses arrive late. If you schedule poorly (e.g., two buses leave at the same gate), chaos ensues. This is precisely the *Scheduling + Sequencing* problem that KSRTC, Indian Railways, FedEx, and Amazon Logistics solve daily using Operations Research techniques.

---

## 1.3 Core Distinction Between the Two Terms

| Aspect | Scheduling | Sequencing |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision Variable** | Time (start/end) | Order (sequence) |
| **Primary Question** | WHEN? | WHAT ORDER? |
| **Typical Output** | Gantt Chart, Timetable | Permutation of jobs |
| **Granularity** | Time-phased | Order-based |
| **Application in Transport** | Bus timetable, Crew rosters | Vehicle trip assignment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> This topic appears in **Module 4 of PCIET502** and is frequently asked as a **Part B (14-mark) numerical problem** in End Semester Examinations. Students are expected to solve at least one sequencing problem using **Johnson's Rule** and one scheduling optimization using **Gantt Charts / Priority Rules**.

## 1.4 Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Notation)

- **Makespan (T_max)** — Total time to complete all jobs. **Lower is better.**
- **Flow Time (F_i)** — Time a job spends in the system. **Lower is better.**
- **Idle Time** — Time a machine/resource sits unused. **Lower is better.**
- **Tardiness (T_i)** — Lateness beyond due date. **Zero tardiness is ideal.**
- **Utilization (U)** — Ratio of productive time to total available time. **Higher is better.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Transportation Scheduling Problems

Transportation scheduling problems are broadly classified along three dimensions:

### A. By Number of Machines (Service Facilities)
- **n jobs – 1 machine** (trivial; just order by due date)
- **n jobs – 2 machines** (Johnson's Rule applicable)
- **n jobs – 3 machines** (use CDS heuristic or Jackson's algorithm)
- **n jobs – m machines** (complex; use heuristics/branch-and-bound)

### B. By Job Flow Pattern
- **Permutation Flow Shop** — Order of jobs is same on all machines.
- **Non-Permutation Flow Shop** — Order changes across machines.
- **Job Shop** — Each job has a unique routing.

### C. By Performance Objective
- Minimize makespan
- Minimize mean flow time
- Minimize maximum tardiness
- Minimize number of tardy jobs

---

## 2.2 Johnson's Rule for n Jobs – 2 Machines (Most Important for KTU)

Johnson's algorithm produces an **optimal sequence** for processing *n* jobs on *2* machines in a flow shop, with the objective of **minimizing makespan**.

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. Examine the processing times $(A_i, B_i)$ for each job $i$ on Machine 1 and Machine 2.
2. Find the **smallest** processing time among all $A_i$ and $B_i$.
3. **If the minimum is on Machine 1** → Place that job **as early as possible** (leftmost available position in sequence).
4. **If the minimum is on Machine 2** → Place that job **as late as possible** (rightmost available position in sequence).
5. **Tie-Breaking Rule**:
   - If minimum is on both machines for the *same* job → place at the leftmost.
   - If minimum is on Machine 1 for one job and Machine 2 for another but equal → place Machine 1 job first.
6. Remove the assigned job and **repeat Steps 2–5** until all jobs are sequenced.

### Conditions for Optimality
- Each job must be processed in the same order on both machines.
- Johnson's rule is **optimal only for 2 machines**; for 3 or more, heuristics like CDS (Campbell, Dudek, Smith) are used.

---

## 2.3 Sequencing Priority Rules (Dispatching Rules)

When a real-time decision must be made, the following **priority rules** are commonly used to sequence jobs/vehicles:

| Priority Rule | Decision Logic | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** (First Come First Served) | Earliest arrival first | Fairness |
| **SPT** (Shortest Processing Time) | Smallest processing time first | Minimizing flow time |
| **EDD** (Earliest Due Date) | Smallest due date first | Minimizing tardiness |
| **LPT** (Longest Processing Time) | Largest processing time first | Load balancing |
| **CR** (Critical Ratio) | Smallest (Due Date – Now) / Remaining Time | Dynamic scheduling |
| **SLACK** | Smallest (Due Date – Now – Processing Time) | Slack management |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Ratio (CR)** is widely used in transportation for real-time vehicle dispatching: $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Due Date} - \text{Current Time}}{\text{Remaining Processing Time}}$. A CR < 1.0 signals the job is *behind schedule*.

---

## 2.4 Vehicle Scheduling Problem (VSP) in Transport

The **Vehicle Scheduling Problem** is a classic transportation problem where:
- A set of **trips** (each with a departure time and arrival time at the depot) must be covered.
- A fleet of **vehicles** is available at the depot.
- **Objective**: Minimize the number of vehicles required (or total deadhead kilometers) such that each trip is covered exactly once, and a vehicle can serve trip $j$ after trip $i$ if the depot arrival time of $i \leq$ depot departure time of $j$.

This is solved using **network flow algorithms** or **set covering formulations** in practice.

---

## 2.5 Crew Scheduling in Transportation

After vehicle schedules are finalized, **crew (driver) schedules** must be constructed:
- A driver cannot work continuously beyond a **statutory limit** (e.g., 8 hours per shift in India under Motor Transport Workers Act).
- Crew schedules must include **meal breaks, rest periods, and changeovers**.
- Objective: **Minimize total crew cost** subject to labor regulations and trip coverage constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sequential optimization is: **Trip Scheduling → Vehicle Scheduling → Crew Scheduling**. This hierarchy is called the **"Three-Stage Transportation Planning"** in KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\max} = \max(C_{n,1}, C_{n,2})$ | Makespan = max of last completion on either machine | hours |
| 2 | $C_{i,1} = A_i + C_{i-1,1}$ | Completion of job $i$ on Machine 1 | hours |
| 3 | $C_{i,2} = B_i + \max(C_{i,1}, C_{i-1,2})$ | Completion on Machine 2 starts after both ready | hours |
| 4 | $\bar{F} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i$ | Mean flow time | hours |
| 5 | $F_i = C_{i,2} - r_i$ | Flow time of job $i$ (assume $r_i = 0$) | hours |
| 6 | $\text{Idle Time} = \sum C_{i-1,1} - \sum B_{i-1}$ for slots where $C_{i-1,1} < C_{i-2,2}$ | Idle time on Machine 2 | hours |
| 7 | $\text{CR}_i = \dfrac{d_i - t}{p_i^{\text{rem}}}$ | Critical Ratio for job $i$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $U_m = \dfrac{\sum_{i} p_{i,m}}{T_{\max}}$ | Machine utilization | fraction |
| 9 | $T_i = \max(0, C_i - d_i)$ | Tardiness of job $i$ | hours |
| 10 | $L_{\max} = \max(C_i - d_i)$ | Maximum lateness | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where**:
> $A_i, B_i$ = processing times of job $i$ on Machine 1 and Machine 2.
> $C_{i,m}$ = completion time of job $i$ on machine $m$.
> $d_i$ = due date of job $i$.
> $r_i$ = release time (often 0).
> $p_i^{\text{rem}}$ = remaining processing time.
> $\bar{F}$ = mean flow time.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **KSRTC / Indian Railways** uses Johnson's rule variants for loco scheduling and rake (train) sequencing.
- **Amazon / Flipkart** delivery vehicles use CR-based dynamic dispatch.
- **Airlines** (IndiGo, Air India) use 3-stage planning: Aircraft Routing → Crew Pairing → Crew Rostering.
- **Port Operations** (Cochin Port, JNPT): Quay crane sequencing using CDS heuristic.
- **Bus Rapid Transit (BRT)** systems use Vehicle Scheduling for minimum fleet size.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Johnson's Rule (n=5 jobs, 2 machines)

### Problem Statement
Five jobs must be processed on 2 machines in the same order (Machine 1 → Machine 2). Processing times (in hours) are:

| Job | Machine 1 ($A_i$) | Machine 2 ($B_i$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 5 | 2 |
| 2 | 1 | 6 |
| 3 | 9 | 7 |
| 4 | 3 | 8 |
| 5 | 10 | 4 |

**Find the optimal sequence using Johnson's Rule that minimizes makespan. Also compute makespan, idle time on Machine 2, and mean flow time.**

---

### Step 1: Identify Smallest Processing Time in the Table

List of all processing times:
$$\{5, 2, 1, 6, 9, 7, 3, 8, 10, 4\}$$

**Smallest = 1**, which corresponds to **Job 2 on Machine 1**.

By Johnson's Rule: since minimum is on **Machine 1** → place Job 2 **as early as possible (leftmost)**.

$$\text{Sequence so far: } [\, \text{Job 2} \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \_ \,]$$

---

### Step 2: Remove Job 2 and Repeat

Remaining jobs: {1, 3, 4, 5} with times $(A, B) = (5,2), (9,7), (3,8), (10,4)$.

List of remaining times: $\{5, 2, 9, 7, 3, 8, 10, 4\}$

**Smallest = 2**, which corresponds to **Job 1 on Machine 2**.

By Johnson's Rule: minimum is on **Machine 2** → place Job 1 **as late as possible (rightmost)**.

$$\text{Sequence so far: } [\, \text{Job 2} \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \text{Job 1} \,]$$

---

### Step 3: Remove Job 1, Repeat

Remaining jobs: {3, 4, 5} with times $(A, B) = (9,7), (3,8), (10,4)$.

List of remaining times: $\{9, 7, 3, 8, 10, 4\}$

**Smallest = 3**, which corresponds to **Job 4 on Machine 1**.

Place Job 4 **as early as possible (leftmost available slot)**.

$$\text{Sequence so far: } [\, \text{Job 2} \, \vert \, \text{Job 4} \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \text{Job 1} \,]$$

---

### Step 4: Remove Job 4, Repeat

Remaining jobs: {3, 5} with times $(A, B) = (9,7), (10,4)$.

List of remaining times: $\{9, 7, 10, 4\}$

**Smallest = 4**, which corresponds to **Job 5 on Machine 2**.

Place Job 5 **as late as possible (rightmost available slot)**.

$$\text{Sequence so far: } [\, \text{Job 2} \, \vert \, \text{Job 4} \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \text{Job 5} \, \vert \, \text{Job 1} \,]$$

---

### Step 5: Place Remaining Job 3 in the Only Empty Slot

$$\boxed{\text{Optimal Sequence: Job 2} \rightarrow \text{Job 4} \rightarrow \text{Job 3} \rightarrow \text{Job 5} \rightarrow \text{Job 1}}$$

---

### Step 6: Compute Makespan Using the Gantt Chart

We use the **completion time formulas**:

$$C_{i,1} = A_i + C_{i-1,1}, \quad C_{i,2} = B_i + \max(C_{i,1}, C_{i-1,2})$$

With $C_{0,1} = C_{0,2} = 0$.

**Job 2** ($A = 1, B = 6$):
$$C_{2,1} = 1 + 0 = 1 \text{ hour}$$
$$C_{2,2} = 6 + \max(1, 0) = 7 \text{ hours}$$

**Job 4** ($A = 3, B = 8$):
$$C_{4,1} = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ hours}$$
$$C_{4,2} = 8 + \max(4, 7) = 12 \text{ hours}$$

**Job 3** ($A = 9, B = 7$):
$$C_{3,1} = 9 + 4 = 13 \text{ hours}$$
$$C_{3,2} = 7 + \max(13, 12) = 20 \text{ hours}$$

**Job 5** ($A = 10, B = 4$):
$$C_{5,1} = 10 + 13 = 23 \text{ hours}$$
$$C_{5,2} = 4 + \max(23, 20) = 27 \text{ hours}$$

**Job 1** ($A = 5, B = 2$):
$$C_{1,1} = 5 + 23 = 28 \text{ hours}$$
$$C_{1,2} = 2 + \max(28, 27) = 30 \text{ hours}$$

---

### Step 7: Final Result

$$\boxed{T_{\max} = \max(C_{1,1}, C_{1,2}) = \max(28, 30) = 30 \text{ hours}}$$

**Idle Time on Machine 2:**

Machine 2 is idle whenever $C_{i-1,2} < C_{i,1}$ (i.e., the previous job finishes on M2 before the current job arrives from M1).

- After Job 2 on M2 ends at hour 7. Job 4 arrives at M2 at hour 4. So $C_{4,1} = 4 < C_{2,2} = 7$ → **No idle time**.
- After Job 4 on M2 ends at hour 12. Job 3 arrives at M2 at hour 13. So $C_{3,1} = 13 > C_{4,2} = 12$ → **Idle for 1 hour** (M2 idle from t=12 to t=13).
- After Job 3 on M2 ends at hour 20. Job 5 arrives at M2 at hour 23. So $C_{5,1} = 23 > C_{3,2} = 20$ → **Idle for 3 hours** (M2 idle from t=20 to t=23).
- After Job 5 on M2 ends at hour 27. Job 1 arrives at M2 at hour 28. So $C_{1,1} = 28 > C_{5,2} = 27$ → **Idle for 1 hour** (M2 idle from t=27 to t=28).

$$\boxed{\text{Total Idle Time on M2} = 0 + 1 + 3 + 1 = 5 \text{ hours}}$$

**Mean Flow Time:**

$$\bar{F} = \frac{1}{5}(C_{2,2} + C_{4,2} + C_{3,2} + C_{5,2} + C_{1,2}) = \frac{1}{5}(7 + 12 + 20 + 27 + 30)$$

$$\bar{F} = \frac{96}{5} = 19.2 \text{ hours}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Idle Time Identification Rule

For sequencing problems, a compact way to track idle time on Machine 2 is:

$$\text{Idle}_i = \max\left(0,\, C_{i-1,2} - C_{i,1}\right)$$

Equivalently, idle time on M2 occurs at slot $i$ when:

$$C_{i,1} > C_{i-1,2} \implies \text{Machine 2 waits for Machine 1}$$

This is the canonical KTU relationship and is the basis for the Gantt chart construction.

## 3.3 Comparison of Alternative Sequences (Sanity Check)

To confirm optimality, let's compare Johnson's sequence (30 hrs) with **FCFS** (1→2→3→4→5):

| Job | $C_{i,1}$ | $C_{i,2}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 5 | 7 |
| 2 | 6 | 13 |
| 3 | 15 | 22 |
| 4 | 18 | 30 |
| 5 | 28 | 34 |

FCFS makespan = 34 hours, which is **worse** than Johnson's 30 hours. Johnson's is **optimal** ✓.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam, **always state the optimal sequence, makespan, idle time, and mean flow time**. Partial solutions often lose 3–4 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Johnson's Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Read n jobs with times A_i, B_i"] --> B["Create candidate set of all unassigned jobs"]
    B --> C{"Find smallest processing time in set"}
    C -->|"Min on Machine 1"| D["Place job at LEFTMOST empty slot"]
    C -->|"Min on Machine 2"| E["Place job at RIGHTMOST empty slot"]
    C -->|"Tie - same job"| D
    C -->|"Tie - different jobs"| F["Place Machine 1 job first, then Machine 2 job"]
    D --> G["Remove job from candidate set"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{"Any jobs remaining?"}
    H -->|"Yes"| C
    H -->|"No"| I["Sequence is complete: Compute C_i1 and C_i2"]
    I --> J["Compute Makespan, Idle Time, Mean Flow Time"]
    J --> K["END: Report optimal sequence"]
```

## 4.2 Three-Stage Transportation Planning Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Stage 1: Trip Scheduling"] --> P2["Stage 2: Vehicle Scheduling"]
    P2 --> P3["Stage 3: Crew Scheduling"]
    P1A["Determine departure and arrival times of each trip"] --> P1
    P2A["Assign trips to minimum number of vehicles"] --> P2
    P3A["Assign drivers subject to duty hour limits"] --> P3
    P2 -.Feedback.-> P1
    P3 -.Feedback.-> P2
```

## 4.3 Gantt Chart for Johnson's Sequence (2 → 4 → 3 → 5 → 1)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Gantt Chart - Johnson Sequence (M1 and M2)
    dateFormat HH
    axisFormat %H
    section Machine 1
    Job 2 (1h) :job2m1, 00, 1h
    Job 4 (3h) :job4m1, after job2m1, 3h
    Job 3 (9h) :job3m1, after job4m1, 9h
    Job 5 (10h) :job5m1, after job3m1, 10h
    Job 1 (5h) :job1m1, after job5m1, 5h
    section Machine 2
    Job 2 (6h) :job2m2, 01, 6h
    Job 4 (8h) :job4m2, after job2m2, 8h
    Job 3 (7h) :job3m2, after job4m2, 7h
    Job 5 (4h) :job5m2, after job3m2, 4h
    Job 1 (2h) :job1m2, after job5m2, 2h
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Real-Time Dispatch Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Real-time Trip / Order Queue"] --> S1{"Apply Priority Rule"}
    S1 -->|"FCFS"| D1["Select earliest arrival"]
    S1 -->|"SPT"| D2["Select shortest processing"]
    S1 -->|"EDD"| D3["Select earliest due date"]
    S1 -->|"CR"| D4["Select smallest CR value"]
    D1 --> A["Assign to next available vehicle"]
    D2 --> A
    D3 --> A
    D4 --> A
    A --> M["Update vehicle status and schedule"]
    M --> L["Log dispatch decision"]
    L --> R
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define scheduling and sequencing. How do they differ in the context of transportation management?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Scheduling** is the time-phased allocation of resources (vehicles, drivers, trips) over a planning horizon, specifying start and end times of each operation. (1.5 marks)
- **Sequencing** is the determination of the optimal *order* in which a set of jobs, trips, or vehicles must be processed through one or more service facilities to optimize a performance measure such as makespan or flow time. (1 mark)
- **Difference**: Scheduling determines *when* each operation occurs; sequencing determines *in what order* operations occur. In KSRTC, scheduling produces the timetable; sequencing determines the order of trips assigned to each bus. (0.5 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State Johnson's Rule for sequencing n jobs on 2 machines. Why is it not optimal for 3 machines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Johnson's Rule (2 marks)**: Examine processing times $(A_i, B_i)$ for each job. Find the smallest time. If the smallest is on Machine 1, schedule that job first (leftmost). If the smallest is on Machine 2, schedule that job last (rightmost). Remove the job and repeat until all jobs are placed.
- **Why not optimal for 3 machines (1 mark)**: Johnson's Rule is mathematically proven optimal only for the 2-machine flow shop. For 3 or more machines, the algorithm fails because it cannot guarantee that a job that "looks best" on one machine will not cause excessive idle time on another. Heuristics like **CDS (Campbell-Dudek-Smith)** are used instead, which apply Johnson's rule on pairs of machines iteratively.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Five jobs are to be processed on two machines in the order M1 → M2. The processing times (in hours) are given below:

| Job | M1 | M2 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | 7 | 4 |
| B | 5 | 1 |
| C | 3 | 6 |
| D | 4 | 2 |
| E | 8 | 5 |

Apply Johnson's algorithm to determine the **optimal sequence** and find the corresponding **makespan**.

**(b) [7 marks)** For the sequence obtained in part (a), compute the **idle time on Machine 2** and the **mean flow time** of the jobs. Also draw a Gantt chart representation.

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Applying Johnson's Algorithm:**

Step 1: List all processing times. Smallest = 1, corresponds to **Job B on M2**. Place B at the rightmost slot.

$$\text{Sequence: } [\_ \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, B]$$

Step 2: Remove B. Remaining times: $\{7,4,3,6,4,2,8,5\}$. Smallest = 2, corresponds to **Job D on M2**. Place D at the next rightmost slot.

$$\text{Sequence: } [\_ \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, D \, \vert \, B]$$

Step 3: Remove D. Remaining times: $\{7,4,3,6,8,5\}$. Smallest = 3, corresponds to **Job C on M1**. Place C at the leftmost slot.

$$\text{Sequence: } [C \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, D \, \vert \, B]$$

Step 4: Remove C. Remaining times: $\{7,4,6,8,5\}$. Smallest = 4. Appears twice: Job A on M1 and Job A on M2 (same job, tie → place at leftmost). Place A at the next leftmost slot.

$$\text{Sequence: } [C \, \vert \, A \, \vert \, \_ \, \vert \, D \, \vert \, B]$$

Step 5: Remaining job E goes to the empty slot.

$$\boxed{\text{Optimal Sequence: } C \rightarrow A \rightarrow E \rightarrow D \rightarrow B}$$

**Makespan Calculation:**

| Job | M1 Time | $C_{i,1}$ | M2 Time | $C_{i,2}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| C | 3 | 3 | 6 | 6 |
| A | 7 | 10 | 4 | 14 |
| E | 8 | 18 | 5 | 23 |
| D | 4 | 22 | 2 | 25 |
| B | 5 | 27 | 1 | 26 |

Detailed step-by-step:
- Job C: $C_{C,1} = 3$, $C_{C,2} = 6 + \max(3, 0) = 9$. Wait — let me recompute. With $A_C = 3, B_C = 6$: $C_{C,1} = 3$, $C_{C,2} = 6 + \max(3, 0) = 9$. 
- Job A: $C_{A,1} = 3 + 7 = 10$, $C_{A,2} = 4 + \max(10, 9) = 14$.
- Job E: $C_{E,1} = 10 + 8 = 18$, $C_{E,2} = 5 + \max(18, 14) = 23$.
- Job D: $C_{D,1} = 18 + 4 = 22$, $C_{D,2} = 2 + \max(22, 23) = 25$.
- Job B: $C_{B,1} = 22 + 5 = 27$, $C_{B,2} = 1 + \max(27, 25) = 28$.

$$\boxed{T_{\max} = \max(27, 28) = 28 \text{ hours}}$$

**Part (b) — Idle Time and Mean Flow Time:**

Idle time on M2 occurs when $C_{i,1} > C_{i-1,2}$:

- After C: $C_{C,2} = 9$, $C_{A,1} = 10 > 9$ → **Idle = 1 hour**.
- After A: $C_{A,2} = 14$, $C_{E,1} = 18 > 14$ → **Idle = 4 hours**.
- After E: $C_{E,2} = 23$, $C_{D,1} = 22 < 23$ → **No idle time**.
- After D: $C_{D,2} = 25$, $C_{B,1} = 27 > 25$ → **Idle = 2 hours**.

$$\boxed{\text{Total Idle Time on M2} = 1 + 4 + 0 + 2 = 7 \text{ hours}}$$

**Mean Flow Time:**

$$\bar{F} = \frac{1}{5}(9 + 14 + 23 + 25 + 28) = \frac{99}{5} = 19.8 \text{ hours}$$

**Gantt Chart (text representation):**

```
Time →  0  3      10          18      22  27
M1:     |C|   A(7)     |E(8)| D(4)|B|
M2:        |C(6)| idle |A(4)| idle|E(5)| D(2)| idle|B(1)|
         0  3  9     10  14     18  23  25    27  28
```

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Vehicle Scheduling Problem (VSP)** in public transport. Describe how Johnson's sequencing principles can be extended to minimize the number of vehicles required to cover a given set of trips.

**(b) [7 marks]** A logistics company has **6 delivery trips** that must be covered by a fleet of vehicles based at a single depot. Each trip has a departure time and a return-to-depot time as given:

| Trip | Departure | Return to Depot |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| T1 | 08:00 | 09:30 |
| T2 | 09:00 | 11:00 |
| T3 | 10:30 | 12:00 |
| T4 | 12:00 | 13:30 |
| T5 | 13:00 | 15:00 |
| T6 | 14:30 | 16:30 |

Determine the **minimum number of vehicles** required and the **trip assignment plan** using the vehicle scheduling logic.

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Vehicle Scheduling Problem (7 marks):**

The **Vehicle Scheduling Problem (VSP)** is the problem of assigning a set of trips to a minimum number of vehicles (or equivalently, minimum total cost) such that:
- Each trip is assigned to exactly one vehicle.
- A vehicle can serve trip $j$ after trip $i$ only if the **depot return time of $i$ ≤ depot departure time of $j$**.
- The vehicle starts and ends at the depot.

**Extension of Johnson's logic:**
While Johnson's algorithm is for 2-machine flow shop, its underlying principle — **earliest availability and minimum gap pairing** — extends to VSP through the following approach:
1. Sort trips by **departure time**.
2. For each trip, find the **next trip** that can be chained (return time of previous ≤ departure time of next) with the **smallest idle gap**.
3. Build a **trip chaining graph** (a bipartite or interval graph).
4. Apply **greedy matching** to chain trips, minimizing total idle time and number of vehicles.

This problem is mathematically formulated as a **set-covering problem** and solved using network flow or integer programming. (1.5 marks for definition + 2 marks for logic + 2 marks for formulation + 1.5 marks for Johnson's extension)

**Part (b) — Minimum Number of Vehicles (7 marks):**

Using the **chaining logic** (greedy assignment):

| Vehicle | Trip Chain | Logic |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Vehicle 1 | T1 → T2 → T3 → T4 → T5 → T6 | T1 returns 09:30 ≥ T2 departs 09:00 ✓; T2 returns 11:00 ≥ T3 departs 10:30 ✓; T3 returns 12:00 ≥ T4 departs 12:00 ✓; T4 returns 13:30 ≥ T5 departs 13:00 ✓; T5 returns 15:00 ≥ T6 departs 14:30 ✓. |

All 6 trips can be chained sequentially on **one vehicle** because the return time of each trip is always ≤ the next trip's departure time.

$$\boxed{\text{Minimum number of vehicles} = 1}$$

**Vehicle Utilization:**
$$\text{Active service time} = 1.5 + 2 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 2 = 10.5 \text{ hours}$$
$$\text{Total elapsed time} = 16.5 - 8.0 = 8.5 \text{ hours}$$
$$\text{Idle time} = 8.5 - 6.5 = 2.0 \text{ hours of slack between trips}$$

Since the problem constraints are easily satisfiable, **one vehicle suffices**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong tie-breaking in Johnson's Rule** (–2 marks): When a processing time appears on both machines for *different* jobs (not the same job), the convention is to place the Machine 1 job first. Many students incorrectly place Machine 2 first.
> 2. **Confusing makespan formula** (–2 marks): Makespan is $T_{\max} = \max(C_{n,1}, C_{n,2})$, **not** just $C_{n,2}$. The makespan of a 2-machine problem may be governed by the last completion on Machine 1.
> 3. **Missing the Gantt Chart** (–1 mark): KTU expects a Gantt chart in 14-mark answers. Without it, the student loses presentation marks.
> 4. **Forgetting idle time formula** (–2 marks): Idle time on M2 is $\max(0, C_{i,1} - C_{i-1,2})$ summed over all jobs. Students often compute only makespan and skip idle time.
> 5. **Using Johnson's Rule for 3 machines** (–3 marks): Johnson's Rule is **only** for 2 machines. For 3 machines, mention CDS heuristic explicitly.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Scheduling** = *When*; **Sequencing** = *What order*.
- ✅ **Johnson's Rule** is optimal only for **n jobs – 2 machines** flow shop.
- ✅ Tie-breaking: same job with min on both → leftmost; different jobs with equal min → Machine 1 job first.
- ✅ **Makespan** = $\max(C_{n,1}, C_{n,2})$.
- ✅ **Idle time on M2** = $\max(0, C_{i,1} - C_{i-1,2})$ summed over all $i$.
- ✅ **Mean Flow Time** = $\frac{1}{n}\sum C_{i,2}$ (when release time = 0).
- ✅ Priority rules: **FCFS, SPT, EDD, LPT, CR, SLACK** — know what each minimizes.
- ✅ **Critical Ratio (CR)** = $\frac{d_i - t}{p_i^{\text{rem}}}$ — used for dynamic dispatch.
- ✅ **Three-stage transportation planning**: Trip Scheduling → Vehicle Scheduling → Crew Scheduling.
- ✅ **VSP objective**: minimize number of vehicles OR minimize deadhead km.
- ✅ For 3+ machines, use **CDS heuristic** (k iterations of Johnson's on M1+M(k+1) pairs).
- ✅ Always draw a **Gantt chart** in 14-mark numerical answers.
- ✅ Vehicles idle when $C_{i,1} > C_{i-1,2}$ (Machine 2 waits for Machine 1).
- ✅ KTU commonly tests Johnson's rule for 5–6 jobs on 2 machines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Mantra for KTU 2024**:
> *"Smallest time? M1 → front, M2 → back. T_max = max(last M1, last M2). Idle = M2 waiting. Gantt = full marks."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solutions for Vehicle Routing Problems and Multi-Stage Transportation Problems Included with Node Capacities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET502 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solutions for Vehicle Routing Problems and Multi-Stage Transportation Problems Included with Node Capacities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — VEHICLE ROUTING & MULTI-STAGE TRANSPORTATION WITH NODE CAPACITIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** is a combinatorial optimization and Integer Programming problem that asks *"What is the optimal set of routes for a fleet of vehicles to traverse in order to deliver goods to a given set of customers?"* It generalises the classical **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** by introducing a *fleet* of identical vehicles, all originating from and returning to a single **Central Depot**.

**Mathematical Statement:**
$$\text{VRP} = \min \sum_{k \in K} \sum_{(i,j) \in A} c_{ij} \, x_{ijk}$$

Subject to:
- Each customer is visited **exactly once**.
- Each vehicle route begins and ends at the **depot**.
- Vehicle **load capacity** is never violated.
- Tour **time / distance** bound is respected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Morning Newspaper Run":**
> Imagine you run a newspaper distribution agency in a city. You have **3 delivery vans** (capacity: 200 papers each) starting from the *Press Office* (depot). You must deliver to **15 street corners** (customers), but no van can carry more than 200 papers. The VRP asks: *"Which 5 corners should Van-1 cover, which 5 for Van-2, etc., so the total distance driven is the minimum?"* The **savings** come from grouping nearby customers into a single zig-zag route rather than sending each van on a separate back-and-forth trip.

### 1.2 Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Term — CVRP:**
> A **Capacitated VRP** imposes an upper bound on the total demand that any single vehicle can carry in a route. It is the most commonly tested variant in KTU examinations because it couples the routing geometry with a real-world physical constraint (truck tonnage / container volume).

$$\text{CVRP Constraint:} \quad \sum_{j \in C} d_j \, y_{jk} \;\leq\; Q \quad \forall \, k \in K$$

Where:
- $d_j$ = demand of customer $j$
- $Q$ = vehicle capacity
- $y_{jk} = 1$ if customer $j$ is assigned to vehicle $k$, else $0$

### 1.3 Multi-Stage Transportation Problem (MSTP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Multi-Stage Transportation Problem** is an extension of the classical (single-stage) transportation problem in which goods flow through **intermediate transshipment nodes** (warehouses, consolidation centres, cross-docks) before reaching the final destination. In a **Two-Stage Problem**, supply at *Plants → Warehouses* (Stage 1) and *Warehouses → Customers* (Stage 2) is optimised jointly.

**Decision Variables:**
- $x_{ij}$ = units shipped from Plant $i$ to Warehouse $j$ (Stage 1)
- $y_{jk}$ = units shipped from Warehouse $j$ to Customer $k$ (Stage 2)

### 1.4 Node Capacities (Transshipment Capacity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition — Node Capacity in MSTP:**
> A **Node Capacity** is the maximum throughput allowed at an intermediate node (warehouse, port, distribution centre). It represents a *bottleneck* that must be respected to prevent queueing, congestion, or stock-pile overflow at the transshipment facility.

$$\text{Node Capacity Constraint:} \quad \sum_{i} x_{ij} \;\leq\; C_j \quad \forall \, j \in W$$

Where $C_j$ is the maximum handling capacity of warehouse $j$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Toll Booth Bottleneck":**
> Think of a highway with three toll booths. Even if 1,000 cars per hour arrive upstream, the toll booths can collectively process only **800 cars per hour**. The toll plaza is the *transshipment node*, and its *capacity* of 800 cars/hr is the node capacity. If 1,000 cars arrive, the system *congest*, and the optimisation must reroute some cars to a different plaza. The **Multi-Stage Transportation Problem with Node Capacities** is exactly this bottleneck-aware rerouting of goods.

### 1.5 KTU 2024 Module 4 — Topic Mapping

| Sub-Topic | Real-World Entity | KTU Cognitive Level |
|---|---|---|
| Classical VRP | Fleet routing | L3 — Apply |
| Capacitated VRP (CVRP) | Truck tonnage | L4 — Analyse |
| VRP with Time Windows | Delivery schedules | L4 — Analyse |
| Clarke-Wright Savings | Route consolidation | L5 — Evaluate |
| Multi-Stage Transport | Plant → DC → Retailer | L3 — Apply |
| Node-Capacity Constraints | Port / Warehouse throughput | L5 — Evaluate |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vehicle Routing Family — Variants Tree

```
                 ┌── Travelling Salesman (1 vehicle)
                 │
VRP (Base) ──────┼── Capacitated VRP (CVRP)
                 │       └── VRP with Time Windows (VRPTW)
                 ├── VRP with Pickup & Delivery (VRPPD)
                 ├── Open VRP (OVRP)
                 └── Multiple Depot VRP (MDVRP)
```

## 2.2 Logic Flow — Why Vehicle Routing Exists

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core "Why":**
> If every customer is served by a *separate* trip from the depot, the total cost equals $n \times (\text{round trip depot to customer})$. The **savings** of routing are obtained when two customers are served in a *single* trip instead of two — the depot-to-customer leg is shared.

**Step-by-step Operational Logic:**

1. **Aggregation:** Group customers into clusters such that total demand per cluster $\leq$ vehicle capacity $Q$.
2. **Sequencing:** Within each cluster, determine the visiting order that minimises total distance (an embedded TSP).
3. **Assignment:** Allocate each cluster to a specific vehicle $k \in K$.
4. **Feasibility Check:** Verify load, time, and node-capacity constraints at every intermediate point.
5. **Iteration:** Re-optimise if any constraint is violated or a cost reduction is found.

## 2.3 Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm — Logic Decomposition

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** This is the **most-tested** VRP heuristic in KTU 2024 scheme papers. Memorise the formula and the 6-step procedure.

**Step 1 — Initialise:** Create $n$ separate routes $\{0 \to i \to 0\}$ for each customer $i$.

**Step 2 — Compute Savings:** For every pair $(i,j)$:
$$S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$$

**Step 3 — Sort:** Arrange all $S_{ij}$ in **descending order**.

**Step 4 — Merge Iteratively:** Scan the sorted list. For each $S_{ij}$, merge routes of $i$ and $j$ if:
- Neither $i$ nor $j$ is **interior** to its current route.
- The combined demand $\leq$ vehicle capacity $Q$.

**Step 5 — Repeat** until no further feasible merges exist.

**Step 6 — Output** the final route set.

## 2.4 Multi-Stage Transportation — Network Topology

A **Two-Stage Network** has the following structure:

```
 PLANTS (Sources)  ──Stage 1──►  WAREHOUSES (Transshipment)  ──Stage 2──►  CUSTOMERS (Destinations)
   s_i (supply)                       C_j (capacity)                         d_k (demand)
```

**Mathematical Model:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimise } \quad & Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} x_{ij} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{p} d_{jk} y_{jk} \\[4pt]
\text{s.t.} \quad
& \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \;\leq\; s_i \quad \forall i \quad \text{(Plant supply)} \\
& \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} \;\leq\; C_j \quad \forall j \quad \text{(Node capacity)} \\
& \sum_{k=1}^{p} y_{jk} \;\leq\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} \quad \forall j \quad \text{(Warehouse flow balance)} \\
& \sum_{j=1}^{n} y_{jk} \;\geq\; D_k \quad \forall k \quad \text{(Customer demand)} \\
& x_{ij}, \, y_{jk} \;\geq\; 0
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Method Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAVE THIS TABLE** — these are the exact equations the KTU board examiner expects to see written out in a 14-mark solution.

| # | Concept | Formula / Method | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | VRP Objective | $\min \sum_k \sum_{(i,j)} c_{ij} x_{ijk}$ | $c_{ij}$ = ₹ per km, $x_{ijk}$ = binary |
| 2 | Clarke-Wright Savings | $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$ | Symmetric: $S_{ij} = S_{ji}$ |
| 3 | CVRP Load Bound | $\sum_{j \in R_k} d_j \leq Q$ | $Q$ = vehicle capacity (tonnes) |
| 4 | Vehicle Count | $m = \lceil \sum d_j / Q \rceil$ | Minimum vehicles required |
| 5 | MSTP Total Cost | $Z = Z_{\text{Stage 1}} + Z_{\text{Stage 2}}$ | Additive across stages |
| 6 | Node Capacity | $\sum_i x_{ij} \leq C_j$ | Hard constraint, *not* objective |
| 7 | Transshipment Balance | $\sum_i x_{ij} = \sum_k y_{jk}$ | Pure transshipment (no storage) |
| 8 | Penalty / Dummy Cost | $M = 0$ for real arc, large for dummy | Used in MODI / VAM when unbalanced |
| 9 | Nearest-Neighbour Rule | Pick the closest unvisited node | Heuristic; greedy |
| 10 | Sweep Algorithm Angle | $\theta_j = \arctan(y_j - y_0, x_j - x_0)$ | Polar sweep around depot |

## 2.6 Why These Models Matter in Real Engineering

- **Logistics Cost Reduction:** A 1% improvement in route consolidation across an FMCG fleet can save **crores of ₹/year** in diesel.
- **Carbon Footprint:** VRP optimisation directly reduces Scope-1 emissions of any manufacturing firm.
- **Port Congestion:** Node-capacity constraints in MSTP model the realistic bottleneck of Indian ports (Mundra, Cochin).
- **Cold-Chain:** CVRP + time-window combination is the backbone of pharmaceutical distribution (e.g., vaccines from Serum Institute, Pune).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Clarke-Wright Savings on a 5-Customer CVRP

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A depot at node **0** must serve **5 customers** (A, B, C, D, E). Vehicle capacity $Q = \mathbf{40}$ units. Distances (symmetric) are given below. Customer demands: A = 10, B = 15, C = 20, D = 10, E = 15. Find the optimal route set using the **Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm**.

**Distance Matrix $d_{ij}$:**

| From \ To | 0 (Depot) | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 |
| **A** | 10 | 0 | 12 | 18 | 22 | 28 |
| **B** | 15 | 12 | 0 | 10 | 16 | 22 |
| **C** | 20 | 18 | 10 | 0 | 8 | 14 |
| **D** | 25 | 22 | 16 | 8 | 0 | 6 |
| **E** | 30 | 28 | 22 | 14 | 6 | 0 |

### Step 1 — Initial Routes (one per customer)

| Route | Customers | Total Distance | Load |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R_1$ | 0 → A → 0 | $10 + 10 = 20$ | 10 |
| $R_2$ | 0 → B → 0 | $15 + 15 = 30$ | 15 |
| $R_3$ | 0 → C → 0 | $20 + 20 = 40$ | 20 |
| $R_4$ | 0 → D → 0 | $25 + 25 = 50$ | 10 |
| $R_5$ | 0 → E → 0 | $30 + 30 = 60$ | 15 |

**Total initial distance** = $20 + 30 + 40 + 50 + 60 = \mathbf{200}$ units.

### Step 2 — Compute Savings $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{AB} &= 10 + 15 - 12 = 13 \\
S_{AC} &= 10 + 20 - 18 = 12 \\
S_{AD} &= 10 + 25 - 22 = 13 \\
S_{AE} &= 10 + 30 - 28 = 12 \\
S_{BC} &= 15 + 20 - 10 = 25 \\
S_{BD} &= 15 + 25 - 16 = 24 \\
S_{BE} &= 15 + 30 - 22 = 23 \\
S_{CD} &= 20 + 25 - 8 = 37 \\
S_{CE} &= 20 + 30 - 14 = 36 \\
S_{DE} &= 25 + 30 - 6 = 49
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Sort Savings in Descending Order

| Rank | Pair | Savings $S_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (D, E) | **49** |
| 2 | (C, D) | **37** |
| 3 | (C, E) | **36** |
| 4 | (B, C) | **25** |
| 5 | (B, D) | **24** |
| 6 | (B, E) | **23** |
| 7 | (A, B) | **13** |
| 8 | (A, D) | **13** |
| 9 | (A, C) | **12** |
| 10 | (A, E) | **12** |

### Step 4 — Iterative Merging

**Merge 1 — Pair (D, E), $S = 49$:**
- New route: 0 → D → E → 0
- Load: $10 + 15 = 25 \leq 40$ ✓
- Route distance: $25 + 6 + 30 = 61$

**Merge 2 — Pair (C, D), $S = 37$:**
- C is alone, D is an *endpoint* of 0–D–E–0. Merge C as predecessor of D.
- New route: 0 → C → D → E → 0
- Load: $20 + 10 + 15 = 45 > 40$ ✗ **Capacity Violated.**
- **Reject** this merge.

**Merge 3 — Pair (C, E), $S = 36$:**
- C is alone, E is an endpoint. Merge C as predecessor of E.
- New route: 0 → C → E → 0 (replacing 0 → E → 0, leaving 0 → D → 0 separate)
- Load: $20 + 15 = 35 \leq 40$ ✓
- Route distance: $20 + 14 + 30 = 64$

**Merge 4 — Pair (B, C), $S = 25$:**
- B is alone, C is an endpoint of 0 → C → E → 0. Merge B as predecessor of C.
- New route: 0 → B → C → E → 0
- Load: $15 + 20 + 15 = 50 > 40$ ✗ **Capacity Violated.**
- **Reject.**

**Merge 5 — Pair (A, B), $S = 13$:**
- Both A and B are alone. Merge.
- New route: 0 → A → B → 0
- Load: $10 + 15 = 25 \leq 40$ ✓
- Route distance: $10 + 12 + 15 = 37$

**Merge 6 — Pair (A, D), $S = 13$:**
- A is interior of 0 → A → B → 0. **Cannot merge.** Reject.

**Merge 7 — Pair (A, C), $S = 12$:**
- A is interior. Reject.

### Step 5 — Final Route Set

| Route | Description | Load | Distance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R_A$ | 0 → A → B → 0 | 25 | 37 |
| $R_C$ | 0 → C → E → 0 | 35 | 64 |
| $R_D$ | 0 → D → 0 | 10 | 50 |

**Total Final Distance** = $37 + 64 + 50 = \mathbf{151}$ units.

### Step 6 — Savings Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= \text{Initial Total} - \text{Final Total} \\
&= 200 - 151 = \mathbf{49 \text{ units saved}} \quad (\text{24.5\% cost reduction})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The KTU board examiner will *always* check that you have written the capacity check explicitly. A missing "≤ 40 ✓" or "> 40 ✗" remark can cost you 1 full mark in a 14-mark answer.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Two-Stage Transportation with Node Capacities

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Two plants $P_1, P_2$ ship to two warehouses $W_1, W_2$ which in turn ship to three retailers $R_1, R_2, R_3$.
> - $P_1$ supply = 30, $P_2$ supply = 20
> - Warehouse capacities: $C_{W_1} = 25$, $C_{W_2} = 30$
> - $R_1$ demand = 15, $R_2$ demand = 20, $R_3$ demand = 15
> - Stage 1 costs $c_{ij}$ (Plant → Warehouse), Stage 2 costs $d_{jk}$ (Warehouse → Retailer).
>
> **Find the minimum cost feasible shipment plan.**

**Cost Matrices:**

Stage 1 — Plant to Warehouse:

| | $W_1$ | $W_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 8 | 6 |
| $P_2$ | 5 | 9 |

Stage 2 — Warehouse to Retailer:

| | $R_1$ | $R_2$ | $R_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ | 4 | 7 | 5 |
| $W_2$ | 6 | 3 | 8 |

### Step 1 — Balance the Problem

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Supply} &= 30 + 20 = 50 \\
\text{Total Demand} &= 15 + 20 + 15 = 50 \\
\text{Balanced ✓} && (\text{No dummy needed})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve Stage 1 (Plant → Warehouse)

Apply **Minimum Cost Method (LCM)**:

**Allocation 1:** Minimum cost in Stage 1 = 5 at $(P_2, W_1)$. Allocate $\min(20, 25) = 20$. Now $P_2$ exhausted, supply remaining at $P_1$ = 30.

**Allocation 2:** Next minimum = 6 at $(P_1, W_2)$. Allocate $\min(30, 30) = 30$. Now $W_2$ full, $P_1$ exhausted.

**Stage 1 Allocation Table:**

| | $W_1$ (25) | $W_2$ (30) | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 0 | **30** | 30 |
| $P_2$ | **20** | 0 | 20 |
| Demand | 25 | 30 | 50/50 |

**Stage 1 Cost:**
$$Z_1 = (30 \times 6) + (20 \times 5) = 180 + 100 = \mathbf{280}$$

### Step 3 — Solve Stage 2 (Warehouse → Retailer)

Available at $W_1$ = 20 units (received from $P_2$). Available at $W_2$ = 30 units (received from $P_1$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Capacity Check:** $W_1$ capacity is 25, but only 20 units arrived (since $P_2$ had 20 only). $W_1$ is *not* a bottleneck here, but $W_2$ capacity 30 is exactly filled. **Constraint is active at $W_2$**.

Apply **LCM** to Stage 2:

**Allocation 1:** Minimum cost in Stage 2 = 3 at $(W_2, R_2)$. Allocate $\min(30, 20) = 20$. $R_2$ satisfied, $W_2$ remaining = 10.

**Allocation 2:** Next minimum = 4 at $(W_1, R_1)$. Allocate $\min(20, 15) = 15$. $R_1$ satisfied, $W_1$ remaining = 5.

**Allocation 3:** Next minimum = 5 at $(W_1, R_3)$. Allocate $\min(5, 15) = 5$. $W_1$ exhausted.

**Allocation 4:** Next minimum = 6 at $(W_2, R_1)$. $R_1$ already satisfied. Move to 8 at $(W_2, R_3)$. Allocate $\min(10, 10) = 10$. $R_3$ satisfied, $W_2$ exhausted.

**Stage 2 Allocation Table:**

| | $R_1$ (15) | $R_2$ (20) | $R_3$ (15) | Available |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ | **15** | 0 | **5** | 20 |
| $W_2$ | 0 | **20** | **10** | 30 |
| Demand | 15 | 20 | 15 | 50/50 |

**Stage 2 Cost:**
$$Z_2 = (15 \times 4) + (5 \times 5) + (20 \times 3) + (10 \times 8) = 60 + 25 + 60 + 80 = \mathbf{225}$$

### Step 4 — Total Minimum Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{total}} &= Z_1 + Z_2 \\
&= 280 + 225 = \mathbf{505 \text{ currency units}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify Node-Capacity Feasibility

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_i x_{i,W_1} &= 0 + 20 = 20 \;\leq\; 25 \quad \checkmark \\
\sum_i x_{i,W_2} &= 30 + 0 = 30 \;\leq\; 30 \quad \checkmark \;\;(\text{Active constraint})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Interpretation:**
> In this network, the second warehouse $W_2$ is operating at its *full capacity* — any growth in upstream supply would require expanding $W_2$ or rerouting. This is precisely the kind of insight a supply-chain manager extracts from an MSTP with node capacities.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Which Algorithm for Which Problem?** This decision matrix appears directly in KTU 2024 ESE questions.

| Problem Type | Recommended Method | Complexity | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small VRP (≤ 10 nodes) | Exact (Branch & Bound) | High | Rare |
| Medium VRP (10–50) | Clarke-Wright Savings | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | **High (7 marks)** |
| Large VRP (> 50) | Genetic Algorithm / Tabu | Heuristic | Medium |
| Single-Stage TP | LCM / VAM / MODI | $O(n^2)$ | High |
| Two-Stage TP | Sequential LCM on each stage | $O(n^2)$ | **High (7 marks)** |
| TP with Node Capacity | LCM + feasibility check | $O(n^2)$ | **High (7 marks)** |
| Unbalanced TP | Add Dummy with $M = 0$ | $O(n^2)$ | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Capacitated VRP

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputA[Customer Demand Data] --> proc1
    inputB[Distance Matrix] --> proc1
    inputC[Vehicle Capacity Q] --> proc1
    proc1[Initial Route Generator] --> proc2
    proc2[Clarke Wright Savings Computation] --> proc3
    proc3[Savings Sort Descending] --> proc4
    proc4{Feasibility Check}
    proc4 -- Capacity OK --> proc5[Merge Routes]
    proc4 -- Capacity Violated --> proc6[Reject Merge]
    proc5 --> proc7{All Pairs Scanned?}
    proc6 --> proc7
    proc7 -- No --> proc4
    proc7 -- Yes --> proc8[Output Final Route Set]
    proc8 --> outputA[Route 1: 0 to A to B to 0]
    proc8 --> outputB[Route 2: 0 to C to E to 0]
    proc8 --> outputC[Route 3: 0 to D to 0]
    proc8 --> outputD[Total Cost Z]
```

## 4.2 Two-Stage Transportation Network with Capacity-Bounded Transshipment

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Plant P1 Supply 30] -- c 8 --> W1
    P1 -- c 6 --> W2[Warehouse W2 Cap 30]
    P2[Plant P2 Supply 20] -- c 5 --> W1[Warehouse W1 Cap 25]
    P2 -- c 9 --> W2
    W1 -- d 4 --> R1[Retailer R1 Demand 15]
    W1 -- d 7 --> R2[Retailer R2 Demand 20]
    W1 -- d 5 --> R3[Retailer R3 Demand 15]
    W2 -- d 6 --> R1
    W2 -- d 3 --> R2
    W2 -- d 8 --> R3
    styleW1[Capacity Check 20 le 25]
    styleW2[Capacity Check 30 le 30 ACTIVE]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Solving a Multi-Stage TP with Node Capacity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read Problem Statement] --> B{Total Supply equals Total Demand?}
    B -- No --> C[Add Dummy Source or Sink with zero cost]
    B -- Yes --> D[Stage 1 LCM on Plant to Warehouse]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Node Capacity Respected?}
    E -- No --> F[Reroute via alternative warehouse or expand capacity]
    E -- Yes --> G[Stage 2 LCM on Warehouse to Retailer]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Total Cost Z1 plus Z2]
    H --> I[Apply MODI Optimality Test]
    I --> J{All Reduced Costs ge 0?}
    J -- No --> K[Iterate to optimal]
    J -- Yes --> L[Output Optimal Shipment Plan]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — VRP Route Consolidation (Before vs After Clarke-Wright)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BEFORE[CVRP Before Savings - 5 Separate Trips]
        depot1[Depot 0]
        depot1 -- 20 km --> A1[A]
        A1 -- 20 km --> depot1
        depot1 -- 30 km --> B1[B]
        B1 -- 30 km --> depot1
        depot1 -- 40 km --> C1[C]
        C1 -- 40 km --> depot1
        depot1 -- 50 km --> D1[D]
        D1 -- 50 km --> depot1
        depot1 -- 60 km --> E1[E]
        E1 -- 60 km --> depot1
    end
    subgraph AFTER[CVRP After Savings - 3 Consolidated Trips]
        depot2[Depot 0]
        depot2 -- 10 km --> A2[A]
        A2 -- 12 km --> B2[B]
        B2 -- 15 km --> depot2
        depot2 -- 20 km --> C2[C]
        C2 -- 14 km --> E2[E]
        E2 -- 30 km --> depot2
        depot2 -- 25 km --> D2[D]
        D2 -- 25 km --> depot2
    end
```
