<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP). How does it differ from the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** is a combinatorial optimisation problem that seeks the minimum-cost set of delivery routes for a *fleet of vehicles* originating from a central depot to serve a set of geographically dispersed customers, subject to vehicle capacity constraints. **(1.5 marks)**

It differs from the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** in three key ways:
- TSP uses a **single vehicle**; VRP uses **multiple vehicles** from one depot.
- TSP has **no capacity limit**; VRP (CVRP) imposes a **load bound $Q$** on each route.
- TSP is a *single closed tour*; VRP produces **multiple sub-tours**, each starting and ending at the depot. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is a Multi-Stage Transportation Problem? Explain the role of node capacities.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Multi-Stage Transportation Problem (MSTP)** is a logistics network in which goods move from *sources* through one or more *intermediate transshipment nodes* (warehouses) before reaching *final destinations*, with the objective of minimising total transportation cost across all stages. **(1.5 marks)**

**Node capacities** specify the maximum throughput permissible at each intermediate node, expressed as $\sum_i x_{ij} \leq C_j$. They model real-world bottlenecks like port handling limits, warehouse floor space, and truck-bay availability. They transform the classical TP into a *capacity-constrained* problem requiring feasibility verification after every allocation. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** A depot at node **O** must serve 5 customers with the following distance and demand data. Vehicle capacity $Q = 30$ units. Find the **optimal routes** using the **Clarke-Wright Savings Algorithm**. **(7 marks)**

| Customer | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 8 | 12 | 10 | 6 | 14 |
| $d_{0i}$ | 10 | 15 | 20 | 12 | 18 |

Symmetric inter-customer distances: $d_{AB}=8$, $d_{AC}=15$, $d_{AD}=10$, $d_{AE}=12$, $d_{BC}=10$, $d_{BD}=14$, $d_{BE}=11$, $d_{CD}=9$, $d_{CE}=13$, $d_{DE}=7$.

**(b)** Formulate the **Two-Stage Transportation Problem** as a Linear Program. Clearly state all decision variables, the objective function, and every constraint category. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Initialise 5 separate routes** (1 mark)

Total initial distance = $2 \times (10+15+20+12+18) = \mathbf{150}$ units.

**Step 2 — Compute savings** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{AB} &= 10+15-8 = 17 \\
S_{AC} &= 10+20-15 = 15 \\
S_{AD} &= 10+12-10 = 12 \\
S_{AE} &= 10+18-12 = 16 \\
S_{BC} &= 15+20-10 = 25 \\
S_{BD} &= 15+12-14 = 13 \\
S_{BE} &= 15+18-11 = 22 \\
S_{CD} &= 20+12-9 = 23 \\
S_{CE} &= 20+18-13 = 25 \\
S_{DE} &= 12+18-7 = 23
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sort descending** (0.5 mark): (B,C) & (C,E) at 25, (C,D) & (D,E) at 23, (B,E) at 22, (A,B) at 17, (A,E) at 16, (A,C) at 15, (B,D) at 13, (A,D) at 12.

**Step 4 — Merge with capacity check** (3 marks)

- **Merge (B,C), S=25:** Route 0→B→C→0, load = $12+10=22 \leq 30$ ✓
- **Merge (C,E), S=25:** C is interior to 0–B–C–0. **Reject.**
- **Merge (C,D), S=23:** C is interior. **Reject.**
- **Merge (D,E), S=23:** D alone, E alone. Route 0→D→E→0, load = $6+14=20 \leq 30$ ✓
- **Merge (B,E), S=22:** B is interior. **Reject.**
- **Merge (A,B), S=17:** A alone, B is endpoint of 0–B–C–0. Route 0→A→B→C→0, load = $8+12+10=30 \leq 30$ ✓
- All other merges involve interior nodes — reject.

**Step 5 — Final routes** (0.5 mark)

| Route | Path | Load | Distance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R_1$ | O → A → B → C → O | 30 | $10+8+10+20 = 48$ |
| $R_2$ | O → D → E → O | 20 | $12+7+18 = 37$ |

**Total Cost** = $48 + 37 = \mathbf{85}$ units, a **saving of 65 units (43.3%)** from the initial 150.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Decision Variables** (1 mark)
- $x_{ij}$ = units shipped from Plant $i$ ($i = 1,\dots,m$) to Warehouse $j$ ($j = 1,\dots,n$)
- $y_{jk}$ = units shipped from Warehouse $j$ to Retailer/Customer $k$ ($k = 1,\dots,p$)

**Objective Function** (1 mark)
$$\min Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} x_{ij} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{p} d_{jk} y_{jk}$$

**Constraints** (5 marks — list each category)

1. **Plant supply** (1 m): $\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} \leq s_i$, $\forall i$
2. **Node capacity at warehouse** (1 m): $\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} \leq C_j$, $\forall j$
3. **Transshipment balance at warehouse** (1 m): $\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{p} y_{jk}$, $\forall j$
4. **Retailer demand** (1 m): $\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_{jk} \geq D_k$, $\forall k$
5. **Non-negativity** (1 m): $x_{ij}, y_{jk} \geq 0$ for all $i,j,k$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** A company has **3 plants** $P_1, P_2, P_3$ with supplies 50, 40, 60 units. Goods are shipped to **2 warehouses** $W_1, W_2$ with capacities 80 and 70, and from there to **4 retailers** $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ with demands 30, 40, 50, 30. Stage 1 costs (Plant→Warehouse) and Stage 2 costs (Warehouse→Retailer) are given below. **Find the minimum cost feasible plan** and check all node capacities. **(7 marks)**

**Stage 1 cost matrix:**

| | $W_1$ | $W_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 4 | 6 |
| $P_2$ | 8 | 5 |
| $P_3$ | 7 | 9 |

**Stage 2 cost matrix:**

| | $R_1$ | $R_2$ | $R_3$ | $R_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ | 5 | 3 | 7 | 4 |
| $W_2$ | 6 | 8 | 4 | 5 |

**(b)** Explain the **Sweep Algorithm** for solving the Vehicle Routing Problem. How does it differ from the Clarke-Wright Savings method? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Balance check** (0.5 mark): Total supply = $50+40+60 = 150$. Total demand = $30+40+50+30 = 150$. **Balanced.**

**Solve Stage 1 (LCM)** (2 marks)

| | $W_1$ (80) | $W_2$ (70) | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ (50) | **50** (cost 4) | 0 | 50 |
| $P_2$ (40) | 0 | **40** (cost 5) | 40 |
| $P_3$ (60) | **30** (cost 7) | **30** (cost 9) | 60 |
| Allocated | 80 | 70 | 150/150 |

$$Z_1 = 50(4) + 40(5) + 30(7) + 30(9) = 200 + 200 + 210 + 270 = \mathbf{880}$$

**Capacity check** (0.5 mark): $W_1$ loaded to 80 (≤ 80 ✓), $W_2$ loaded to 70 (≤ 70 ✓). Both **active constraints**.

**Solve Stage 2 (LCM)** (3 marks)
Available: $W_1 = 80$, $W_2 = 70$.

| | $R_1$ (30) | $R_2$ (40) | $R_3$ (50) | $R_4$ (30) | Avail |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ (80) | 0 | **40** (cost 3) | 0 | **30** (cost 4) | 80→10→0 |
| $W_2$ (70) | **30** (cost 6) | 0 | **40** (cost 4) | 0 | 70→40→0→0 |
| Allocated | 30 | 40 | 40 | 30 | 150 |

**Wait — $R_3$ needs 50 but only 40 allocated.** Need to adjust: reallocate.

**Corrected Stage 2** (1 mark)

- Allocate $W_1 \to R_2 = 40$ (cost 3)
- Allocate $W_2 \to R_3 = 50$ (cost 4) — but $W_2$ has 70 available, so 50 is fine.
- Allocate $W_2 \to R_1 = 20$ (cost 6). Now $W_2$ has 0 left.
- Allocate $W_1 \to R_1 = 10$ (cost 5) to satisfy remaining $R_1$ demand.
- Allocate $W_1 \to R_4 = 30$ (cost 4) to satisfy $R_4$ demand. $W_1$ has 0 left.

$$Z_2 = 40(3) + 50(4) + 20(6) + 10(5) + 30(4) = 120 + 200 + 120 + 50 + 120 = \mathbf{610}$$

**Total Cost** (0.5 mark)
$$Z = Z_1 + Z_2 = 880 + 610 = \mathbf{1490 \text{ units}}$$

**Final Feasibility Summary** (0.5 mark)

| Node | Loaded | Capacity | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ | 80 | 80 | ✓ Active |
| $W_2$ | 70 | 70 | ✓ Active |
| $R_1$ | 30 | 30 | ✓ |
| $R_2$ | 40 | 40 | ✓ |
| $R_3$ | 50 | 50 | ✓ |
| $R_4$ | 30 | 30 | ✓ |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Sweep Algorithm Steps** (4 marks)

1. Position the depot at the origin of a polar coordinate system.
2. Compute the polar angle $\theta_j = \arctan\left( \dfrac{y_j - y_0}{x_j - x_0} \right)$ for every customer $j$ relative to the depot.
3. Sort customers in **ascending order of angle**.
4. **Sweep** clockwise from angle 0, accumulating customers into a cluster until the next customer's demand would *exceed* the vehicle capacity $Q$.
5. **Close** the current route, start a new vehicle, and continue the sweep.
6. Within each cluster, apply a TSP heuristic (e.g., nearest-neighbour) to determine visiting order.

**Comparison with Clarke-Wright** (3 marks)

| Aspect | Sweep Algorithm | Clarke-Wright |
|---|---|---|
| Logic basis | Geometric (angular) | Distance savings |
| Cluster formation | Sweep until capacity hit | Pairwise merging of max savings |
| Computational step | $O(n \log n)$ for sorting | $O(n^2 \log n)$ for savings list |
| Strength | Visually intuitive, good for *radial* customer distributions | Better for *arbitrary* distance matrices |
| KTU preference | Common in 7-mark conceptual Q | Common in 7-mark numerical Q |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Before Writing the Exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "demand" check after Clarke-Wright merging.** A route with combined demand exceeding $Q$ is *infeasible* even if total distance is low. **Loss: 1 to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Mistaking node capacity for vehicle capacity.** They are *different* constraints. Node capacity $C_j$ limits flow *through* a transshipment point; vehicle capacity $Q$ limits load *on* a single vehicle.
> 3. **Omitting the depot return arc.** Every route must end at the depot. A route 0 → A → B is incomplete; the correct form is 0 → A → B → 0. **Loss: 0.5 mark per route.**
> 4. **Forgetting to balance the transportation problem.** An unbalanced TP cannot be solved by LCM. Always add a dummy with **$M = 0$** cost.
> 5. **Writing $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$ without units.** State "kilometres" or "distance units" — examiners expect dimensional awareness.
> 6. **Confusing Stage 1 cost with Stage 2 cost in MSTP.** Always use the *correct* matrix for each stage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read this 30 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **VRP definition** — multi-vehicle routing from one depot; generalises TSP.
- ✅ **CVRP** — VRP with load bound $\sum_{j \in R_k} d_j \leq Q$.
- ✅ **VRPTW** — VRP with time windows (rare in KTU, but appears in 2-mark MCQ).
- ✅ **Clarke-Wright Savings formula** — $S_{ij} = d_{0i} + d_{0j} - d_{ij}$; sort descending; merge subject to capacity.
- ✅ **Sweep Algorithm** — polar angle grouping around depot.
- ✅ **Nearest Neighbour** — greedy TSP solver; choose closest unvisited node.
- ✅ **MSTP** — Two-stage flow: Plant → Warehouse → Retailer.
- ✅ **Transshipment balance** — inflow = outflow at every intermediate node.
- ✅ **Node capacity constraint** — $\sum_i x_{ij} \leq C_j$ (hard limit, not objective).
- ✅ **LCM rule** — allocate to the cell with the *lowest* available cost, the *maximum feasible* quantity.
- ✅ **Dummy row/column** — to balance supply and demand; use $M = 0$ cost.
- ✅ **Active constraint** — node where capacity is *exactly* reached (key insight for managerial decisions).
- ✅ **Solved cost formula for VRP** — initial total $-$ final total $=$ *route savings*.
- ✅ **KTT typical problem sizes** — 5 to 6 customers, 2 plants, 2 to 3 warehouses, 3 to 4 retailers.
- ✅ **Memorise** the two $14$-mark question structures: *one numerical + one formulation/conceptual*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Logistics Decision Models Including Bin Packing Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET502 - SUPPLY CHAIN AND LOGISTICS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application Logistics Decision Models Including Bin Packing Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Application Logistics Decision Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Application Logistics Decision Models** are quantitative, mathematically formulated frameworks that translate real-world logistics problems (allocation, loading, routing, cutting, and packaging) into solvable optimization structures. They convert operational trade-offs into objective functions subject to physical, financial, and policy constraints so that the **lowest-cost** or **highest-utilization** decision can be identified deterministically or heuristically.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4), these models sit at the intersection of *Operations Research* and *Supply Chain Execution*. The most frequently examined models are:

| Model Family | Core Logistics Question Answered |
|---|---|
| **Bin Packing Problem (BPP)** | How do I load a fixed-capacity vehicle/warehouse with the *fewest* bins? |
| **Cutting Stock Problem (CSP)** | What is the *minimum* number of stock rolls to satisfy piece demand? |
| **Transportation / Transshipment Model** | Which plant-to-warehouse lane carries how much at *minimum cost*? |
| **Assignment Model** | Which vehicle / driver handles which shipment at *minimum cost*? |
| **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** | What is the *shortest* set of routes serving all customers? |
| **Facility Location Model** | Where should DCs be opened to *minimize* fixed + variable cost? |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Picture you are moving houses. You have a fixed-size container truck and a pile of differently sized boxes (TV, books, lamps, clothes). **Application Logistics Decision Models are the "Tetris engine"** of the supply chain: they tell you, given limited capacity, *how* to squeeze the maximum number of items into the minimum number of containers, *which* lane to ship on, or *where* to place a new warehouse. The Bin Packing Problem is literally the math version of packing your moving truck.

## 1.2 The Bin Packing Problem (BPP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (1D-BPP):**
> Given a set of *n* items, each with a size $s_i \in (0, C]$ (where $C$ is the uniform bin capacity), the **Bin Packing Problem** asks for the **minimum number of bins** required to pack all items such that the sum of sizes in any bin does not exceed $C$.

### Key Parameters
- **Bin capacity $C$** — the maximum allowable total size per bin (e.g., **10 tonnes**, **32 m³** of a container, **1000 kg** of a pallet).
- **Item size $s_i$** — weight, volume, or a normalized dimensionless measure.
- **Number of bins used $Z$** — the objective to be **minimized**.

### Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Logistics Analogy — Three Real Scenes:**
> 1. **Truck Loading:** Each pallet has a weight; the truck has a max payload. We want minimum trucks.
> 2. **Container Stowage:** A 20-ft container has a volume of ~33 m³; boxes have volumes; we want minimum containers.
> 3. **Memory Allocation in Computing:** OS allocates memory pages to processes; minimum number of frames.
> 4. **Warehouse Cut-Length Optimization:** Cutting fabric rolls to satisfy garment orders; minimum stock rolls.

## 1.3 Why BPP is Central to KTU Module 4

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Bin Packing is **NP-hard**. Exact methods (Integer Linear Programming, Branch-and-Bound) become computationally explosive beyond ~50 items. Hence the KTU 2024 syllabus places equal weight on:
> - **Mathematical formulation** (for analytical clarity in exams)
> - **Heuristic / approximation algorithms** (for industrial applicability)
> - **Lower-bound theory** (for evaluating heuristic quality)

This is precisely the *Application Logistics Decision* stance: **model the real world mathematically, then solve it efficiently under practical constraints**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 1D Bin Packing Problem — Structured Logic

The BPP has a layered structure that KTU examiners love to test. We dissect it step-by-step:

### Layer 1 — Sets and Indices
- Let $I = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ be the set of **items** to be packed.
- Let $J = \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$ be the set of **available bins** (an upper bound, e.g., $m = n$).

### Layer 2 — Parameters
- $s_i$ = size (weight/volume) of item $i$, with $0 < s_i \le C$.
- $C$ = uniform capacity of each bin.

### Layer 3 — Decision Variables
- $y_j \in \{0, 1\}$ = **1** if bin $j$ is used, **0** otherwise.
- $x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ = **1** if item $i$ is assigned to bin $j$, **0** otherwise.

### Layer 4 — Objective
- **Minimize the number of bins opened:**
$$\min \ Z = \sum_{j \in J} y_j$$

### Layer 5 — Constraints
- **Capacity constraint:** the total size in any opened bin must not exceed $C$.

$$\sum_{i \in I} s_i \, x_{ij} \le C \, y_j \quad \forall \, j \in J$$

- **Assignment constraint:** each item must be placed in *exactly one* bin.

$$\sum_{j \in J} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall \, i \in I$$

- **Integrality:** $x_{ij}, \, y_j \in \{0, 1\}$.

### Layer 6 — The "Why" Behind the Logic
- The $y_j$ on the right of the capacity equation is the **clever trick**: it automatically *switches off* the capacity constraint for unused bins ($y_j = 0 \Rightarrow$ RHS = 0, so the inequality becomes trivial).
- The assignment equation guarantees **no item is dropped or duplicated** — a non-negotiable logistics rule.

---

## 2.2 Lower Bounds on the Optimal Number of Bins

Lower bounds (LBs) give a *floor* on $Z^*$, against which heuristics are benchmarked.

### 2.2.1 Continuous (Weight) Lower Bound $L_1$

$$L_1 = \left\lceil \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i}{C} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{S_{total}}{C} \right\rceil$$

*Rationale:* Even if we could fractionalize items, we cannot fit more total size than $C$ per bin.

### 2.2.2 Largest-Item Lower Bound $L_2$

$$L_2 = \left\lceil \frac{s_{\max}}{C} \right\rceil \cdot n_{\max} \quad \text{? No — corrected form: } L_2 \ge n_{\text{size}>C/2}$$

*Simpler statement:* any item with $s_i > C/2$ occupies a bin alone (or shares it only with a very small item). A more practical bound:

$$L_2 = \left\lceil \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i}{C} \right\rceil \text{ and } L_2 \ge \text{ count of items with } s_i > C/2$$

### 2.2.3 Combined Lower Bound

$$L = \max(L_1, \, L_2, \, L_3)$$

where $L_3$ may be a dual-feasible bound or pair-based bound.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula a KTU 2024 student is expected to reproduce** for Module 4 BPP questions. Memorize the symbols, units, and the row corresponding to the question type.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Application / Use | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Objective function | $\min \ Z = \sum_{j=1}^{m} y_j$ | Total bins used | Integer, dimensionless |
| 2 | Capacity constraint | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i \, x_{ij} \le C \, y_j, \ \forall j$ | Truck/container payload | $s_i, C$ in same unit (kg, m³) |
| 3 | Assignment constraint | $\sum_{j=1}^{m} x_{ij} = 1, \ \forall i$ | Each item packed once | All items indexed |
| 4 | Integrality | $x_{ij}, y_j \in \{0,1\}$ | Binary nature | Boolean |
| 5 | Continuous LB | $L_1 = \lceil S_{total}/C \rceil$ | Theoretical minimum | $S_{total} = \sum s_i$ |
| 6 | Item-count LB | $L_2 = n_{>C/2}$ | Large-item counting | Count, not size |
| 7 | FFD asymptotic bound | $Z_{FFD} \le \tfrac{11}{9} Z_{OPT} + 1$ | Worst-case guarantee | Proven by Dósa & Sgall (2013, later improved) |
| 8 | Worst-case ratio (FF) | $Z_{FF} \le 1.7 \cdot Z_{OPT}$ | Simpler bound | Less tight than FFD |
| 9 | 2D-BPP constraint | $w_i \, x_{ij} \le W \, y_j, \ h_i \, x_{ij} \le H \, y_j$ | Pallet/box packing | $W, H$ = bin width, height |
| 10 | Container TEU factor | $\text{Fill ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Used volume}}{\text{Container capacity}}$ | Logistics efficiency | Decimal 0 to 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the backbone of **Load Planning Software** (e.g., CargoPlanner, OptiDock), **Warehouse Management Systems (WMS)**, and **ERP Transportation Modules** in SAP/Oracle. The lower-bound calculations enable **performance dashboards** that compare actual bin usage to theoretical optimum.

---

## 2.4 Heuristic Families for BPP

### Family 1 — Online / Single-Pass Heurrics
| Heuristic | Rule | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First Fit (FF)** | Place each item in the *first* bin that has room. | $O(n \log n)$; simple. | Worst-case ratio $\le 1.7 \cdot Z_{OPT}$. |
| **Best Fit (BF)** | Place each item in the bin with the *smallest residual* that still fits. | Tighter packing locally. | More computation, similar bound. |

### Family 2 — Offline / Sorted Heuristics
| Heuristic | Rule | Worst-Case Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| **First Fit Decreasing (FFD)** | Sort items in non-increasing size, then apply FF. | $\le \tfrac{11}{9} Z_{OPT} + 1$ |
| **Best Fit Decreasing (BFD)** | Sort items descending, then apply BF. | $\le \tfrac{11}{9} Z_{OPT} + 1$ |

### Family 3 — Metaheuristics
- **Genetic Algorithm (GA):** encodes bin assignments as chromosomes.
- **Simulated Annealing (SA):** accepts worse solutions probabilistically to escape local optima.
- **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO):** pheromone trails guide bin selection.
- **Branch-and-Price / Column Generation:** exact for large instances (Gilmore–Gomory for CSP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 FFD Guarantee (worth memorizing verbatim):**
> For the First Fit Decreasing heuristic applied to 1D-BPP, the asymptotic worst-case ratio satisfies
> $$Z_{FFD} \le \frac{11}{9} Z_{OPT} + \frac{6}{9}$$
> This means FFD never wastes more than ~22% of bins beyond the optimum in the worst case.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — FFD on a 1D-BPP Instance

We solve a classic KTU-style problem completely, showing every step.

### Problem Statement
> A logistics dispatcher has **eight** cartons with weights (in tonnes) of $\{8, 7, 5, 4, 4, 3, 3, 3\}$. Each delivery truck can carry a **maximum payload** of **10 tonnes**. Apply the **First Fit Decreasing (FFD)** heuristic. State the number of trucks used, the load on each truck, and compare against the **continuous lower bound**.

---

### Step 1 — Sort Items in Non-Increasing Order

The FFD algorithm's *preprocessing* step requires descending sort by size:

$$S_{sorted} = \{8, 7, 5, 4, 4, 3, 3, 3\}$$

(In this instance, the original list is already in descending order — convenient for teaching, but typically a sort is required.)

---

### Step 2 — Compute the Continuous Lower Bound $L_1$

The total weight is

$$\sum_{i=1}^{8} s_i = 8 + 7 + 5 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 37 \text{ tonnes}$$

The lower bound (even with fractional cuts) is

$$L_1 = \left\lceil \frac{37}{10} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.7 \rceil = 4 \text{ trucks}$$

Additionally, items with $s_i > 5$ (i.e., $> C/2$) are $\{8, 7\}$ — so $L_2 = 2$. Hence

$$L = \max(L_1, L_2) = \max(4, 2) = 4 \text{ trucks}$$

So **at least 4 trucks are theoretically required**.

---

### Step 3 — Initialize Bins and Apply FFD Iteratively

We maintain a running residual for each opened bin. We process each item in sorted order:

#### Iteration 1: Item $s_1 = 8$
- No bin open → open **Bin 1**, place 8.
- Bin 1 load: 8; residual: $10 - 8 = 2$.

| Bin | Load | Residual |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | 8 | 2 |

#### Iteration 2: Item $s_2 = 7$
- B1 residual = 2 < 7 → does not fit. Open **Bin 2**, place 7.
- Bin 2 load: 7; residual: $10 - 7 = 3$.

| Bin | Load | Residual |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | 8 | 2 |
| B2 | 7 | 3 |

#### Iteration 3: Item $s_3 = 5$
- B1 residual = 2 < 5 → no.
- B2 residual = 3 < 5 → no.
- Open **Bin 3**, place 5. Residual = 5.

| Bin | Load | Residual |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | 8 | 2 |
| B2 | 7 | 3 |
| B3 | 5 | 5 |

#### Iteration 4: Item $s_4 = 4$
- B1 residual = 2 < 4 → no.
- B2 residual = 3 < 4 → no.
- B3 residual = 5 ≥ 4 → **place in B3**.
- B3 load: $5 + 4 = 9$; residual: $10 - 9 = 1$.

| Bin | Load | Residual |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | 8 | 2 |
| B2 | 7 | 3 |
| B3 | 5+4=9 | 1 |

#### Iteration 5: Item $s_5 = 4$ (second 4-tonne item)
- B1 residual = 2 < 4 → no.
- B2 residual = 3 < 4 → no.
- B3 residual = 1 < 4 → no.
- Open **Bin 4**, place 4. Residual = 6.

| Bin | Load | Residual |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | 8 | 2 |
| B2 | 7 | 3 |
| B3 | 9 | 1 |
| B4 | 4 | 6 |

#### Iteration 6: Item $s_6 = 3$
- B1 residual = 2 < 3 → no.
- B2 residual = 3 ≥ 3 → **place in B2**.
- B2 load: $7 + 3 = 10$; residual: 0.

| Bin | Load | Residual |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | 8 | 2 |
| B2 | 7+3=10 | 0 |
| B3 | 9 | 1 |
| B4 | 4 | 6 |

#### Iteration 7: Item $s_7 = 3$ (second 3-tonne item)
- B1 residual = 2 < 3 → no.
- B2 residual = 0 < 3 → no.
- B3 residual = 1 < 3 → no.
- B4 residual = 6 ≥ 3 → **place in B4**.
- B4 load: $4 + 3 = 7$; residual: 3.

| Bin | Load | Residual |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | 8 | 2 |
| B2 | 10 | 0 |
| B3 | 9 | 1 |
| B4 | 4+3=7 | 3 |

#### Iteration 8: Item $s_8 = 3$ (third 3-tonne item)
- B1 residual = 2 < 3 → no.
- B2 residual = 0 < 3 → no.
- B3 residual = 1 < 3 → no.
- B4 residual = 3 ≥ 3 → **place in B4**.
- B4 load: $7 + 3 = 10$; residual: 0.

| Bin | Final Load | Residual |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | 8 | 2 |
| B2 | 10 | 0 |
| B3 | 9 | 1 |
| B4 | 10 | 0 |

---

### Step 4 — Final Result and Comparison

$$\boxed{Z_{FFD} = 4 \text{ trucks}, \quad L_1 = 4 \text{ trucks}}$$

**Truck-wise load summary:**

| Truck | Items Loaded | Total Load (tonnes) | Utilization |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 8 | 8 | 80% |
| T2 | 7, 3 | 10 | 100% |
| T3 | 5, 4 | 9 | 90% |
| T4 | 4, 3, 3 | 10 | 100% |

**Mean utilization:**

$$\bar{U} = \frac{8 + 10 + 9 + 10}{4 \times 10} = \frac{37}{40} = 0.925 = 92.5\%$$

Since $Z_{FFD} = L_1$, the FFD solution is **optimal** for this instance.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Model

| Real-World Logistics Situation | Recommended Model | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Pallet loading with weight & volume | **2D / 3D BPP** | Spatial dimensions matter |
| Cutting fabric/steel/paper to demand | **Cutting Stock Problem** | Minimize stock rolls used |
| Allocate cargo to vessels for export | **BPP + Transportation Model** | Bin = vessel capacity, lane cost |
| Last-mile delivery routing | **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** | Sequencing + capacity |
| Choose warehouse location | **Capacitated Facility Location** | Fixed + transport cost |
| Cross-dock staffing | **Assignment / Queuing Model** | Worker-to-task allocation |
| Demand fulfillment under uncertainty | **Newsvendor / Stochastic Inventory** | Probabilistic demand |
| JIT component supply | **EOQ with safety stock** | Minimize total cost |

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Duality — Bin Packing ↔ Cutting Stock

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight (Examiner favourite):**
> The **Bin Packing Problem** and the **Cutting Stock Problem** are mathematical duals of one another.
> - **BPP** minimizes *bins used* given *item sizes*.
> - **CSP** minimizes *stock rolls used* given *demand quantities and piece lengths*.
>
> **Formally**, an instance of 1D-CSP with stock length $L$ and piece lengths $a_1, a_2, \ldots$ satisfying demands $d_1, d_2, \ldots$ is solvable by **Gilmore–Gomory column generation**, which is the LP relaxation of an *integer* BPP. The optimal number of cutting patterns times stock length equals the optimal BPP bin count — a duality that KTU Module 4 students must recognize.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 First Fit Decreasing (FFD) Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start FFD Procedure] --> B[Input: list of item sizes s1 to sn and bin capacity C]
    B --> C[Sort items in non-increasing order]
    C --> D[Initialize empty list of bins]
    D --> E[Select next item from sorted list]
    E --> F{Any open bin exists?}
    F -->|No| G[Open new bin and assign item]
    F -->|Yes| H[Scan bins from first to last]
    H --> I{Item fits in current bin?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Assign item to current bin]
    I -->|No| K[Move to next bin]
    K --> I
    J --> L{More items remaining?}
    G --> L
    L -->|Yes| E
    L -->|No| M[Output: bin assignment and total bin count Z]
    M --> N[End]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Each diamond is a decision; each rectangle is an action. The left branch (open new bin) is invoked only when no existing bin has residual $\ge$ item size.

---

## 4.2 Application Logistics Decision Model — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Real World Logistics Problem] --> B[Problem Structuring]
    B --> C[Mathematical Model Formulation]
    C --> D{Problem Size and Complexity}
    D -->|Small n less than 50| E[Exact Solver ILP Branch and Bound]
    D -->|Large n or NP hard| F[Heuristic Engine FF BF FFD BFD]
    D -->|Very Large Industrial| G[Metaheuristics GA SA ACO]
    E --> H[Optimal Solution Z star]
    F --> I[Approximate Solution Z approx with bound]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Validation Against Lower Bound L]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Decision Output to Operations]
    K --> L[Truck Loading Plan]
    K --> M[Warehouse Slotting Plan]
    K --> N[Container Stowage Plan]
    K --> O[Stock Cutting Plan]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is the **decision-support pipeline** for application logistics. Raw operational data is structured into a model, routed to an appropriate solver, validated against theoretical bounds, and finally dispatched as a usable operational plan.

---

## 4.3 Bin Packing Variants — Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bin Packing Problem Family] --> B[One Dimensional BPP]
    A --> C[Two Dimensional BPP]
    A --> D[Three Dimensional BPP]
    A --> E[Variable Sized BPP]
    A --> F[Online BPP]
    B --> B1[Cutting Stock Dual]
    B --> B2[FFD BFD FF BF Heuristics]
    C --> C1[Pallet Loading]
    C --> C2[Strip Packing]
    D --> D1[Container Loading]
    D --> D2[Truck Cargo Stowage]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCIET502 Module 4]**
> Define the **Bin Packing Problem (BPP)**. Mention any **two real-world logistics applications** where it is applied.

**Model Answer (3 marks — each point carries 1 mark):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The Bin Packing Problem (BPP) is a combinatorial optimization problem in which a set of items of known sizes $s_i$ must be packed into the *minimum number* of identical bins of fixed capacity $C$, such that the sum of sizes in any bin does not exceed $C$.

> **Application 1 (1 mark):** **Truck/vehicle load planning** — assigning cargo weights to delivery trucks to minimize the fleet size required for a given shipment manifest.

> **Application 2 (1 mark):** **Container stowage in shipping** — packing cartons into 20-ft or 40-ft containers (TEU/FEU) such that the *fewest* containers are used for an export consignment.

*(Other acceptable applications: cutting stock in paper/textile industries, memory page allocation in cloud servers, pallet loading in warehouses.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCIET502 Module 4]**
> State and briefly explain the **First Fit Decreasing (FFD)** heuristic. Mention **one advantage** and **one limitation** of FFD.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement (1 mark):** First Fit Decreasing (FFD) is an offline heuristic for 1D-BPP. The items are first **sorted in non-increasing order of size**, and then each item, in sorted order, is placed into the **first bin** (among those already opened) that has sufficient residual capacity to accommodate it. If no existing bin can hold the item, a **new bin is opened**.

> **Advantage (1 mark):** It has a strong theoretical worst-case bound: $Z_{FFD} \le \frac{11}{9} Z_{OPT} + 1$, which means the number of bins used is at most ~22% above the optimum in the worst case.

> **Limitation (1 mark):** It is still a heuristic — it does not *guarantee* optimality for every instance; in pathological cases it may use one or more bins than the true optimum.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCIET502 Module 4]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Formulate the **One-Dimensional Bin Packing Problem (1D-BPP)** as a **Binary Integer Linear Program (BILP)**. Clearly define the sets, parameters, decision variables, objective function, and all constraints with their physical interpretation.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A logistics dispatcher has **eight cartons** with weights (in tonnes) $\{8, 7, 5, 4, 4, 3, 3, 3\}$ to be loaded on trucks of **capacity $C = 10$ tonnes each**. Apply the **First Fit Decreasing (FFD)** heuristic step by step. State the total number of trucks used and the **mean truck utilization** in percentage. Verify whether the result is optimal by computing the **continuous lower bound** $L_1$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Sets and Indices (1 mark):**
- $I = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ — the set of items to be packed.
- $J = \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$ — the set of *available* bins (typically $m = n$ as a safe upper bound).

**Parameters (1 mark):**
- $s_i$ = size of item $i$ (weight/volume, $0 < s_i \le C$).
- $C$ = uniform capacity of each bin.

**Decision Variables (1 mark):**
- $y_j \in \{0, 1\}$ = 1 if bin $j$ is *used*, 0 otherwise.
- $x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ = 1 if item $i$ is *assigned* to bin $j$, 0 otherwise.

**Objective Function (1 mark):**

$$\min \ Z = \sum_{j=1}^{m} y_j$$

*Interpretation:* The goal is to **minimize the number of bins opened** (i.e., the fleet size or the container count).

**Capacity Constraint (2 marks):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i \, x_{ij} \le C \, y_j, \quad \forall \, j \in J$$

*Interpretation:* If bin $j$ is closed ($y_j = 0$), RHS = 0, so the inequality is trivially satisfied and no item is placed in it. If bin $j$ is open ($y_j = 1$), the total size of items assigned to bin $j$ must not exceed the truck capacity $C$.

**Assignment Constraint (1 mark):**

$$\sum_{j=1}^{m} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall \, i \in I$$

*Interpretation:* Every item is assigned to **exactly one** bin — neither dropped nor duplicated.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Sort (1 mark):** The list is already in non-increasing order: $\{8, 7, 5, 4, 4, 3, 3, 3\}$.

**Step 2 — Lower bound (1 mark):**

$$\sum s_i = 37 \implies L_1 = \lceil 37 / 10 \rceil = 4 \text{ trucks}$$

**Step 3 — FFD iterations (3 marks):**

| Item | Action | Bin Loads After |
|---|---|---|
| 8 | Open B1: 8 | B1=8 |
| 7 | B1 cannot take; open B2: 7 | B1=8, B2=7 |
| 5 | B1, B2 cannot; open B3: 5 | B1=8, B2=7, B3=5 |
| 4 | B1, B2 cannot; B3 takes it (9) | B1=8, B2=7, B3=9 |
| 4 | Cannot fit in B1, B2, B3; open B4: 4 | B1=8, B2=7, B3=9, B4=4 |
| 3 | B1 no; B2 takes it (10 full) | B1=8, B2=10, B3=9, B4=4 |
| 3 | B1, B2, B3 no; B4 takes it (7) | B1=8, B2=10, B3=9, B4=7 |
| 3 | B1, B2, B3 no; B4 takes it (10 full) | B1=8, B2=10, B3=9, B4=10 |

**Step 4 — Result and verification (1 mark):**

$$Z_{FFD} = 4 \text{ trucks}, \quad L_1 = 4 \implies Z_{FFD} = L_1 \Rightarrow \textbf{optimal}$$

**Step 5 — Mean truck utilization (1 mark):**

$$\bar{U} = \frac{8 + 10 + 9 + 10}{4 \times 10} = \frac{37}{40} = 0.925 = \mathbf{92.5\%}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCIET502 Module 4]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **three real-world logistics scenarios** where the **Bin Packing Problem** arises naturally. For each scenario, identify the item, the bin, the capacity $C$, and the optimization objective.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the four principal heuristics — **First Fit (FF)**, **First Fit Decreasing (FFD)**, **Best Fit (BF)**, and **Best Fit Decreasing (BFD)** — using a comparison matrix. Solve the same instance $\{8, 7, 5, 4, 4, 3, 3, 3\}$ with capacity $C = 10$ using **Best Fit Decreasing (BFD)** and verify whether the answer differs from FFD.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Three Scenarios (7 marks: ~2.3 each)

**Scenario 1 — Truck Payload Optimization (2 marks):**
- *Item:* each customer shipment (weight in kg).
- *Bin:* a delivery truck with maximum payload $C$ (e.g., 10 tonnes).
- *Objective:* Minimize the number of trucks deployed to deliver all shipments.

**Scenario 2 — Container Loading in Maritime Logistics (2 marks):**
- *Item:* export cartons of varying volumes (m³).
- *Bin:* 20-ft TEU container (capacity $\approx 33$ m³ or 28 tonnes).
- *Objective:* Minimize number of containers (TEUs) billed to the shipper.

**Scenario 3 — Warehouse Pallet / Slot Allocation (2 marks):**
- *Item:* SKUs (Stock Keeping Units) with their pick-frequencies.
- *Bin:* fixed pick-face slot with capacity $C$ (volume or weight threshold).
- *Objective:* Minimize number of pick-face slots used OR minimize *travel distance* weighted version.

*(Acceptable alternatives: cloud-VM allocation to servers, newsprint cutting, airline baggage loading, etc. Each scenario should explicitly name item / bin / capacity / objective.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Comparison & BFD Solution (7 marks)

**Comparison Matrix (4 marks):**

| Criterion | FF | FFD | BF | BFD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sorting step** | No | Descending | No | Descending |
| **Placement rule** | First bin with room | First bin with room | Bin with min residual that fits | Bin with min residual that fits |
| **Time complexity** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n \log n + n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n \log n + n^2)$ |
| **Worst-case bound** | $\le 1.7 \cdot Z_{OPT}$ | $\le \tfrac{11}{9} Z_{OPT}+1$ | $\le 1.7 \cdot Z_{OPT}$ | $\le \tfrac{11}{9} Z_{OPT}+1$ |
| **Practical quality** | Good | Best of the four | Good | Comparable to FFD |
| **Use case** | Online packing | Offline known list | Online packing | Offline known list |

**BFD Solution (3 marks):**

Sorted list: $\{8, 7, 5, 4, 4, 3, 3, 3\}$.

| Item | Action (best fit = tightest residual) | Bin Loads After |
|---|---|---|
| 8 | Open B1: 8 (resid 2) | B1=8 |
| 7 | B1 resid 2 < 7; open B2: 7 (resid 3) | B1=8, B2=7 |
| 5 | B1, B2 cannot; open B3: 5 (resid 5) | B1=8, B2=7, B3=5 |
| 4 | B1, B2 cannot; B3 has resid 5 ≥ 4 → place in B3 (resid 1) | B1=8, B2=7, B3=9 |
| 4 | B1, B2, B3 cannot; open B4: 4 (resid 6) | B1=8, B2=7, B3=9, B4=4 |
| 3 | B1 resid 2 < 3; B2 resid 3 → place in B2 (resid 0); B3 resid 1; B4 resid 6 → place in B4 (resid 3). **Best Fit chooses B2** because resid 0 < resid 3 → tightest fit wins. | B1=8, B2=10, B3=9, B4=7 |
| 3 | B1, B2, B3 no; B4 resid 3 → place in B4 (resid 0) | B1=8, B2=10, B3=9, B4=10 |
| 3 | No bin fits; open B5: 3 (resid 7) | B1=8, B2=10, B3=9, B4=10, B5=3 |

$$Z_{BFD} = 5 \text{ trucks}$$

**Verification:** $Z_{BFD} = 5 > L_1 = 4$, hence BFD is **suboptimal** for this instance (uses one extra truck compared to FFD's 4). This example illustrates that BFD's "tightest fit" can leave fragmented residuals that later items cannot exploit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to sort in FFD/BFD.** KTU examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark if the sort step is skipped.
> 2. **Misinterpreting the capacity equation.** Students often write $\sum s_i x_{ij} \le C$ without the $y_j$ on the RHS. The $y_j$ is what *links* the capacity to the bin-opening decision — losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing lower bound with optimum.** $L_1$ is a *floor* on $Z^*$, not $Z^*$ itself. A common error: claiming optimality when $Z_{heuristic} = L_1$ *only* if no other bound is tighter.
> 4. **Arithmetic slip in FFD iteration.** A 1-tonne addition error cascades to wrong bin counts. Always re-verify the residual of the *last* item in each bin.
> 5. **Ignoring integrality.** $x_{ij}, y_j$ must be binary. Dropping this constraint turns the model into a *fractional* LP, which is a *different problem*.
> 6. **Best Fit vs. First Fit confusion.** BF chooses the *tightest fit* (minimum residual), not the bin with the *largest* residual. KTU 2024 question stems often test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Glance Summary — KTU 2024 Module 4 (Bin Packing & Application Logistics Decision Models)**

- **BPP Goal:** Minimize bins used given item sizes and uniform capacity $C$.
- **BPP is NP-hard** → heuristics dominate industrial use; exact ILP limited to small $n$.
- **1D-BPP ILP triplet:** objective $\min \sum y_j$, capacity $\sum s_i x_{ij} \le C y_j$, assignment $\sum x_{ij} = 1$, integrality $\{0,1\}$.
- **Continuous lower bound** $L_1 = \lceil S_{total} / C \rceil$ is the *cheapest* bound to compute.
- **FFD is the gold standard offline heuristic** — simple, with provable bound $\le \tfrac{11}{9} Z_{OPT}+1$.
- **BF vs. FF:** FF = first available; BF = tightest fit. Both online. BF can leave fragmented residuals.
- **FFD = sort descending, then FF.** **BFD = sort descending, then BF.** The sort is what makes the worst-case bound tight.
- **BPP ↔ CSP duality:** BPP minimizes bins for given items; CSP (Gilmore–Gomory) minimizes stock for given demand. They are LP duals.
- **2D/3D BPP variants** add width/height (and depth) constraints. Real-world use: pallets, containers, truck cargo bays.
- **Lower bound $L_2$** counts items with $s_i > C/2$ — each occupies a bin alone or with a small partner.
- **Industrial tools** for BPP: CargoPlanner, OptiCut, PalletStackingPro, and ERP/WMS optimization modules.
- **Validation rule:** if $Z_{heuristic} = L_1$, the solution is *guaranteed optimal* for that instance. If $Z_{heuristic} > L_1$, the gap is the **optimality gap** $= (Z_{heuristic} - L_1)/L_1 \times 100\%$.
- **Mean utilization** $\bar{U} = S_{total} / (Z \cdot C)$ is the *truck-fill ratio* — a key KPI in freight logistics.
- **KTU 2024 trap questions** include: writing $|x|$ in tables (use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX), forgetting the $y_j$ multiplier, and confusing BF's "best" with "first available."
- **Real-world extensions** to know by name: Capacitated VRP (CVRP), Container Loading Problem (CLP), Strip Packing, Bin Packing with Item Fragmentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
